## Thread: Cobol data protection? Get a dog... — 22 msgs

### Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2003-10-01T10:08:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0310010908.185ca01@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello everyone,

according to the cobol book that I am reading, some Submodules that are called
in the computer's open environments memory that have "active components" or
"Java applets" can overwrite the working areas of my programs or even clobber
the global variables. Is there a way to protect my cobol's working area? should
I use win32 APIs to LOCK the memory heaps? should I get a german shepherd?

thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-10-01T20:46:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031001164647.10007.00000087@mb-m21.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0310010908.185ca01@posting.google.com>`

```

KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM  (KELLIE FITTON)
Date: 10/1/03 12:08 PM EST
Message-id: <41758a6b.0310010908.185ca01@posting.google.com>

<<
according to the cobol book that I am reading, some Submodules that are called
in the computer's open environments memory that have "active components" or
"Java applets" can overwrite the working areas of my programs or even clobber
the global variables. Is there a way to protect my cobol's working area? should
I use win32 APIs to LOCK the memory heaps? should I get a german shepherd?

>>

This problem is universal and is not restricted to a COBOL caller. A submodule
can get to 'your' vaiables, 'your' code and global variables and even
supposedly secure executable code in memory (like the operating system itself)
.

You are doing quite well if your academic work has carried you to this
threshold already.

There is one critical factor and an important fact that will cast a perspective
on this issue for you as you start out.

The submodule needs an address to do damage. With an address it can start.
Amounts can be added to addresses or subtracted from addresses. Once a
submodule has an adress it can _potentially_ change whatever is there; code or
data, or _potentially_ change what is nearby.

You use the phrase 'open environment' I will let you define that so folks can
explore vulnerabilities in the particular kind of environment that you are
actually working with or that the book is explaining to you.  'Open' can mean a
lot of things. The only closed computer environment is one with the plug yanked
out and all batteries removed.

The basic issue is trusted computer environment versus not trusted computer
environment.  Can the _humans_ who have access to your system be trusted?

The perspective is that in the olden days pieces of the systems were usually
only connected to devices in places where there were folks who could be
trusted, by and large.

Now, what is different, is that we are connecting many computer environments to
everyone.

If your schools system provides any email service for you outbound, that means
that it might actually sometimes relay email through 
its memory and on to another system.  It is not inconceivable that someone in a
cave on the otherside of the world could relay something through the system at
your academic institution.
It is possible that you would prefer that it not become active anywhere along
the line near you. So you have a need to understand 'active', but the issue
that has changed is proximity. 

This did not used to happen. We are now connecting huge corporate information
repositories to the global net. That is a very big issue. It is a change.

A submodule, by the loose definition we may attribute to it here, has the
characteristic of being activated. An activated entity inside of a computer can
change data, code or addresses
of data or code. Because we have connected more computers to more persons with
unproven trustworthiness, anything that can become active is an important
management concern.

Active Controls, and Java Applets are only the begining.  ANYTHING that can
become active is an issue. It does not matter what the source code language or
the development environment or even the runtime environment. 

In addition to executable code and data, most modern systems have attributes
called privilege.
This is information that is associated with an executable, a thing which might
become active.

For example, the operating system, or even a productivity suite, might be
executed in an environment with very high privileges; allowing them to update
anything.  Such high-level code may call your program (say a COBOL program),
setting you up with fairly low privileges. 

When your code is invoked the operating context must hand you a return address
so that when you are done you can go home to the operating system (or Office
Suite program).

During processing you elect to invoke something. You activate by perhaps any
number of conventional strategies that are acceptable practices in your shop. 
If you intend for that submodule to return to you when it is done you must pass
your return address to it. If you want that submodule do do anything to your
data you are likely to consider passing an address to your data.  It is not
unlikely that the return address for the operating system was stored in a
memory location close to the data you want to
pass to the submodule. 

The submodule is activated. It does many things so that the world will think it
is just real nice.  But it has a malicious piece of code. It cannot execute the
bad code, because to do its evil it needs privileges it does not have and that
you do not have. It uses the address to your data that you want changed to hunt
around and find the return address that will ultimately leap from your code
back to the operating system, and switches it to the entry point for its own
malicious code.

So far no sign of trouble. The subprogram returns to you.  You got great data
results from the submodule, you publish and present magnificent indications
that all is well, ... and then, .... drum roll please, ... and then you return
to the return address.

The hardware takes you now to the malicious code, and it now has the privileges
of the OS or productivity office suite that had invoked you.

This may even at first look to forensics like you did it!

There are many possibilities once you have an address.

The object oriented technologies are actually going to make this a little
worse, because they tend to cluster addresses of functions in memory. Once a
hacker finds a zone of interests there are then numerous addresses adjacent
that are of value. This will be true of COBOL object oriented code, just like
anyother. Object Orientation happens greatly modularizes code, and as a fact
collects addresses for those modules into arrays. Call them sitting ducks.

On PCs the stack pointer itself can be used to get to your stack, which is
where your invokers return address is ... there really isn't much chaining to
be done.

On a mainframe return addresses live briefly in one of the registers of the
architected register file (specifically R14). A module like your module will
must likely have saved that in a zone managed by another register (usually
R13). R13 chains are a bit more of a challenge then stack pointers. But the
technique is intended to be used by average programmers.

And more important, Intel devices are widely distributed, as are copies of the
MicroSoft packages, so bad people can experiment and research for hours on end
until they find an exploit path. The older equipment is not acutally
less vulnerable, it is just _less_ accessible for research purposes.

Eventually there will be german shepherds, but I am afraid that hardware and
software vendors are entirely prepared to see us pay a much heavy price before
any real change occurs.

Most systems do not treat addresses of code in any special way once it is
stored. That is a generic problem. If the address of executable code is stored,
and that location can be manipulated, then the system cannot be considered
secure. 

Note that in object orientation, we can override methods. In some
implementations this means that the array with method addresses has selected
cells updated at instantiation time.

The larger the number of untrustworthy people connected to such a system, the
more the security decreases.

We eventually need to wrap every passed address and every stored address with
FITTON shepherd attributes. That is probably a long way off.

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

- **From:** charles.stevens@unisys.com (Chuck Stevens)
- **Date:** 2003-10-02T10:00:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c60a0b82.0310020900.10f1f4e6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0310010908.185ca01@posting.google.com> <20031001164647.10007.00000087@mb-m21.aol.com>`

```
rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote in message news:<20031001164647.10007.00000087@mb-m21.aol.com>...

No real problems with the post, except for this ... 

> On a mainframe return addresses live briefly in one of the registers of the
> architected register file (specifically R14). A module like your module will
> must likely have saved that in a zone managed by another register (usually
> R13). 

Really?  I've looked all through the architecture manuals of the
mainframe and I see no mention at all of an "architectured register
file", containing R13, R14 or otherwise.  I do see MPCW machine
operators being used for this purpose, both for the likes of
ENTR/EXIT/RETN and for DBFL/DBTR/DBUN, but the addresses are held most
typically in the hardware stack associated with the run time, and
that's where they stay until the space containing them isn't valid any
more or the object code they point to is no longer active.

> R13 chains are a bit more of a challenge then stack pointers. ... 

Perhaps that's a good reason why the architects of "real" mainframes
abandoned the idea of a fixed number of general-purpose registers
implemented in hardware in favor of a stack-oriented design using
descriptors to point to memory over forty years ago ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-10-04T03:17:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031003231753.02137.00000368@mb-m01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c60a0b82.0310020900.10f1f4e6@posting.google.com>`

```

A hard working student,.

KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM  (KELLIE FITTON)
Date: 10/1/03 12:08 PM EST
Message-id: <41758a6b.0310010908.185ca01@posting.google.com>


Writes:

<<
Hello everyone,

according to the cobol book that I am reading, some Submodules that are called
in the computer's open environments memory that have "active components" or
"Java applets" can overwrite the working areas of my programs or even clobber
the global variables. Is there a way to protect my cobol's working area? should
I use win32 APIs to LOCK the memory heaps? should I get a german shepherd?

thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.

>>



As you know by default, COBOL 
passes values BY REFERENCE. USING clauses can
be coded to achieve passing BY VALUE, as well 
(that is fairly recent in COBOL). 


You may wish to take note of 



Standford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory,
October 1973, Memo AIM 224
Computer Science Department Report No CS-403

"Hints on Programming Language Design"
By C. A. R. Hoare


"Section. 8 Variables"

(snips)

An assignment to x
"... if x is a reference variable may change any
other variable (of appropriate type) in the whole machine. ""

(snips, then at the end of the section)


"References are like jumps, leading wildly from one part of a data
structure to another. Their introduction into high level languages has
been a step backward from which we may never recover."


The early languages of interest to Hoare had parameter passing
by value, by reference and a thing they called BY NAME. Phrases similar
to BY NAME can mean different things.

I am a student of ALGOL and not an experienced practitioner. My summary
of an ALGOL parameter passed BY NAME is that it is a two level reference.

The problem this solved was that things could move, so if you had 
a mere reference, the thing you want might not be there anymore. 

Imagine shopping carts at an online bookstore, either your cart has 
moved to a new 'service station', or the cart at the station of interest
is the next cart instantiation. 

They did try to achieve what they called pseudo parallel systems with
the work leading from ALGOL to SIMULA.

So the ALGOLites used a pass BY NAME feature to pass you a reliable 
indication of where yet another pointer was. And you would have to double
dereference you pointer to pick up the real intended data.

Hoare thought even less of BY NAME than of BY REFERENCE.  There is 
another factor of early concepts that shows up in the cited paper. 
Early work in some cases tried to make subroutines as pure functions. 
This seems esoteric at first, a silly, boring distinction that is 
now actually rare and seems not related to COBOL or business.

Yet it is really quite important if you want to understand the departure
that was occuring that caused Hoare so much concern. (That departure
now has arrived at a net that connects very untrustworthy individuals
to business data storage).

Some uses of the term 'function' are very restricted. The word can mean 
something like a sub program that we call. But the code is not allowed
to have any 'side effects.'  For the sake of introducing the idea and
relating it to german shepherds. A side effect is simply any permanent 
record of the execution of the code in the sub module, like a move of
a value to memory. So you will occassionaly encounter this use of the term
function.
The only thing that happens in a 'function' is that it returns its 
value.

This is still a very power theme in some development environments. The gist
of this thing is that they wanted to extend mathematical formulas beyond the
simple arithmetic type operators. Functions were engaged to do calcs, these 
were not so much means to structure a system by modularizing it.

So Hoare's view of the 'interface' into a submodule is somewhat influenced
by a residual orientation to that kind of purpose.

From that earlier perspective, miscreant active attachments that interrogate
your Windows mailing lists and audaciously emit flurries of wormy cyberbytes 
out onto the net seem gastly indeed. Worms are geometrically expanding
side effects. Gastly, but to Hoare, maybe not such a surprise.


It is noteworthy that the title page to Hoare's published notes
indicate

Supported By
Advanced Research Projects Agency
ARPT Order No 24y4
Project CODE 3d30

Maybe the title of the quoted section  should have been
"References Considered Harmful."

Best Wishes
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

- **From:** "John Ford" <zjcf it DawnAndJohn dit net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-15T12:46:15-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3f8d87e7$0$41289$a1866201@authen.newsreader.visi.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0310010908.185ca01@posting.google.com> <20031001164647.10007.00000087@mb-m21.aol.com>`

```
Regarding the technique of replacing a calling program's return
address (that normally would return to the operating system), such
that when the calling program returns to its caller, it instead
goes to malicious code: I would think that the outcome would be
highly platform dependent.

The only platform with which I'm intimately familiar is an IBM
mainframe. In that case, simply changing the return address will
not change the security/permissions/rights context. You're simply
diverting execution to the malicious code with the exact same
context that the program had. The fact that the program that
called the malicious code is now "returning" to malicious code
doesn't change anything except the address of the next instruction
to execute.

Which platforms let you change permissions by altering a return
address?

-jcf

"RKRayhawk" <rkrayhawk@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20031001164647.10007.00000087@mb-m21.aol.com...
|
| KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM  (KELLIE FITTON)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| according to the cobol book that I am reading, some Submodules
that are called
| in the computer's open environments memory that have "active
components" or
| "Java applets" can overwrite the working areas of my programs or
even clobber
| the global variables. Is there a way to protect my cobol's
working area? should
| I use win32 APIs to LOCK the memory heaps? should I get a german
shepherd?
|
| >>
|
| This problem is universal and is not restricted to a COBOL
caller. A submodule
| can get to 'your' vaiables, 'your' code and global variables and
even
| supposedly secure executable code in memory (like the operating
system itself)
| .
|
| You are doing quite well if your academic work has carried you
to this
| threshold already.
|
| There is one critical factor and an important fact that will
cast a perspective
| on this issue for you as you start out.
|
| The submodule needs an address to do damage. With an address it
can start.
| Amounts can be added to addresses or subtracted from addresses.
Once a
| submodule has an adress it can _potentially_ change whatever is
there; code or
| data, or _potentially_ change what is nearby.
|
| You use the phrase 'open environment' I will let you define that
so folks can
| explore vulnerabilities in the particular kind of environment
that you are
| actually working with or that the book is explaining to you.
'Open' can mean a
| lot of things. The only closed computer environment is one with
the plug yanked
| out and all batteries removed.
|
| The basic issue is trusted computer environment versus not
trusted computer
| environment.  Can the _humans_ who have access to your system be
trusted?
|
| The perspective is that in the olden days pieces of the systems
were usually
| only connected to devices in places where there were folks who
could be
| trusted, by and large.
|
| Now, what is different, is that we are connecting many computer
environments to
| everyone.
|
| If your schools system provides any email service for you
outbound, that means
| that it might actually sometimes relay email through
| its memory and on to another system.  It is not inconceivable
that someone in a
| cave on the otherside of the world could relay something through
the system at
| your academic institution.
| It is possible that you would prefer that it not become active
anywhere along
| the line near you. So you have a need to understand 'active',
but the issue
| that has changed is proximity.
|
| This did not used to happen. We are now connecting huge
corporate information
| repositories to the global net. That is a very big issue. It is
a change.
|
| A submodule, by the loose definition we may attribute to it
here, has the
| characteristic of being activated. An activated entity inside of
a computer can
| change data, code or addresses
| of data or code. Because we have connected more computers to
more persons with
| unproven trustworthiness, anything that can become active is an
important
| management concern.
|
| Active Controls, and Java Applets are only the begining.
ANYTHING that can
| become active is an issue. It does not matter what the source
code language or
| the development environment or even the runtime environment.
|
| In addition to executable code and data, most modern systems
have attributes
| called privilege.
| This is information that is associated with an executable, a
thing which might
| become active.
|
| For example, the operating system, or even a productivity suite,
might be
| executed in an environment with very high privileges; allowing
them to update
| anything.  Such high-level code may call your program (say a
COBOL program),
| setting you up with fairly low privileges.
|
| When your code is invoked the operating context must hand you a
return address
| so that when you are done you can go home to the operating
system (or Office
| Suite program).
|
| During processing you elect to invoke something. You activate by
perhaps any
| number of conventional strategies that are acceptable practices
in your shop.
| If you intend for that submodule to return to you when it is
done you must pass
| your return address to it. If you want that submodule do do
anything to your
| data you are likely to consider passing an address to your data.
It is not
| unlikely that the return address for the operating system was
stored in a
| memory location close to the data you want to
| pass to the submodule.
|
| The submodule is activated. It does many things so that the
world will think it
| is just real nice.  But it has a malicious piece of code. It
cannot execute the
| bad code, because to do its evil it needs privileges it does not
have and that
| you do not have. It uses the address to your data that you want
changed to hunt
| around and find the return address that will ultimately leap
from your code
| back to the operating system, and switches it to the entry point
for its own
| malicious code.
|
| So far no sign of trouble. The subprogram returns to you.  You
got great data
| results from the submodule, you publish and present magnificent
indications
| that all is well, ... and then, .... drum roll please, ... and
then you return
| to the return address.
|
| The hardware takes you now to the malicious code, and it now has
the privileges
| of the OS or productivity office suite that had invoked you.
|
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| The object oriented technologies are actually going to make this
a little
| worse, because they tend to cluster addresses of functions in
memory. Once a
| hacker finds a zone of interests there are then numerous
addresses adjacent
| that are of value. This will be true of COBOL object oriented
code, just like
| anyother. Object Orientation happens greatly modularizes code,
and as a fact
| collects addresses for those modules into arrays. Call them
sitting ducks.
|
| On PCs the stack pointer itself can be used to get to your
stack, which is
| where your invokers return address is ... there really isn't
much chaining to
| be done.
|
| On a mainframe return addresses live briefly in one of the
registers of the
| architected register file (specifically R14). A module like your
module will
| must likely have saved that in a zone managed by another
register (usually
| R13). R13 chains are a bit more of a challenge then stack
pointers. But the
| technique is intended to be used by average programmers.
|
| And more important, Intel devices are widely distributed, as are
copies of the
| MicroSoft packages, so bad people can experiment and research
for hours on end
| until they find an exploit path. The older equipment is not
acutally
| less vulnerable, it is just _less_ accessible for research
purposes.
|
| Eventually there will be german shepherds, but I am afraid that
hardware and
| software vendors are entirely prepared to see us pay a much
heavy price before
| any real change occurs.
|
| Most systems do not treat addresses of code in any special way
once it is
| stored. That is a generic problem. If the address of executable
code is stored,
| and that location can be manipulated, then the system cannot be
considered
| secure.
|
| Note that in object orientation, we can override methods. In
some
| implementations this means that the array with method addresses
has selected
| cells updated at instantiation time.
|
| The larger the number of untrustworthy people connected to such
a system, the
| more the security decreases.
|
| We eventually need to wrap every passed address and every stored
address with
| FITTON shepherd attributes. That is probably a long way off.
|
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

- **Message-ID:** `<20031020133625.06910.00000526@mb-m28.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3f8d87e7$0$41289$a1866201@authen.newsreader.visi.com>`

```


"John Ford", zjcfit DawnAndJohn dit net, 
had a comment to which I wanted to respond cautiously and with 
humility. I don't want to do anything like write a new recipe, 
or repeat one that is current. But I have found some old exploits 
that can be referenced responsibly.

Let me quote his entire post that ends with the question I'll address.

From: "John Ford" zjcfit DawnAndJohn dit net 
Date: 10/15/03 12:46 PM EST
Message-id: <3f8d87e7$0$41289$a1866201@authen.newsreader.visi.com>
<<
Regarding the technique of replacing a calling program's return
address (that normally would return to the operating system), such
that when the calling program returns to its caller, it instead
goes to malicious code: I would think that the outcome would be
highly platform dependent.

The only platform with which I'm intimately familiar is an IBM
mainframe. In that case, simply changing the return address will
not change the security/permissions/rights context. You're simply
diverting execution to the malicious code with the exact same
context that the program had. The fact that the program that
called the malicious code is now "returning" to malicious code
doesn't change anything except the address of the next instruction
to execute.

Which platforms let you change permissions by altering a return
address?

-jcf>>


Here are two answers to your question.


Solaris:  http://secinf.net/info/unix/stack.txt

" How to bypass Solaris non-executable stack protection "
This is an old Solaris/SPARC return into libc strategy. 
Keep in mind that Itanium, a 
supposedly more efficient processor because of the Register
Stack Engine, has stack technology like the SPARC, and makes
the hacker more efficient in discovery efforts and exploitation
endeavors as the linkage back track does not require reference 
to memory but is instead right there in the CPU.
The technical term for this is CPU family kinship is register
window based linkages.


Windows:  http://www.nextgenss.com/papers/non-stack-bo-windows.pdf

"Non-stack Based Exploitation of Buffer Overrun Vulnerabilities on
Windows NT/2000/XP"

This has comments distinguishing stack based overflow 
from non-stack based overflow (buzz words, both involve the stack),
along with the comment, "... The non-stack based buffer overflow
exploit writer doesn't even need to know assembly language."

The original poster,KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM  (KELLIE FITTON),
Date: 10/1/03 12:08 PM EST, Message-id:
<41758a6b.0310010908.185ca01@posting.google.com>
|
asked about curruption of memory beyond the 
bounds of data items passed in a COBOL CALL to a submodule. My touch
point was that once you pass a reference we are off to the races.

Keeping this thread tied to COBOL and the notion that we are 
connecting valued assets on large computers to a
growing number of people, there is an extension of the lesson on 
connecting to untrustworthy persons available. The original poster,
a student, may wish to begin to learn that there are levels of trust 
and levels of risk.

You can read about how "Slammer Crashed Monitoring System at 
Ohio Nuclear Plant, ... In January, the Slammer worm apparently 
made its way into the computer network at Ohio's Davis-Besse 
nuclear power plant, crashing an important monitoring system. "

at

http://www.securityfocus.com/news/6767

The critical issue is connectivity to untrustworthy persons.
But if you want advanced lessons you must begin to learn the
meaning of who can be trusted (and just as security people will
turn the phrase who has a 'need to know'), the issue in connectivity
becomes who do you '_need_ to trust'.

The cited page mentions a 1997 report it quotes ...


" '[T]he distinct trend within the industry is to link the systems to 
access control center data necessary for business purposes,' reads the 
report. 'One utility interviewed considered the business value of access 
to the data within the control center worth the risk of open connections 
between the control center and the corporate network.'

"Future Safety Concerns 

"An energy sector cybersecurity expert who's reviewed nuclear plant 
networks, speaking on condition of anonymity, said the trend of linking 
operations networks with corporate LANs continues unabated within the 
nuclear energy industry, because of the economic benefits of giving 
engineers easy access to plant data. An increase in plant efficient of a 
couple percentage points 'can translate to millions upon millions of 
dollars per year,' says the expert. 
He says Slammer's effect on Davis-Besse highlights the dangers of such 
interconnectivity. " 


There are two bits of humor in that, first the fun phrase 'cybersecurity
expert.' And then deep in the article cited you will find that it was
a consultant they were holding responsible for a misstep. 
Lynch the outlanders!

-----------------------
You can read about other effects of  Slammer at

http://www.cert.org/advisories/CA-2003-04.html

http://www.kb.cert.org/vuls/id/484891


You can view increasing levels of detail about Slammer at

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SQL_slammer_worm

http://www.caida.org/analysis/security/sapphire/
 (has an interesting graphic).


And if you want to mainline it to the core of your brain visit

SAPPHIRE WORM CODE DISASSEMBLED
http://www.eeye.com/html/Research/Flash/sapphire.txt


There are numerous styles of replacing return addresses and forward
pointing addresses to functions. The reference is the starting point.
A reference to executable code is a distinct category of reference.
Activation records, as they are called by compiler writers, have
the return address in them (this is enregistered on the one mainframe 
that is implicit in interactions on this thread, ... where it is
more exposed then if it were in memory (actually all of that chatter is
MVS specific, the ELF standard for the UNICES puts an item in R15 that
is equivalent to an Intel SP, and the return address is in memory,... 
I think at displacement 0).


All of this is old. But it is really worth mentioning that it was 
also old at the time of the attack on the power plant.  In light of
this we must review any sense within ourselves that the mom and pop 
business machine operators should just get a system administrator. We
need to fight the arrogance, ... we have all been responsible for that.

The SPARC and the Intel boxes are cracked more often because they are
more widely distributed and more widely connected than mainframes.
The mainframes are no less vulnerable, just less researched.

IMHO any device in the register window based linkage CPU category is 
orders of magnitude easier to research than a technology that is 
pushing the tokens of the backtrack to memory, where there is atleast 
a hope of protecting them ('though not much practice).

More generally, remote activation is inherently dangerous. When it is 
accomplished only by stealth, then we need to keep tightening. But 
when remote activation occurs because system purveyors have defaulted 
all active controls to submit to external control then there is a moral 
issue that lands back at the software and hardware designer's desk.

As we use COBOL or anything else, to create web sites that only work when
you submit to external control, and will not work practically if you say no,
then we are propagating an arrogant culture that denies access to anyone
who manifests normal self-interest.

The wild bird comes home to roost when the infected system broadcasts
the damage back to an important node, say a bank, say a clearing house,
say a power plant, say a power network. IF you have an accident at an 
intersection because the lights are out, you would care. If you had an
important other at a hospital denied power, you would empathise. We need
to join together.

This concern is also owned by developers and maintainers of COBOL systems
that are increasingly exposed by connection in 'interoperable' environments
that have the more seriously researched devices along the lines, even
if we are not coding the extensions and enhancements with COBOL. We own
this issue.

We need to address this from the software development side. We need 
technicians who are ethical. We are in this together. We need to build
down these walls of arrogance. It is not reasonable to assume that 
connectors agree to surrender _control_ of their information assets, be they
small businesses or large industrial operations.

Building systems around the central feature of remote activation is not
responsible.

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

- **From:** "Richard Maher" <maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-04T07:28:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bllsra$s4$1@sparta.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0310010908.185ca01@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

On VMS (as well as I'm sure other OSs) memory can be protected at different
access modes. (We have User, Supervisor, Exec and Kernel) Your code normally
runs at User mode and its memory has User Mode Read/Write protection. (On
VMS that implies all inner-mode access but apparently on Itanium this can
change so that Kernel can be denide access even with USER:RW but anyway. .
.)

To get into an inner mode, like EXEC, (in the absence of CMEXEC privilege)
you'd need to write a special sub-program/DLL routine called a User-Written
System Service that handles the change of mode from USER to EXEC for you.
The problem here is that you are now in an implicitly privileged environment
and must take extreme care not to call out to any other RTL routine that can
not be completely trusted. This is why COBOL is seldom used for UWSSs
because the compiler generates all sorts of RTL calls in the code it
produces. But you can use C or MACRO to code your routine and then overlay a
working-storage area with an EXTERNAL variable. If your (or anyone else's)
USER mode code tries to write to that variable(s) you'll get an access
violation ss$_accvio.

Anyway none of this is probably of much use to you but at least you know
it's an option :-)

Regards Richard Maher


KELLIE FITTON <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:41758a6b.0310010908.185ca01@posting.google.com...
> Hello everyone,
>
> according to the cobol book that I am reading, some Submodules that are
called
> in the computer's open environments memory that have "active components"
or
> "Java applets" can overwrite the working areas of my programs or even
clobber
> the global variables. Is there a way to protect my cobol's working area?
should
> I use win32 APIs to LOCK the memory heaps? should I get a german shepherd?
>
> thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-10-04T16:24:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031004122423.09820.00000195@mb-m25.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<bllsra$s4$1@sparta.btinternet.com>`

```
"Richard Maher" maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com 
Date: 10/4/03 2:28 AM EST
Message-id: <bllsra$s4$1@sparta.btinternet.com>

Posts excellent comments about VMS and important experience also in an Itanium
context with reference to access privileges.

I would like to ask several questions.

My focus is on addresses of executable code.

With the Register Stack Architecture of the Itanium, are we not gaining the
efficiency of avoiding explicit push of the stack frame to memory? Thus
portions of the stack frame sequences remain instantiated inside of the CPU for
longer.
Right?

So if an address like a return address is not in memory but is still in a CPU
resource, are we not then concerned about the privileges of access to the
register stack instead of privileges of access to memory?  So, can not a
malicious or errant subroutine munge a return address more easily in this
context?

And if the instantaneously available stack is 96 entries deep, isn't it the
case that the caller's caller's return address is likely still instantaneously
available; such that I really do not have to do any stack frame hopping?  For
access to the higher level return addresses, the efficiency of the RSA
(register stack architeture) makes the malignant code more efficient. Would you
agree with this general proposition?

If some higher level return address will be popped at a privilege transition
point, can not the lower level routine gain possession of those
privileges via the more readily available CPU resident register stack with
modification privileges determined in a manner distinct from privileges
constraining stored memory access?

This would also apply to any function addresses passed around as parms, not
just return addresses.

Also, seemingly esoteric to business programmers, but not really if we are
interoperating with Java and C# on Itaniums holding significant business
assets; what of the abitlity to manually control "register spill and fill"? The
focus is addresses but from the standpoint of the supposed protections that we
would get from VMS or a Linux.

In your experience so far, can you see a way for an outter routine to willfully
fill the register stack in order to get at much higher information including
passed function addresses and return addresses? My tight focus here is this, is
the fill/spill action protected?

Can a bad guy fill the stack, modify it (taking control of a code vector), and
spill it again; filling with the original image to cover the tracks?

So, from what you have seen so far, are we getting what major businesses would
want in 
the access privileges to memory, to the register stack, and to the ability to
spill/fill stacks that contain references to executable code?

Incidentally, I do love Intel Architecture. I find using my dual Xeon Penguin
Computing system absolutely exhilarating.  The accomplishments of Intel with
the more advanced Itanium based systems are extremely important.  But so far I
do not see protection of code addresses, the essential element of activation.

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-10-07T16:59:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031007125956.17428.00000490@mb-m26.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20031004122423.09820.00000195@mb-m25.aol.com>`

```

The original poster considers 'open environments' and 'active components'.

The following current day news item may be instructive.  

http://money.cnn.com/2003/10/07/technology/vivendi_code.reut/index.htm?cnn=yes

"Vivendi's 'Half Life II' code stolen 
Firm delays video game's release after hacker breaks into e-mail, steals 
a third of source code.
October 7, 2003: 7:26 AM EDT "

When environments are not secure, data can unexpectedly become active.

Connecting to untrustworthy contact points reduces the security of a system and
everything that is on it.

For COBOLers, or anyone serious about business data, the issues are how much to
connect, and can anything from the contact point migrate over to other systems?
 

Email is email, but corporate assets, like source code for products, is not
email. Why are corporate assets like that connected to email
systems where data can unexpectedly become active?

Simple. People are not thinking business, they are thinking connectivity.

The product source code was probably neither in any email nor probably within
the mail protocol processor. The hacker had to follow a chain back. The pointer
to that chain was handed to the hacker for free. We can call these pointers
references (although we need a number of words to name the varieties).

References are sometimes as simple as a data item passed BY REFERENCE from a
calling program to a submodule. Such references can be to data or even involve
linkage to a PROCEDURE-POINTER.

In some environments invocation of a submodule also generates a return address,
a kind of PROCEDURE-POINTER as it were; and access to this item is sometimes
provided to the executable submodule  even
enabling modification (switching it).

Generally, on stack based hardware, malignant submodules can get to the chain
of much of what is placed on the stack by higher and higher
levels of software, involving higher and higher levels of privilege. Some
posters indicate that is not always easy with some architectures.

Modern email system, frequently (but not necessarily) are provided with
all manner of conveniences, including smooth facilities for presentation of the
email letter itself and increasing number of attachment types.  Regrettably,
attachments, originating as data, can unexpectedly become active. They are
active within the context of very powerful office productivity suites, and are
routinely these days handed access to the entire disk systems. 

Absolutely everything is done by software and hardware vendors to make it easy
to get to anything from active email attachments. That is not business
thinking.

So we are connecting to more and more untrustworthy individuals. For business
systems coders and managers, the issue is what should we attach? Furthermore,
can attachment activatability potentially survive? Can the damaging code or
interrogating code take up residence, become active
after the email 'session' ends? Gathering a storm for later occurrence.

Active attachments can cause greatly magnified damage if they are provided with
a reference.  The conveniences of modern office suites are the references. The
facilities to get to registries, the facilities to get to file systems (the
directories), the facilities to get to object repositories, the facilities to
execute the whole array of methods within the office suites, all by a complete
stranger, you understand; these are elaborated mechanism that have the
essential characteristic of a reference. We are handing this stuff to people
outside of the business for free.

The capabilities of these modern systems are fully documented. The
documentation is usually free and available online. 

So the communication from a trusted computing action to an untrustworthy
computing action of any kind of a reference to data or to executable is the
technical requirement. The social factor that puts it in perspective, is that
we are connecting to more and more untrustworthy individuals.

The very notion of active attachments for communication from outside of a
business interest is incompetent.

Operating systems and productivity suites that default to permit  activation of
anything from outside of a business are an assault by 
arrogant technical professionals on the most valued assets of a business. These
products are making the hacker productive!

Associating the attribute 'remote' with anything like a procedure call is
suspect behavior by designers and coders, when the activation occurs on the
machine of an unwilling system operator. This stuff is not business
thinking, these are the childlike conceptions of individuals anesthetized by
there wealth.

I frankly believe that it is appropriate for the business community to discuss
mandatory recall of software that allows an outsider to determine what becomes
active. There is an urgent need to shut down activation when
it originates outside of a business. The proximate cause is located at the
_designer_ of the software product that allows activation. 

The motivation of the designer is profit. The incompetent design is rendering a
loss of control of the operator's system. No garbage paper in the shrink wrap
or pixelated screen flash constitutes an agreement to loose
control of that which the business owns. This arrogance needs to end.


Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-10-08T10:27:48+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<20031004122423.09820.00000195@mb-m25.aol.com> <20031007125956.17428.00000490@mb-m26.aol.com>`

```
Bob,

Having just spent a couple of weeks wading through the security on a medium
size LAN system, I'm not sure I completely agree with your analysis.

There are facilities available that can make your networked system extremely
secure, certainly beyond the capabilities of the average "bad guy" to
circumvent.

My problem was getting stuff to run across various servers because the
in-place permissions would not allow legitimate applications to run... kind
of the opposite of what you outlined.

You are right that attachments from outside the Company that can activate
ANY software should be viewed with suspicion, but there are cases where the
ability to activate standard system software remotely can be a very useful
feature. If the right permissions are in place, there is no reason why such
software should be regarded as "dangerous", let alone "recalled".

I don't share your concern about pointers and BY REFERENCE, neither do I
agree that  arrogant system designers are motivated only by money and don't
care about security (certainly isn't true in my case...).

I believe that the employment of a competent Network Administrator can
render many of your concerns irrelevant. Sadly many SMEs can't or won't
invest in such a resource. Some "technical" operations like small software
houses believe they have enough competence in house to prevent "burglaries";
the fact is that Network Administration is a specialized and highly complex
skill that requires someone who understands it properly and has enough
experience to plug the gaps.

The Press (read "uninformed journalists") love to make much of "security
holes" in Operating Systems and system software. In practice, millions of
installations process billions of transactions every day and never have a
security problem.

It would be foolish to abandon the network because it is perceived to be
"insecure". The fact is, it can be as secure as you are prepared to make it.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-10-08T03:47:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031007234724.15771.00000341@mb-m13.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" dashwood@enternet.co.nz 
Date: 10/7/03 7:27 PM EST
Message-id: <3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com>

Made valued comments, concluding with ...

<<

It would be foolish to abandon the network because it is perceived to be
"insecure". The fact is, it can be as secure as you are prepared to make it.

>>

You are right. 

The problem is the generalization of these impressive system features.  When we
turn on the capability for the good guys we turn it on for the bad guys.

In a small way you are dancing very near the area that I call arrogant. Kind of
a dangerous word to use, I know, especially when there are such generous
contributors such as your self that might sense that expression wrong. 

I am calling to task everyone who is anyone technically. It really is not
personal.

The idea that the fix for people who have had damage to their system is to let
them have a system administrator, is exactly analogous to let them have cake.
We need to wake up.

I have interacted as a consultant, looking for an opportunity in Atlanta, with
a non-white placement firm. They were not able to retrieve my resume on the day
of the call back because there system was down.  A virus was making it crash as
soon as it booted. Those fighting their way into the economy from the least
advantaged position are the ones least able to combat the incompetence of
remote control system destruction.  They are the least able to afford another
expensive employee, the system administrator.  And it is harsh to describe them
as unprepared, they are doing everything they can to be prepared.  Such groups
are the ones who would benefit most from computer technology, the efficiencies
give them a fighting chance.

I have interacted with female self employed placement professionals from
Columbia, South Carolina to Los Angeles, California, who have spent useless
time trying to reinstall productivity suites and operating systems. Folks in
this category are generally less financed by banks, less favored by consumers
of the placement services, and in fact disadvataged because they are not
associated with a large establishment that can afford to pay someone to set
permissions, review CERT warnings and to apply patches.  The exact people that
information technology should help the most as a playing field leveler are the
most harmed by the data processing industry's refusal to achnowledge a serious
problem.  These people do no agree to relinquish control of their assets when
they buy computers or software or ISP sevices. They have not agreed to lose
control. 

I am interested in continuing my education at Berkeley by taking a Java course
online. I noticed that a day or two into the semester, all Berkeley.edu sites I
needed were not up. There were reports that day that major university were
finding a sudden surge of viruses as students returned to school and commenced
using campus systems for email (I am not clear on how extensive or how long the
shut down lasted). This is a big problem. It is a real problem. And it is
fundamental.

The handing of a reference to an internal executable capability to an active
mechanism that came from outside of the business computer is not wise.

The arrogance is not coming from excellent technicians such as yourself who
would use these capabilities. The arrogance comes from a number of people in
much more important positions. The designer of the foundation software that
installs with active capabilities by default is responsible. The purveyor of
OSes and productivity suites that by default allow the external control.

I do not think that we should abandon the network. However, I will stick with
my basic view, 
which is we have not thought through the idea of 
remotely activated controls. The entire first generation must be considered a
failure. It is causing palpable damage, and eroding confidence in the
technology and the people who deliver it.  

I believe that OSes, productivity suites, _and_ the hardware that delivered
them, should be subject to recall if they are bring forth loss of control of
the business's assets. No one is agreeing to the loss of control. There is a
fundamental problem here.

It is exactly here, where the fun technology moves away from the toasters and
in the direction of the serious corporate assets that the tripwire must spark.

We have a need for technicians that better understand references, and a need
for professionals that understand peoples lives 
are impacted when the net delivers active damage. Those people count and they
do not
want cake. They want reliable system that they
can trust.

We have all been arrogant, and there is clear evidence that the time for change
has arrived. 
We need to bring this back home and rethink it.



Best Wishes
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-08T05:18:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G6Ngb.70126$Of2.2802252@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com> <20031007234724.15771.00000341@mb-m13.aol.com>`

```
"RKRayhawk" <rkrayhawk@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20031007234724.15771.00000341@mb-m13.aol.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" dashwood@enternet.co.nz
> Date: 10/7/03 7:27 PM EST
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> "insecure". The fact is, it can be as secure as you are prepared to make
it.
>
> >>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The problem is the generalization of these impressive system features.
When we
> turn on the capability for the good guys we turn it on for the bad guys.

This reminds me of the reaction to 9-11 over in the US.  They talked at
length at how they could prevent terrorists from taking over planes.
One of the proposals was to have the plane be setup such that it could be
controlled remotely.  This would obviously prevent the terrorists from being
able to fly into buildings.
This is of course very true....now they could be sitting by the pool at the
hilton and crash a plane into a building.

The fact is that the only proper method to handle safety is to strip search
everyone and put them on randomly assigned seats across a number of planes
on random days....but I will settle for some searches and the banning of all
carry on luggage.

> In a small way you are dancing very near the area that I call arrogant.
Kind of
> a dangerous word to use, I know, especially when there are such generous
> contributors such as your self that might sense that expression wrong.

Not arrogant.  I prefer to think on the optimistic side of wary :-)

I'm always amazed at the assertion that the networks are safe or unsafe.
Most problems are related in fact to dumb end users.....people who allow
remote access to phone mail, people who email their work to their home
account (if the top clearance Mr Lee does it I'm sure most have).    Some
applications have back door passwords.  Many applications arbitrarily su to
root...no one can monitor everything.

I get monthly reminders at work NOT to run KAZAA!?!?

The most secure network access I have seen is still only two written
passwords, Exceed,  and a keychain away from being made very crippled.

The fact is that every day I lock my front door, set my alarm before I
leave.  I also know that a brick will get through my window, it takes 15
minutes to get the police to my house and most valuable items are within
reach.  Fortunately, for most people I don't have a handgun that will
subsequently be used to shoot someone else.

Bad people are more creative than good people...this is a fact.  That is why
some of the most successful security vendors are made up of "unqualified"
hackers.
I read an excellent article once on the two types of businesses that existed
to trap hackers exploiting vulnerabilities - one was a business with proper
employment records and clean criminal records (given the nature of the
access to secure systems they were given) - the other was made up of a
"collection" of various net people who worked on a freelance basis.   They
had opinions on which was more effective, and then also, which was more
liability friendly.

> I am interested in continuing my education at Berkeley by taking a Java
course
> online. I noticed that a day or two into the semester, all Berkeley.edu
sites I
> needed were not up. There were reports that day that major university were
> finding a sudden surge of viruses as students returned to school and
commenced
> using campus systems for email (I am not clear on how extensive or how
long the
> shut down lasted). This is a big problem. It is a real problem. And it is
> fundamental.
How many people have had access to google messed up recently? Do a google
search QHosts.  People infected didn't even have to do anything wrong - just
be a patch behind on IE.  So people blame Micro$oft.....ever been on a
RedHat notification list? Their builds get hacked too.


> The arrogance is not coming from excellent technicians such as yourself
who
> would use these capabilities. The arrogance comes from a number of people
in
> much more important positions. The designer of the foundation software
that
> installs with active capabilities by default is responsible. The purveyor
of
> OSes and productivity suites that by default allow the external control.
But most problems are still applications hosted (by choice) on those
systems...P2P in particular.....when was the last time you downloaded an ISO
and actually checked the message digest from the official site?
More often than not, it's the applications you choose to use.  I use outlook
express....I have preview pane enabled....I'm an idiot.  But I also enable
active x - why ? because until I am liable for damage done by my pc I cannot
be bothered to double click, answer pop ups and have web sites that don't
work.  I have a pc image that I can bring up when disaster hits.

> I do not think that we should abandon the network. However, I will stick
with
> my basic view,
> which is we have not thought through the idea of
> remotely activated controls. The entire first generation must be
considered a
> failure. It is causing palpable damage, and eroding confidence in the
> technology and the people who deliver it.
Once you have a proper network setup and configured you have to have some
faith - it's unfortunate that not all people have actively managed firewalls
and demiliterized zones with strong authentication and authorization.  It's
a risk - but so is driving a Mini Cooper on roads with an Escalade.  The sad
fact is - as you point out - it's the small businesses that probably get hit
the hardest because they just don't have the support structure.

> I believe that OSes, productivity suites, _and_ the hardware that
delivered
> them, should be subject to recall if they are bring forth loss of control
of
> the business's assets. No one is agreeing to the loss of control. There is
a
> fundamental problem here.
It's called liability and it wont happen.
Ever seen a termite warranty - we'll cover you for $300 000 as long as (a)
You have no wood touching the ground (b) You have no moisture and (c) You
don't have any areas that are termite bad spots.....the technical
equivalent? We'll protect you financially as long as you remove the
immediate risks....disable you pc from the network.

> We have a need for technicians that better understand references, and a
need
> for professionals that understand peoples lives
> are impacted when the net delivers active damage. Those people count and
they
> do not want cake. They want reliable system that they can trust.
> We have all been arrogant, and there is clear evidence that the time for
change
> has arrived.
> We need to bring this back home and rethink it.
Cars have been stolen since the day they were fast enough to get away in.
They still haven't fixed the problem.

It comes down to very simple tenets : Evaluate your risk, Evaluate the value
of that risk, and take good ROI measures to protect it.
If you have Credit Cards (which people won't use online but will just hand
to a $3 / hour waiter) don't email it.
If you have Customer Credit cards *really* don't email it.
If you have Large Numbers of Customer Credit cards *really* don't email it,
and hire someone to make it safe for you.

Then hope.

> Best Wishes
> Bob Rayhawk
> RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-08T05:31:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yiNgb.70128$Of2.2803452@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20031004122423.09820.00000195@mb-m25.aol.com> <20031007125956.17428.00000490@mb-m26.aol.com> <3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com...
> Bob,
>
> Having just spent a couple of weeks wading through the security on a
medium
> size LAN system, I'm not sure I completely agree with your analysis.
>
> There are facilities available that can make your networked system
extremely
> secure, certainly beyond the capabilities of the average "bad guy" to
> circumvent.
>
> My problem was getting stuff to run across various servers because the
> in-place permissions would not allow legitimate applications to run...
kind
> of the opposite of what you outlined.
>
> You are right that attachments from outside the Company that can activate
> ANY software should be viewed with suspicion, but there are cases where
the
> ability to activate standard system software remotely can be a very useful
> feature. If the right permissions are in place, there is no reason why
such
> software should be regarded as "dangerous", let alone "recalled".
You're right on.  The issue is not the connectivity but the authorization /
authentication.

Sure anything can run remote when I accept it - but that's a typical end
user no no (sure they software facilitates this).

In your instance you have a specific setup for remote access and this can be
made secure (relatively).

I think the issue is don't mix Servers and Clients.  Keep end users off the
machines that have access to data.

What I don't understand is how someone can "STEAL" code - you're telling me
that they took it AND removed all copies of it? I always thought games
companies had smart people (my friend would blush if he heard me give him a
compliment like that).

> I believe that the employment of a competent Network Administrator can
> render many of your concerns irrelevant. Sadly many SMEs can't or won't
> invest in such a resource.
Mostly a ROI statement.  You don't need insurance *until* your car crashes.
If it's only a beat up old Skoda/CV2/Gremlin (insert crappy car of choice)
it's not worth it.

> The Press (read "uninformed journalists") love to make much of "security
> holes" in Operating Systems and system software. In practice, millions of
> installations process billions of transactions every day and never have a
> security problem.
They like to pick on MS too.  I'm wondering if /when a dominant Linux build
gets a hefty portion of the market that the same level of effort will be
spend on the applications that aren't secure.  I've not heard of too many
stories of MVS / z/OS being hacked recently.

> It would be foolish to abandon the network because it is perceived to be
> "insecure". The fact is, it can be as secure as you are prepared to make
it.
>
> Pete.


JCE

P.S. Cobol Rules.....just to keep at least one of my posts related to the
group :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-08T12:42:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310081142.3afb524b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20031004122423.09820.00000195@mb-m25.aol.com> <20031007125956.17428.00000490@mb-m26.aol.com> <3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com> <yiNgb.70128$Of2.2803452@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote 

> They like to pick on MS too.  I'm wondering if /when a dominant Linux build
> gets a hefty portion of the market that the same level of effort will be
> spend on the applications that aren't secure.  

The problem with Windows is that every copy is exactly the same.  Well
there are variations such as '95, '98, .. XP and there are some
patches, but generally if there is a mechanism to attack there are
millions of identical ones.

With Linux it is not only that there are dozens of distributions, each
with different versions, but also the system can be recompiled by the
implementor or even the user.

With buffer overflows, for example, such as MSBlaster, the overflow
must be exactly the correct bytes to create the code at the exact
addresses.  Any variation by one byte and the attack fails.  It might
screw up the program, but it will fail to connect.

While the majority of web servers run Apache on Linux or BSD (up now
to 64.6%, MS is down at 23.46%) each of these may be recompiled,
patched, use different modules, have different compiler options ... If
a buffer overflow works in one of these versions then the chances of
it working in another is almost nil.  Similar effects with other
mechanisms.

This is the result of having 'standards' based on standards rather
than being 'industry standards' based an implementation (as Windows
is).

It doesn't make Linux immune, but even though it has twice the share
of servers, it is less vulnerable through huge diversity.  Just as
vaccinating a proportion of the population protects almost everyone
against diseases, diversity prevents epidemics that Windows suffers.

Much the same happens with mail readers. The majority of Windows users
will use Outlook Express.  Linux users have dozens and choose to suit
their needs - and none have the vulnerability that will execute code
when a message or attachment is opened, none will automatically feed
an attachment into Word with macro execution turned on.

With Windows users I tend to put in a simple firewall using an old 486
or P100 box running, say, Freesco or Smoothwall and recommend that
they use an in-house web based EMail such as 602's LAN Suite.

This way it cuts down the amount of effort to just those ports that
you want to let through.

Also visit www.grc.com and get a port scan and notes on security, such
as:

"""
DCOMbobulator
Tame Windows insecure DCOM facility	  

Ever since Windows 98, a powerful, but unnecessary and rarely used
facility known as DCOM (Distributed Component Object Model) has been
an integral part of Windows. The DCOMbobulator checks the
effectiveness of Microsoft's security patches and allows the user to
increase the security of their system by simply turning DCOM off.
"""
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-09T15:38:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xhfhb.84839$eS5.14376@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-09T20:09:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310091909.6cc31225@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20031004122423.09820.00000195@mb-m25.aol.com> <20031007125956.17428.00000490@mb-m26.aol.com> <3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com> <yiNgb.70128$Of2.2803452@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <217e491a.0310081142.3afb524b@posting.google.com> <xhfhb.84839$eS5.14376@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote 

> > The problem with Windows is that every copy is exactly the same. ...

> However, the main selling point of windows is *exactly* that.

Maybe, but it is a falacy that it is one user interface that reduces
training costs.  There are sufficient changes in the various GUIs and
options to mean that it is no more difficult to move to, say, KDE,
than to XP.

> Why do people choose Redhat over Debian over Mandrake over Suse over
> Slackware etc.

Why do people choose Audi over BMW over Mercedes over Yugo ?

Why don't they just buy a Ford 'like everyone else does' ?

I use several different distros.  I have Mandrake on one of my desktop
machines and laptop, redhat on a server, Freesco on my router,
smoothwall on customer routers, Pocketlinux as terminal server, fli4l
and LTSP as print servers.

The distros compete in overlapping but somewhat different areas, just
as brands of cars or colas do.

> To be successful you have to be easy.  Easy cuts the need for
> administrators.  Lack of administrators and "standard builds" leads to what
> you describe.
> 
> Therefore. Businesses goals conflict business safety  >:-O

Yes, and they seem to be realising that.
 
> I specifically wondered if Linux would get the same attention once a
> distribution becomes the "Windows alternative"

It may do, but at least Unix/Linux is built onto a decent security
model from the start.  Windows still needs to dump all its 'legacy'
stuff that took no account of multi-user (for TSE) or proper security.

> > While the majority of web servers run Apache on Linux or BSD (up now
> > to 64.6%, 

> Do these number include 3rd Party "powered" by Apache? Just curious.

I don't know what you mean by this.  If it has 'pwered by Apache' then
the Apache web server is running.  The '3rd party' may be an addon or
a module loaded by Apache.
 
> > their needs - and none have the vulnerability that will execute code
> > when a message or attachment is opened, none will automatically feed
> > an attachment into Word with macro execution turned on.

> I'm not sure how you can say *none*.

Because there aren't any.  I suppose it is possible that someone may
write an EMail client that would automatically install software and
run it, but why ?  Most don't even do HTML unless specifically told
to.  Most users don't even know why this would be a problem, they
don't know why Word attachments are foolish to send and worse to
receive.  MS hides the fact even that there are Word documents in the
EMail from them.

> Even Linuxconf had major issues a while back which caused grief for Redhat
> or kept maintaining that they 'didn't own it'.

It wasn't 'sendmail' it was Qmail that for compatibility used a
wrapper with the name sendmail.  Redhat doesn't have Qmail in the
distro, it had to be loaded searately.

This is a major difference between Windows and Linux. MSBlaster and
many viruses were serious problems that attacked machines 'out of the
box', leading to machines becoming 'owned' by outsiders or destruction
of the system.  Linux security issues are mostly related to problems
that only arise with someone deliberately doing something wrong at the
console, not some kid the other side of the world.

> Again - when you download do you check the official message digest?  Do you
> know the source of your tar/rpm. :-)

Usually.
 
> > Also visit www.grc.com and get a port scan and notes on security, such
> These sites just *scare* me needlessly.....kind of like the termite control
> companies.....

Not 'needlessly', they scare you for a reason - to get you to buy
their software.  Actually I put ZoneAlarm on Windows machines, but use
GRC to check them.

> but I did download something trusting that it wasn't a front
> put up by someone to install millions of trojans.

Did you get a port scan ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-08T00:56:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310072356.1c07f747@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20031004122423.09820.00000195@mb-m25.aol.com> <20031007125956.17428.00000490@mb-m26.aol.com> <3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote

> It would be foolish to abandon the network because it is perceived to be
> "insecure". The fact is, it can be as secure as you are prepared to make it.

Or, more specifically: no more secure than one is capable of making
it.

MSBlaster attacked the Windows rpc port used for DCOM.  Having the
port closed makes it immune, having it open is only secure (if
patched) until the next attack.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol data protection? Get a dog...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-10-10T21:49:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031010174917.00574.00000108@mb-m04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```

One of the more effective posters here,

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" dashwood@enternet.co.nz 
Date: 10/7/03 7:27 PM EST
Message-id: <3f835b32$1_7@news.athenanews.com>

Commented in part, ...

<<
It would be foolish to abandon the network because it is perceived to be
"insecure". The fact is, it can be as secure as you are prepared to make it.

>>

In passing, let me cite  

http://money.cnn.com/2003/10/09/technology/hacker.reut/index.htm?cnn=yes

for several reasons. First, although we are obligated to keep this newsgroup at
or near the subject of COBOL. The real interest, atleast generally, is
business.

Additionally, IMHO, it is useful to reset the basic interpretation of the
danger. The danger on the internet is not perceived, it is very real.

So any and how, back to the top.

I find at the incredibly generous IBM site,

http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3lr10/6.1.2?DT=20
020920180651


 the following piece of information concerning 
the PROCEDURE DIVISION header, and particularly the USING clause ...

"Parameters specified in the USING phrase of a method Procedure Division 
header must be passed to the method BY VALUE. "

Now that dove-tails for me. But I have some questions and would like to lean on
some of you who are most knowledgable about the standards process.

Is this BY VALUE only rule for methods the standard? Are most of the vendors
sticking to it?

If it is the standard, is consciously an attempt to keep COBOL in a specific
category of procedural languages? Is this the establishment of a definition for
the language according to a theory that we may get more protection by avoiding
the 'common' reference items?

Or is this just what can be agreed on so far?

Clearly, in an interoperable environment, resolving the addresses when
attempting to
dereference a reference (back to the invokers WS or worse back up through the
LS of a mainline flatland program which can invoke an object) is a serious
problem. So I could see vendors resisting BY REFERENCE, just for
feasibility of early product getting to market soon.  

I am aware that you can pass an object reference to a method, which is a whole
nother can of worms. And indeed, because arrays are objects you can pass arrays
of object references. I need to understand that better. 

But it stands to reason that an object reference
is a different kind of thing than mere reference,
and there is atleast the possibility of evolving
seriously constrictive shepherding for itl.

So, the question is, does this rule reflect a conscious design decision to keep
COBOL in 
a category of procedural type languages that
prevent access by the invokee to the invoker's
data area? Has there been elaborate discussion of this in the standards
process, and does the rule reflect a fairly strong commitment to such a theory
of source coding?


Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus and Oracle compile problem.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-01T18:40:18+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<blf309$m25$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<bla549$gdb$1@flood.xnet.com> <blbu87$bjc$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <blc66b$df6$1@flood.xnet.com> <blcbjr$ctk$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <blclub$ism$1@flood.xnet.com>`

```

"Hemant Shah" <shah@typhoon.xnet.com> wrote in message
>  Thanks, this solved the compile problem, but now when I execute the
program
>  it is able to connect to the database, but I get ORA-1036 whenever I
execute
>  a SQL statement.

The Oracle error Message manual states :

ORA-01036 illegal variable name/number

Cause: Unable to find bind context on user side.

Action: Make sure that the variable being bound is in the SQL statement.



What's the SQL Statement you're attempting to execute?

SimonT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus and Oracle compile problem.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-01T23:03:50+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<blfiei$nht$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<bla549$gdb$1@flood.xnet.com> <blbu87$bjc$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <blc66b$df6$1@flood.xnet.com> <blcbjr$ctk$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <blclub$ism$1@flood.xnet.com> <blf309$m25$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <blf9uv$mp0$1@flood.xnet.com>`

```
>  The variable names are O.K. I can take the exact SQL statement and put it
>  into my C program and it works.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>  We are using charet(ebcdic) option to compile the code, I think
microfocus
>  is converting the variable name to EBCDIC and Oracle does not like it.

Hi.

With Oracle 8 and later, you need to specify the Cobsql directive
COBSQLTYPE=ORACLE8 , rather than COBSQLTYPE=ORACLE , due to some internal
Oracle changes between Oracle 7 and Oracle 8.

With that directive set, your app should run correctly. I checked this out
with an amended version of Oracle's sample2.pco, and your the 1036 error
(with CSQLT=ORACLE), and correct execution (with CSQLT=ORACLE8).

SimonT.
```
