<a id="t-0b7631fb9a"></a>
## Thread: The intrinsic string function and COMP-3 variables... — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### The intrinsic string function and COMP-3 variables...

- **From:** Fooer <member10382@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-20T11:03:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3501366.1066662226@dbforums.com>`

```

Here�s a question from total COBOL newbie...

Is there a way to get rid of my workaround below? I want to use a

COMP-3 declared variable (declared in an outputarea of a module I�m not
responsible of) together with the intinsic string function...



Thanks in advance for your input!



==============================================

CODE SNIPPET

==============================================

01  unsignedRC                       pic 9(4).

01  charRC                             pic x(5).



*//      workaround - the string COBOL-function doesn�t like

*//      COMP-3 variables too much...

         move returnCode in LOBS-READCHUNK-O to unsignedRC

         if returnCode in LOBS-READCHUNK-O < 0

             move '-' to charRC(1:1)

         else

             move '+' to charRC(1:1)

         end-if

         move unsignedRC to charRC(2:4)



         string 'Call to LOBS_readChunk failed!'

                ' RC=' unsignedRC

                ' LOB_ID=' LOBID in LOBS-READCHUNK-I

                ' ChunkNr=' chunkNumber in LOBS-READCHUNK-I

                delimited by size

                into logDescription in useventComm

         end-string
```

#### ↳ Re: The intrinsic string function and COMP-3 variables...

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-20T16:57:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4E-dnfenbIDdxwmiU-KYvw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3501366.1066662226@dbforums.com>`

```
Fooer wrote:
> Here�s a question from total COBOL newbie...
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 01  charRC                             pic x(5).
01  DUMMY                        PIC S9(5) SIGN IS LEADING SEPARATE.
>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>          move returnCode in LOBS-READCHUNK-O to unsignedRC

MOVE LOBS-READCHUNK-O TO DUMMY.
STRING 'xxx ' DUMMY DELIMITED SIZE INTO yyy.

>
>          if returnCode in LOBS-READCHUNK-O < 0
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>          end-string
```

#### ↳ Re: The intrinsic string function and COMP-3 variables...

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-20T17:04:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y5qdnYmf_q5HxgmiU-KYvg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3501366.1066662226@dbforums.com>`

```
Fooer wrote:

or

01  DUMMY2  PIC ----9.

MOVE RC TO DUMMY2

STRING 'xxx ' DUMMY2 ....

But this won't give you a "+" sign.
```

#### ↳ Re: The intrinsic string function and COMP-3 variables...

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-20T16:55:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310201555.8d6efaa@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3501366.1066662226@dbforums.com>`

```
Fooer <member10382@dbforums.com> wrote 

> Is there a way to get rid of my workaround below? I want to use a
> COMP-3 declared variable (declared in an outputarea of a module Iï¿½m not
> responsible of) together with the intinsic string function...

STRING isn't an 'intrinsic function', it is a COBOL verb.


      01  editedRC                     PIC ++++9.

           move returnCode in LOBS-READCHUNK-O to editedRC
>          string 'Call to LOBS_readChunk failed!'
>                 ' RC=' editedRC
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>          end-string
```

#### ↳ Re: The intrinsic string function and COMP-3 variables...

- **From:** Fooer <member10382@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T03:28:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3504560.1066721313@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3501366.1066662226@dbforums.com>`

```

Many thanks guys!

That saved me some crappy code...
```

<a id="t-010fe58d06"></a>
## Thread: Vutil Automation — 8 msgs

### Vutil Automation

- **From:** Mufasa2000 <member44940@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-20T15:54:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3502569.1066679651@dbforums.com>`

```

OK

I have flat files that I need to turn into text files so I can
import into SQL.



I was told to use vutil32 -e (extract)



Fine, but it always asks the key, and how many records.



I want to automate this where the KEY would default to 0 and records
default to 10,000 (I know if you specify more records than is available
it produces an error, but it still writes the file correctly).



I need this because it will be part of a batch file called through
by DTS (SQL)



Any help would be greatly appreciated



Thanks

Mufasa2000
```

Have you tried redirection?

Use Notepad to create a file with the answers as you would type them and
save it to something like vutil.txt.

Then run:    vutil32 -e .... < vutil.txt

I haven't tried this, but if it is reading from stdin, it should work.

news:3502569.1066679651@dbforums.com...

#### ↳ Re: Vutil Automation

- **From:** gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3503301.1066691783@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3502569.1066679651@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi Ji



Good!! It works...



A little addition: to redirect the records on a sequential file,
complete the command as:



VUTIL32 -E  indexed_filename < PARAM.TXT > filename.seq



where PARAM.TXT contains:



          to read on first key

            spaces to all key value

NNNN    number of record to read





Gianni
```

#### ↳ Re: Vutil Automation

- **From:** Mufasa2000 <member44940@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T10:44:27-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3505862.1066747467@dbforums.com>`

```

I worked on this last night, and ended up using the unload.
(thanks Daniel)



I did not get around to trying the other methods, but thanks for the
info.



The only problem with the unload is that it does not create line
delimeters.



I found a work around this when importing into SQL as every record is
the same length of characters.



I do not know much about cobol, so I copied the file I needed to a
seperate directory before I ran the unload command. (not sure if
nessesary, better safe than sorry)



I also found that UNC paths do not work very well when trying to call a
program so I created a mapping.



The path statement is because vutil32 is not loaded in memory and
resides in S: (\\server\mexx)



Below is the batch file I created, so far so good;



net use S: \\server\mexx

S:

copy /y mkcfil.dat sqlfiles\mkc.dat

path=s:

cd sqlfiles



call vutil32 -udt mkc.dat mkc.txt



Thanks again to everyone for your help



Mufasa2000
```

every record.

<a id="t-30b6d95607"></a>
## Thread: Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing — 6 this month / 13 total

### Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-10-20T17:40:44-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F94484C.5BFFD357@istar.ca>`

```
As a z/OS (IBM mainframe) COBOL programmer, I am trying to get a handle
on the uses for more than 2 gigabytes of addressability for an
application.  Since both the i-series/AS400 and many Unix systems have
64 bit addressing, I was wondering if there were any applications on
these systems that take advantage of the large address range that have
COBOL main programs and/or subroutines.  If so, I would appreciate it if
the driver for the large addressing range could be discussed and how use
of such a range made the application development easier.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc.
```

#### ↳ Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-20T22:11:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84Zkb.3974$np1.1823@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3F94484C.5BFFD357@istar.ca>`

```
Clark,
    Just a "personal" guess on this.  For MANY places where COBOL already
supports 64-bit addressing, there is little if any difference between
procedural locations and dataset locations.   I know that this is the MAJOR
motive behind 64-bit COBOL in "Unix-like" environments.  The COBOL programs
are "required" to address "large data files" via addresses (and hence 31- or
32-bit addresses limit the size of DATA FILES in such environments).

As far as IBM's OS/400 / i-series, I believe (but won't swear to it) that
they have large "pointers" and addresses in order to support "cross-system"
(separate processors) access.

Neither of these are really "relevant" in a z/OS (or similar) IBM mainframe
environment.

As far as current or future z/OS (or z/VM) "need" for 64-bits, my guess is:

 A) "RMODE 64" (if available for anyone) won't really be a need for COBOL
ever
 B) "AMODE 64" *might* be needed (or at least "useful") for applications
that
    - currently use and create temporary VSAM files (that only last for the
length of a single program - not even a jobstream)
   - programs that need to share data between COBOL and some other language
that DOES support it (e.g. C/C++)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@XXXX.optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T08:41:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xi6lb.6215$6j.414275@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<84Zkb.3974$np1.1823@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3F94484C.5BFFD357@istar.ca> <84Zkb.3974$np1.1823@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> Clark,
>     Just a "personal" guess on this.  For MANY places where COBOL already
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> that DOES support it (e.g. C/C++)

I would expect that the push for 64 bit addressing in the IBM mainframe 
world would come from the same places that the push for 31 bit 
addressing came from, i.e., the big CICS (and IMS?) regions which have a 
*lot* going on (anyone else remember Virtual Storage Constraint 
Relief?). Hard for me to imagine a batch job step requiring 64 bit 
addressing because the applications I'm familiar with scarcely exploit XA.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T18:48:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031021144837.09972.00000719@mb-m21.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Xi6lb.6215$6j.414275@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
Increased pressure on data space is also comming from the markup language
processes.
XML databases (stat) are very expanded versions of data with lots of overhead.
The comming arrangements will possibly involve more UTF-16 encoding as well,
instead of 8 bit data encoding. 

Outside of the app the SQL queries always put stress on memory when the indexes
can't do it all for a given inquiry. IBM has a way to push much of the VSAM
access method out to the coupler with RLS (record level sharing) for CICS and
DB2. But if the customer  does not buy into the fancy parts, the big files need
space in core for the inquiries.

Also the Big Blue boxes are supposedly able to sport as many as 9000 Linux
images simulataneously.  If those are at all busy with IP ports they must have
some space. 

On the code side all code generators tend to create very large programs. I
personally have been amazed at what I have seen, but have not seen anything
like gigabyte length load modules.  

A coming issue is the address requirements for the object oriented COBOL, and
its interoperation with mainframe Java. Java has a resolution process that
applies when classes are first referenced. The desktop technologies try to keep
resolved classes in memory. And to be competitive maybe COBOL and mainframe
Jave will have to do the same.

If the basic tendency to make things big and lists long as is traditional in
COBOL carries over to OO COBOL, then resolved classes with deep inheritance may
well stress memory availability. So one would anticipate that there could be a
lot to keep around in a full blown OO COBOL app. But that is at the OS, or
system service level, versus the application itself.

Any merge of  a few of these concerns, like code generator with OO COBOL, or OO
COBOL with heavy 16 bit XML parsing, and the like and  the 2-gig 31 bit address
might need some help.

Also, keep in mind that OO development tools allow for the serialization of
vast arrays of records.  This implies in-core access by means of a mere index
into data objects that could be huge.  The OO view of data is not (necessarily)
a record in a file view. It is more like the whole file at once (as one
alternative).

Batch OO COBOL programs are definite candidates for huge data memory
requirements.

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T15:33:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310211433.60c09755@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<Xi6lb.6215$6j.414275@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <20031021144837.09972.00000719@mb-m21.aol.com>`

```
rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote

> Also, keep in mind that OO development tools allow for the serialization of
> vast arrays of records.  This implies in-core access by means of a mere index
> into data objects that could be huge.  The OO view of data is not (necessarily)
> a record in a file view. It is more like the whole file at once (as one
> alternative).

It is this view of data that makes OO ideal for such things as
simulation, graphics, UIs, presentations and many other things.

But it also tends to make it rather poor for business needs.

Unlike many computer applications, business systems tend to have a
need to share data and coordinate the updating of it from many sources
concurrently.  For this the shared ISAM (with locking) mechanism, the
serialised CICS mechanism and the SQL transactions are ideal.

> Batch OO COBOL programs are definite candidates for huge data memory
> requirements.

Sucking all the data into the private memory of an OO program where it
becomes unshared and inaccessible to other users while it is updated
and regurgitated is a rather poor way of dealing with most business
data.

Of course if batch is how the business deals with its data then by all
means use OO and have it all in the private RAM space of the
application, each user probably gets their own private extract each
day that they load into Excell to be able to do their actual work
anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Applications using over 2 gigabytes of addressing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** mossjm@wideopenwest.com (QCBLSRC)
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T23:41:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3473e5fa.0310272341.439ced32@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<Xi6lb.6215$6j.414275@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <20031021144837.09972.00000719@mb-m21.aol.com> <217e491a.0310211433.60c09755@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0310211433.60c09755@posting.google.com>...
> rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> data.


Riplin,

I concur...OO sucks for business applications. And we wonder why COBOL
has lived so long?.  The intensive memory requirements to process
large OO applications is a performance drag than most systems aren't
equipped to handle.  However, I also agree that after-hours batch
processing would be a great place for OO.

With regards to AS/400 64-bit addressing, the AS/400 has the best I/O
subsystems in the industry.  Having 2 GB of address space would be
quite a big program indeed, however the AS/400's "single level store"
could handle it.

QCBLSRC
```

Which is a better first computer language to learn? Cobol or Fortran?
Are they still used professionally? Or are they only learning
languages?

Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** "Mark Westwood [EPCC]" <markw@epcc.ed.ac.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T09:18:29+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,rec.woodworking,rec.photo.digital,rec.photo.equipment.35mm,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bn2q4l$588$1@scotsman.ed.ac.uk>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi

For what it's worth - neither Fortran nor COBOL should be regarded as a 
good first computer language to learn.  That is, unless you have a need 
to learn Fortran or COBOL.

The first language to learn depends on your answer to this question: why 
do you want to learn a programming language ?  For a lot of answers a 
good first language is Java, for others it's LISP/Scheme, for many it's C,

Mark

English Teacher wrote:
> Which is a better first computer language to learn? Cobol or Fortran?
> Are they still used professionally? Or are they only learning
> languages?
> 
> Thanks!
```

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>

```
Hi English Teacher.

I'll give you the benefit of the doubt about your question "Are they still
used professionally? Or are they only learning languages?"

I can't answer for Fortran, but COBOL, according to various estimates, makes
up 60% - 70% - 80% of the worlds computer code. It's not a "leaning
language" - it was specifically designed to process business rules and
business logic. True, it's been around for over 40 years but has not stood
still. It is still here after many "replacements" have come forward and
since fallen by the wayside.

Check out www.microfocus.com for an idea of where it's at with COBOL at the
moment.

Paul

"English Teacher" <EnglishTeacher@ModernEnglish.com> wrote in message
news:b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com...
> Which is a better first computer language to learn? Cobol or Fortran?
> Are they still used professionally? Or are they only learning
> languages?
>
> Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T07:32:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,rec.woodworking,rec.photo.digital,rec.photo.equipment.35mm,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E7ycnbRlo8L_ugiiU-KYkA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>`

```
English Teacher wrote:
> Which is a better first computer language to learn? Cobol or Fortran?
> Are they still used professionally? Or are they only learning
> languages?

A first language is not taught to learn the language. It is taught as a tool
to help learn programming concepts. As such, the first language should, at a
minimum, not get in the way of learning these concepts.

Let me give you an example: Plane Geometry. Geometry is taught in every
high-school in the land, but the demand for surveyors is extremely limited.
Nobody teaches Geometry for its own sake. Geometry is a TOOL to teach
logical thinking and deduction.

Now, to answer your question: Between the two, Fortran is superior to COBOL
for the task of learning basic programming skills (because it's easier). You
may never use Fortan in the real world, but how often do you have to
construct the bisector of an angle?
```

#### ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T08:58:45-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,rec.woodworking,rec.photo.digital,rec.photo.equipment.35mm,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-CCB643.08584521102003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>,
 EnglishTeacher@ModernEnglish.com (English Teacher) wrote:

> Which is a better first computer language to learn? Cobol or Fortran?
> Are they still used professionally? Or are they only learning
> languages?
> 
> Thanks!

They both suck as first languages, the are learning languages only in 
the "Oh, #$%^, what happened" school of bad experiences.  Both are still 
used very widely throughout the world.

Your goal should be to learn to program, not learn a language.  As such, 
there are several that are well designed with that goal in mind.

Pascal or Java are great fits for this problem.  Pascal is a bit better 
at teaching the purist theory, Java is a bit better at teaching the 
practical with its rich support library.  Either (or both) would be a 
fine first language.

Cobol, Fortran, Basic and C/C++ are terrible fits.  Avoid them until you 
have enough experience at programming to understand their shortcuts and 
shortcomings.

I also strongly recommend learning at least one assembler.  You don't 
have to become a guru, or even use it, but it will greatly improve your 
high level programming to understand what is going on under the covers.
```

#### ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T14:54:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,rec.photo.digital,rec.photo.equipment.35mm,rec.woodworking

- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>`

```

On 21-Oct-2003, EnglishTeacher@ModernEnglish.com (English Teacher) wrote:

> Which is a better first computer language to learn? Cobol or Fortran?
> Are they still used professionally? Or are they only learning
> languages?

I've never considered Cobol a learning language, and wouldn't think it is well
suited for that task.   In fact it's biggest threat is that it isn't taught in
schools enough to meet the professional demands.   It's a working language that
until recently was easily the most widely programmed professional language.  
It's used for business applications.
```

#### ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** busbus@thesafety.net (Ray Kinzler)
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T10:30:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,rec.woodworking,rec.photo.digital,rec.photo.equipment.35mm,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1ff7772.0310210930.48d688da@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>`

```
My thoughts are : What will you be doing with this knowledge
afterwards?  COBOL and FORTRAN are both linear programming languages. 
Java is object-oriented.  They really differ from each other in the
way one has to think.

My thoughts are to simply learn using macros in Excel--which is
nothing more than Visual Basic.  It's an easy language to learn and
you can get 'ideas' on how to do things by recording macros and within
many Excel books and even the manual you get with it.

#### ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** "Steven M. Scharf" <scharf.steven@linkearth.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-22T05:01:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,rec.woodworking,rec.photo.digital,rec.photo.equipment.35mm,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<maolb.785$wc3.223@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>`

```
"English Teacher" <EnglishTeacher@ModernEnglish.com> wrote in message
news:b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com...
>Which is a better first computer language to learn? Cobol or Fortran?
>Are they still used professionally? Or are they only learning
>languages?

APL is probably the best, Cobol and Fortran are obsolete.

You'll need to get an APL keyboard, but these are readily available
at "http://shop.store.yahoo.com/pckeyboards/adcolkey.html"
This is better than putting little stickers on your keys.

You can get a free version of APL at:
"ftp://watserv1.uwaterloo.ca/languages/apl/apl-plus/freeapl.zip"

ALGOL might also be a good choice for you, but there are only
MS-DOS versions, not Windows versions, see:
"http://www.angelfire.com/biz/rhaminisys/binaries/algol60.zip"
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-22T12:00:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,rec.woodworking,rec.photo.digital,rec.photo.equipment.35mm,comp.lang.cobol

```
"Steven M. Scharf" <scharf.steven@linkearth.net> wrote:
>
> APL is probably the best, ...

Please, please tell me you are joking! It is a tossup whether APL or RPG
are best qualified for the title Worst Programming Language Created By
Man. Experienced APL programmers often rewrite their own programs,
written only weeks ago, rather than try to modify them. APL was designed
by Kenneth E. Iverson, who clearly believed brevity is the *only* criteria
for a programming language. "Readable APL" is an oxymoron.
```

#### ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-22T12:29:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,rec.woodworking,rec.photo.digital,rec.photo.equipment.35mm,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031022133040.785$ZQ@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>`

```
"English Teacher" <EnglishTeacher@ModernEnglish.com> wrote:
> Which is a better first computer language to learn? Cobol or Fortran?
> Are they still used professionally? Or are they only learning
> languages?

Back in the 80's I was contracted by a major computer manufacturer to
teach a course on hardware, assembly language and system software
concepts to a group of their clients. The 25 or so students ranged from
a couple of clerks, obviously sent by a clueless management, to one very
intelligent, well educated guy with 5 years FORTRAN programming
experience with Du Pont. Most were common COBOL programmers,
in no way outstanding. The poor clerks were lost from the beginning, of
course. The COBOL programmers, without exception, grasped all the
necessary concepts with little difficulty. It is intuitively easy, for
example, to make the leap from the layout of a record in WS to memory
allocation, or from subscripts to pointers. But, except for the clerks, the
most lost person in the class was the most intelligent and educated of the
bunch, the guy with 5 years programming exclusively in FORTRAN. I
spent more time with him than any of the others, but he simply never
caught on. This guy was very bright, no dummy. But his mental model of
how a computer works was so distorted by the FORTRAN model that he
was unable to bridge the gap during the two weeks of the class.  Since in
FORTRAN variable 'appear magically', he has no concept of memory
allocation. Because in FORTRAN you can write a complex expression
and it is automatically calculated, he had no concept of how expressions
must be decomposed and executed piece by piece, as it must be. This is
only two examples, but the FORTRAN model is very, very different from
what is actually going on inside, at the hardware/assembly level. Draw
your own conclusions, but IMHO any process that makes it *harder* to
understand what is really going on in there is not good.
```

> understand what is really going on in there is not good.

Fascinating point of view - one that I would argue just the other way 
around: Fortran's mental model of computation is just that - 
computation.  All irrelevant complications and minutae are hidden by the 
"processor", i.e., compiler and run-time library.

complicated ...

#### ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** cyarno57@yahoo.com (cyarno57)
- **Date:** 2003-10-22T20:35:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,rec.woodworking,rec.photo.digital,rec.photo.equipment.35mm,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71e035dd.0310221935.65b80ef1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>`

```
EnglishTeacher@ModernEnglish.com (English Teacher) wrote in message news:<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com>...
> Which is a better first computer language to learn? Cobol or Fortran?
> Are they still used professionally? Or are they only learning
> languages?
> 
> Thanks!

There is far more work for COBOL programmers than there is for
Fortran. Go to Monster.com and check out the number of jobs. COBOL is
the language for business applications, it's used world wide. Fotran
will not get you a job unless your very mathematically oriented and
like  that sort of coding. For just learning the rudiments of
programming I'd recommend either Visual Basic or plain old Basic.
That's how I started 20+ years ago. C, C++, Java involve concepts that
can be very challenging for a beginner to learn. Basic would still be
my choice, then move on to another language. Another good one is AWK
which is unix type utility. Skip the pattern matching and just learn
the basic language, you'll be able to leverage that learn either C,
C++ or Java. You can download a free windows version of Awk from the
net.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T10:18:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031023111904.259$IB@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <71e035dd.0310221935.65b80ef1@posting.google.com>`

```
While not my first choice, one advantage of using BASIC as a first programming
language is that you can do it for free (assuming you already have a PC). Every
Windows version from 3.1 through 95, 98, ME and NT (but not 2K or XP) came
with QBASIC on the CD. AFAIK, all Windows versions, certainly the newer ones,
come with Windows Script Host, which will run VB Script & Java Script programs.
You can download the latest version of Windows Scripting Host & documentation
from Microsoft. Another thing about a text based BASIC for a beginner is that it is
very easy to learn. C/C++ or Java can be very intimidating. VB can be too, once you
move beyond trivial examples.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

- **From:** Richard Maine <nospam@see.signature>

isn't free.  It certainly didn't come for free on any PC I have.  It
didn't come at all, free or not, on several of them.  I think you mean
"assuming you already have Windows", which isn't the same thing as
assuming you already have a PC.

You can get Fortran compilers for free for any PC, regardless of operating
system.  (And the same goes for some other languages.)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Maine <nospam@see.signature>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T08:42:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <71e035dd.0310221935.65b80ef1@posting.google.com> <20031023111904.259$IB@news.newsreader.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> writes:

> But my point was that, since 90%+ PCs run Windows (I'm not saying that
> is a 'good thing' :), most people already have a BASIC on their PC.

Yes, I was mostly picking on the distinction between "assuming you
have a PC" and "assuming you have Windows."  Sure, there is 90%+
overlap, but why not say Windows if that's what you mean, instead of
saying PC and then explaining that you really mean Windows?  Isn't it
is just as easy to say what is meant in the first place?  Being a
programmer (of any language), precision is important to me; I know
that the computer is likely to do exactly what I tell it instead of
the sort of similar thing that I really meant.

Also note that the previous caveat about Windows XP and 2000 is a
pretty big one, since those are the only versions of Windows you
have been able to get for some time.  It rather majorly cuts down
the 90%+ if you exclude them.  If there is a basic on my XP systems,
I wouldn't know where to find it; nor I'm sure would most other
XP users.  If I went digging hard enough (particularly assisted
by google searches to give me hints about where to look), I might
be able to find where it might be buried, but...

Once "already..on their PC" changes into "buried somewhere obscure on
the OS CD" or "downloadable from somewhere on the net", then the
statement applies to Fortran (and other languages) as well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

Richard Maine  <nospam@see.signature> wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> writes:
>

Being a programmer you might have noticed that the statements made here 
might well have been intended to be interpreted by human beings, not by 
computers.

When asked to go to the kitchen and bring back a cup of water... do you 
come back empty-handed when there are no cups and only glasses?  

When someone says 'I dropped a glass on the table and it broke' do you
respond 'Well, how in the world did you find a table so flimsy that
dropping a glass on it would break it?' 

When you read 'Filet mignons are considered by some folks to be the finest
of meats, sausages the wurst' do you smile?

I would say that one shouldn't confuse one's vocation (or avocation) with 
one's entirety as a human being... but what do I know, I'se jes' a 
COBOL-codin' fool.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Maine <nospam@see.signature>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T12:43:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com writes:

> In article <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov>,
> Richard Maine  <nospam@see.signature> wrote:

> >Yes, I was mostly picking on the distinction between "assuming you
> >have a PC" and "assuming you have Windows."

> Being a programmer you might have noticed that the statements made here 
> might well have been intended to be interpreted by human beings, not by 
> computers.

Yep.  And also being a human being, I interpret the words PC and
Windows quite differently.  Perhaps you think that every human being
shares your particular interpretation of every word in the language,
but I've noticed somewhat more variety than that in my experience.

I'm using a PC right now.  I'm not using Windows.  Being a human being
that often is often involved with helping other human beings debug
programs, I notice that problems are *OFTEN* a direct result of human
beings misusing terms and directly causing confusion.  As a result,
I keep in mind a list of terms known to have caused confusion in
the past.  Whenever one of those terms is used in a user's problem
description, I pay particular attention to clarifying whether the
user really means what he said or not; it is not safe to assume
either way.

Examples abound, such as saying unformatted I/O when what is meant is
actually list-directed (which is a form of formatted I/O).

I have also more that once seen time wasted in going down blind alleys
specifically because of people saying PC when they meant Windows, so
that one is also on the list.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

Had you learned certain things from that experience you might not have

human beings have and still do interpret those combinations of characters
or the sounds associated with them as equally nonsensical so your

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Maine <nospam@see.signature>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T15:51:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ue7k2utfk8.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> writes:

> Richard Maine wrote:
> >
> > I'm using a PC right now.  I'm not using Windows.
> 
> But it's fair to equate PC and Windows.

"Fair" doesn't seem particularly relevant compared to "wrong".  I
don't care whether or not it is fair, or proper, or anything else,
that many programmers have PCs that don't have Windows on those PCs.
In fact, many of them don't.  The fraction is substantially higher
among programmers (the relevant audience for this discussion) than the
general populace.  The fraction is quite high among the programmers I
work with.

But regardless of what the fraction is...and recalling that I am
implying no judgement here at all about what is best for people to
use...

I fail to understand the resistance to actually saying what is
meant.  Are you trying to argue that there is some particular
reason why people should not refer to Windows as Windows?  Is
something to be gained by avoiding such simple clarity?  It isn't
like it is any harder to say "Windows" than "PC".  Is there
some reason why "PC" is more politically correct?  (Oops, wrong
"PC").

Something is certainly lost by confusing the terms PC and Windows.
I've seen it lost.  I've seen time wasted, wrong software obtained,
wrong people sent to fix a problem.  I am not speculating; I have
personally seen all these things happen.  Just telling the actual
truth instead of what someone thinks ought to be a "fair" equivalence
would have helped a lot.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Toon Moene <toon@moene.indiv.nluug.nl>
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T12:09:34+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F9A4BDE.7030208@moene.indiv.nluug.nl>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <ue7k2utfk8.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov>`

```
Richard Maine wrote:

 > It isn't
> like it is any harder to say "Windows" than "PC".  Is there
> some reason why "PC" is more politically correct?  (Oops, wrong
> "PC").

The funny thing is that, at my Institute, I see the move the other way 
around: The clerks have the PC's and the researchers have the workstations.

Of course, both are PC's (hardware wise) but no sane researcher wants to 
be associated with that software product from the Pacific Northwest :-)

[ The "workstations" run Linux ]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T19:19:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote

> > I'm using a PC right now.  I'm not using Windows.
> 
> But it's fair to equate PC and Windows.

The first machine called a 'PC' was the Apple ][.  This did not run
'Windows'. When you use the term 'PC' you are probably referring to
the 'IBM PC' or a clone or derivitive thereof rather than the other
PCs that have existed.  These IBM PCs existed for many years before
there even was a 'Windows', and even then it was another decade before
it became pervasive.

It may well be an equation that you _want_ to make, or one that you
make from ignorance of everything else ('ignorance' deriving from
'ignore'), but it is not as general as you like to think.
 
> If I see a person in a skirt with a come-hither smile, I assume "female."

Do you then claim that all females wear skirts and have a come-hither
smile ?

Not all females wear a skirt, certainly not all females have a smile. 
Not all PCs run Windows.

> Your experience is obviously different.

Why do you think it possible to extrapolate from your rather limited
view of 'PCs' and 'females' onto others experiences ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T13:56:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310251256.6540a0b9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <bndbkc$ifi$00$1@news.t-online.com> <bndpjc$tgk$04$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
"Michael Metcalf" <metcalfm@acm.org> wrote

> > You sure? I saw a PERQ in 1981. Wasn't that first? The IBM followed months
> > later.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> computer.

The Three Rivers PERQ was a 'Workstation'.  With an A4 graphics
screen, a 14inch hard disk, a graphics tablet and puck, it was rather
expensive for a 'personal' anything.  I was at ICL in Bracknell when
they took a licencing agreement to resell them in 1981. I was able to
play with them and I still have some manuals for them here.

At the time I was working with MF Cobol for the ICL DRS 20 networked
multi-processor micros, but there was also the 'ICL Personal Computer'
- a rebadged Rair Black Box 8085 with CP/M or MP/M II (I still have
bits of a couple of those too).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Being PC

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T00:58:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310280058.497c3558@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <aMnm/oHpvS0T092yn@visi.com> <bnjsm3$kmq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0310271431.fa8cfff@posting.google.com> <slrnbpram8.drj.mbkennelSPAMBEGONE@lyapunov.ucsd.edu>`

```
Dr Chaos <mbkennelSPAMBEGONE@NOSPAMyahoo.com> wrote

> This was seen as the category, but the manufacturers wanted
> to emphasize their own brand name. 
> 
> IBM turned it around by making the "PC" part of its intentionally
> marketed name.

Actually IBM used the full wording of their trade mark 'IBM Personal
Computer' for quite some time.  For example I have an add here in the
Oct 82 Byte that has the words 'Personal Computer' 9 times and 'PC'
none.

A year later and the terminology is: exactly the same.

Another year on: there is a change 6 times 'Personal Computer', no
'PC'.

Certainly almost _everyone_else_ abbreviated it to 'PC' but IBM
carefully used the full 'Personal Computer' in their marketing for a
long time.

Of course if you have _actual_ evidence that IBM made 'PC' part of its
intentionally marketed name in preference to its trade marked
'Personal Computer' I would be interested in seeing it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Being PC

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Dr Chaos <mbkennelSPAMBEGONE@NOSPAMyahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T18:20:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran
- **Message-ID:** `<slrnbptcru.hnh.mbkennelSPAMBEGONE@lyapunov.ucsd.edu>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <aMnm/oHpvS0T092yn@visi.com> <bnjsm3$kmq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0310271431.fa8cfff@posting.google.com> <slrnbpram8.drj.mbkennelSPAMBEGONE@lyapunov.ucsd.edu> <217e491a.0310280058.497c3558@posting.google.com>`

```
On 28 Oct 2003 00:58:57 -0800, Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> Dr Chaos <mbkennelSPAMBEGONE@NOSPAMyahoo.com> wrote
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> marketed name.

> Certainly almost _everyone_else_ abbreviated it to 'PC' but IBM
> carefully used the full 'Personal Computer' in their marketing for a
> long time.

I didn't make that distinction.  However, the abbreviation was
obvious and equivalent to saying "IBM" as I. B. M.  and not
International Business Machines. 

Humans understand these are fully equivalent and use them so.  

> Of course if you have _actual_ evidence that IBM made 'PC' part of its
> intentionally marketed name in preference to its trade marked
> 'Personal Computer' I would be interested in seeing it.

The name of its operating system was commonly called PC-DOS which was
well known to be abbreviated form of Personal Computer-Disk Operating
System, but people don't like the long form.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Being PC

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** sbdpumbaa@aol.com (bendel boy)
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T08:53:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran
- **Message-ID:** `<4db10a80.0310290853.3ff3afed@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <aMnm/oHpvS0T092yn@visi.com> <bnjsm3$kmq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0310271431.fa8cfff@posting.google.com> <bnm7ga$eb2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
The first UK nagazine for the sector was Personal Computer World,
abbreviated to PCW. That was in the late 1970s.

I remember them being called home computers more so than personal
computers. By 1986 the IBM was being called the IBM PC - which is when
I first encountered one. We had Amstrad machines called CPC by the
mid-1980s. But most machines were called by their names: BBC,
Sinclair, etc. If needed they would then have been called a 'computer'
rather than a 'personal computer' or 'PC.'

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:<bnm7ga$eb2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On 27-Oct-2003, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> But I don't remember them called "PCs".   How common was this?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T18:58:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310261858.59706a0b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <bndbkc$ifi$00$1@news.t-online.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C6718B.12144126102003@corp.supernews.com> <aPVmb.15571$Ec1.1415378@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"James Giles" <jamesgiles@worldnet.att.net> wrote

> > The Apple line dates back to 1977, with its mostly similar but caseless
> > precursor, the Apple I, being released in 1976.
> 
> I thought the current Apple line dated back to the LISA (1983?).

The 'current' Apple line is based on Power PC and Mach/NeXT/BSD Unix. 
The previous line was Motorola 68k and MacOS, the line before that was
Lisa.  Before that (and with much overlap) it was the 6502 based Apple
][ in various guises and the Apple III.

> With respect to the context of this discussion, Apples before
> that were IBM-PC style text-based boxes that came bundled with
> Basic.

If you mean by 'IBM-PC style' that it had a system box and separate
keyboard, then no.  The Apple ][ was (mostly) a one piece system with
the keyboard on the front of the system box.  But it did have a
separate monitor (or TV). Apart from that it was not at all like an
IBM PC - different processor, different OS.  The IBM PC did copy some
aspects of Apple ][ - plug in cards, BASIC in ROM, cassette tape
interface (on PC and PC Jr).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T22:32:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310262232.69ad3b6b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <bndbkc$ifi$00$1@news.t-online.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C6718B.12144126102003@corp.supernews.com> <aPVmb.15571$Ec1.1415378@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0310261858.59706a0b@posting.google.com> <Iu0nb.16173$Ec1.1459603@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"James Giles" <jamesgiles@worldnet.att.net> wrote 

> >> With respect to the context of this discussion, Apples before
> >> that were IBM-PC style text-based boxes that came bundled with
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> had Basic bundled in it.  

Both the Apple ][ and the IBM PC were capable of graphics, except if
it was an MDA card on the IBM.  But surely you have it 'backwards', it
is the IBM PC that was in 'Apple style'.  After all the Apple was
years before the IBM PC.

In fact that was deliberate.  Apples with Visicalc and with CP/M (on
the Microsoft Z80 card) were appearing in IBM sites.  The IBM PC was
put together to be a 'better Apple ]['.  It had the same BASIC
(AppleSoft BASIC was written by MS as was ROM BASIC), the same
software: Visicalc, dBase II, WordStar, the cassette interface and
slightly better disks: 160Kb instead of 100Kb.
```

In article 
<aPVmb.15571$Ec1.1415378@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
 "James Giles" <jamesgiles@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> > In article <bndbkc$ifi$00$1@news.t-online.com>,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Basic.

The current line, Macintosh, does.  But that took plenty of things from 
the ][ line and carried it forward, and some from the Mac line and 
carried it backwards.

The original ]['s had a text based GUI package available that sorta 
reminds me of IBM's CUA -- lots of specs, little API assistance (that I 
knew of).  Apple developed to it, but few others did.  'Mousemenus' or 
something like that.  Came out about the time of the built in 80-column 
support.  1981?  Interestingly, Apple was a big supporter of the 
Pascal-System -- an early byte-code virtual machine that would allow 
binary objects to be written once, run anywhere...hmmmm...

The last ][ had a color GUI with a call compatible API to the Macintosh 
-- the first color (pseudo)-Mac.  Eventually, add in boards existed for 
both lines to run the others software.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Gary L. Scott" <garyscott@ev1.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T20:03:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F9DCE8E.EC349355@ev1.net>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <bndbkc$ifi$00$1@news.t-online.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C6718B.12144126102003@corp.supernews.com> <aPVmb.15571$Ec1.1415378@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <joe_zitzelberger-0B1888.07133827102003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> 
> In article
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> binary objects to be written once, run anywhere...hmmmm...

Actually, there were quite extensive APIs developed relative to CUA,
especially mainframe and OS/2.  There was a potentially capable
windowing API written for the mainframe, but was hobbled mostly by the
attached hardware (displays/terminals) that most had.  If you were lucky
enough like me to have a $22000 display attachment (1024x1024 1:1 pixel
aspect ratio), you could substantially duplicate a windows GUI.  Of
course, since these were often attached over 9600 baud SNA links, you
had to take that into consideration in your API design which often meant
falling back to a text menuing system and a graphics drawing/display
subfield.


> 
> The last ][ had a color GUI with a call compatible API to the Macintosh
> -- the first color (pseudo)-Mac.  Eventually, add in boards existed for
> both lines to run the others software.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T05:31:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031025063229.540$A2@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
> The first machine called a 'PC' was the Apple ][.

Actually, the first machine to be called PC was the IBM PC. If you
were involved with microcomputers at the time, or if you examine the
magazines from the era, it is unarguably clear that what we now call
'PCs' were called 'microcomputers' until IBM announced the 'IBM
Personal Computer' in 1981.

Until that time, computers were very incompatible between brands.
Even when running the same OS, such as CP/M, the premiere OS at
the time for Intel CPUs, disk formats varied widely, and there was an
active market for disk format conversion programs. The IBM PC was
so successful that the industry was largely forced to become 'IBM PC
compatible', shortened to 'PC compatible', and finally to 'PC'. The term
PC and the IBM PC architecture became so ubiquitous that the meaning
of 'IBM PC compatible' became lost, and even computers using other
architectures, such as Apple's, became known as PCs. The fact that
95% of all PCs made today can boot a Windows 98 boot disk is
attributable to the original IBM PC architecture (with extensions) still
incorporated in almost every PC on the planet, except for those made
by Apple.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T12:08:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310251108.798d0e1d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <20031025063229.540$A2@news.newsreader.com>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote 

> > The first machine called a 'PC' was the Apple ][.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Personal Computer' in 1981.

No, that is not true, and the evidence is from exactly where you say. 
In 1980 Apple were advertising their machine as a 'Personal Computer'.

From the August 1981 Byte magazine, which is prior to the IBM being
announced:

"Lowest prices on Personal Computers" 
"Turn you Apple into the worlds's most versatile Personal Computer".
(MS Ad).
There was a magazine called 'Personal Computer Letter' 
"The Personal Computer - Apple" (Apple ad).

It was a common enough generic term for complete small computers -
rather than kits.

> even computers using other
> architectures, such as Apple's, became known as PCs. 

They _are_ 'Personal Computers' It is a generic term from before the
IBM 5150 and still is a generic term.  That is the nature of my
complaint, your use of the generic term as only applying to one
design.

> The fact that
> 95% of all PCs made today can boot a Windows 98 boot disk is

So here you _do_ use it as a generic term.

> attributable to the original IBM PC architecture (with extensions) still
> incorporated in almost every PC on the planet, except for those made
> by Apple.

Which has nothing to do with the quality of the design, or of Windows
software, but is entirely related to the cost of production reducing
as the volumes increase.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T15:53:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031025165434.562$zx@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <20031025063229.540$A2@news.newsreader.com> <217e491a.0310251108.798d0e1d@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote
> > >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> "The Personal Computer - Apple" (Apple ad).

This is merely confirmation of my statements, since the IBM PC was
released on August 12, 1981 (PC Magazine, Vol 1, Issue 1, page 42).
The heat was already on, when everybody realized Big Blue was going
to be in the market. In those days, Big Blue cast a Big Shadow, and
dominated the mainframe computer world like Microsoft dominates
the PC software world now. The term 'personal computer' may have
been used by a very few people in very limited ways, but it simply
was not a term in general use before the IBM PC.

> It was a common enough generic term for complete small computers -
> rather than kits.

Absolutely not true!  I don't know where you were in those years, but I
was following the industry avidly from the mid 1970's. I still have the
first several issues at least of most of the hobby computer magazines of
the 70's, including (premiere date) Creative Computing (Dec 1974),
Byte (Sep 1975), Dr. Dobbs (Jan 1976) and KiloBaud (Jan 1977). By
late 1977 I was writing software on several different microcomputers
for sale, and by 1979 I was part owner of a computer store selling the
Apple, Commodore, Amiga, Atari, etc., and carried the IBM PC from
day one. I've actually played with the Apple III, which most people have
never heard of (not to mention the Lisa :). Ours was the first microcomputer
store in Alabama, the first Apple dealer in Alabama, and the first IBM PC
dealer in Alabama. We received one of the first IBM PCs for a display
model. At the time, it was a big deal that IBM *did not* call their new
computer a 'microcomputer', but a 'Personal Computer'. They did this to
distinguish the IBM PC from the rest of the market, which was well
understood at the time. There were even jokes about it at the time, and
many people resisted the eventual change in nomenclature. For a long time
the term 'PC' was sufficient to make clear that you meant an IBM PC,
nothing else. When Compaq was released (we sold them too) it *was
not* referred to as a PC at the time, but 'a Compaq', or sometimes
generically as 'a PC clone'.

> > attributable to the original IBM PC architecture (with extensions) still
> > incorporated in almost every PC on the planet, except for those made
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> as the volumes increase.

Are you sure you read what I wrote? I made no such implication, in fact
I specifically said it was not necessarily a 'Good Thing' that so many of
today's PCs run Windows. To note a situation does not necessarily imply
that you approve of it. Personally, I use Windows because that is where
the vast client base is. Given the choice, I would rather use something
lean and mean, perhaps Linux.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T12:29:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7m0opvc1gec662r1ec6im93nfb5d1oru14@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <20031025063229.540$A2@news.newsreader.com> <217e491a.0310251108.798d0e1d@posting.google.com> <20031025165434.562$zx@news.newsreader.com>`

```
On Sat, 25 Oct 2003 15:53:36 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> enlightened us:

>"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote
…[68 more quoted lines elided]…
>lean and mean, perhaps Linux.

Maybe the term "personal computer" wasn't marketed until the 80's but
there is some truth that the 1971 Kenbak I by John Blankenbaker was
the truly the first "personal computer".  The Altair, considered my
many to the first PC, was introduced in 1975 by Ed Roberts & Bill
Yates, MITS.  In 1977 Personal Computing is published by David
Bunnell.  Apple computer was also founded in 1977.  Also, Commodore
began selling what they called personal computers in 1977.

In 1981, IBM begins selling the IBM PC which becomes the de facto
standard for PC.  This despite the fact that Commodore sold over 1
million VIC-20s that year and it was a much better and cheaper
computer than anything IBM or Microsoft would produce.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"There's nothing wrong with you that reincarnation
won't cure."
----- Jack E. Leonard
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T14:09:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310251309.7c8e1e89@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <20031025063229.540$A2@news.newsreader.com>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote 

> Until that time, computers were very incompatible between brands.
> Even when running the same OS, such as CP/M, the premiere OS at
> the time for Intel CPUs, disk formats varied widely, and there was an
> active market for disk format conversion programs. 

Actually there was just as active an industry for disk format
conversion programs between MS-DOS machines.  FBN's Alien may list 335
formats for CP/M (including some other systems such as Turbo-DOS), it
also has 85 listed MS-DOS diskette formats.

> The IBM PC was
> so successful that the industry was largely forced to become 'IBM PC
> compatible', 

Actually it wasn't the IBM PC itself, many machines were actually
better and could run all the well behaved MS-DOS software, usually
faster.

What forced IBM Compatability was Lotus-123.  While businesses bought
Apple ][s for Visicalc, they bought IBMs for Lotus.
```

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T23:24:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-4E9F2E.23244927102003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <20031025063229.540$A2@news.newsreader.com> <joe_zitzelberger-38BAC1.08283626102003@corp.supernews.com> <20031027144223.529$AU@news.newsreader.com>`

```
In article <20031027144223.529$AU@news.newsreader.com>,
 "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> "Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> this is the case, then the point I was defending above is even stronger. :-)

Actually, you cam bring up multiple virtual copies of any Intel OS in 
any Mac going back to about 1988.  There were also a bunch of hardware 
addins that provided an actual co-processor.

But in all cases (6 or 7 different brands of software and a few dozen 
hardware solutions) the Macintosh System managed the virtualization.  So 
you would not boot the machine with Windows98, but you would boot one or 
more copies of Windows98 in a window.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T14:50:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310271450.2099edb9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <bnjsj5$k9b$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> > Not all females wear a skirt, certainly not all females have a smile.
> > Not all PCs run Windows.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> claimed that his assumption included every possible female.

Either way it is flawed, but then I live in the South Pacific where
people dress in ways that may be frowned upon in 'redneck' areas of
the USA.

> I could come up with a lot of illustrative examples, but none which are even
> more obvious than his.

If one sees Windows then one might assume 'PC' (especially since they
dropped Alpha and MIPS support), but it seemed that he was attempting
to make the analogy in a way that claimed 'if one saw a PC one could
assume Windows' (which was what he originally said).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Gary L. Scott" <garyscott@ev1.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T20:14:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran
- **Message-ID:** `<3F9DD112.CB208D40@ev1.net>`
- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <bnbrje$6aj$1@panix1.panix.com> <uebrs6to9j.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <1JKdnRsgq9TxAwSiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0310241819.1d2c05e5@posting.google.com> <bnjsj5$k9b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0310271450.2099edb9@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> 
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the USA.

So called "redneck" areas of the US are generally less extreme than the
world at large appears to believe sometimes.  The exceptional characters
are very rare.  Also, the term is insulting and stereotypical.  I now
live in a large metro area and make a nice living as an engineer.  I'm
moderate to liberal politically.  I grew up in a so-called redneck and
rural area outside of a national forest (i.e. very sparse population)
and probably was somewhat of a redneck based upon my upbringing,
environment, social status, friends and aquaintences, involvement in
local farming economy, etc.  I don't believe that I was ever naive in
the above sense and I would not consider many of the people I knew as
such.

> 
> > I could come up with a lot of illustrative examples, but none which are even
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> assume Windows' (which was what he originally said).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FORTRAN or COBOL first ?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Jan C. Vorbrüggen <jvorbrueggen@mediasec.de>

- **References:** `<b06436f6.0310202231.61388d11@posting.google.com> <71e035dd.0310221935.65b80ef1@posting.google.com> <20031023111904.259$IB@news.newsreader.com> <m23cdjscgz.fsf@vega.dsl.att.net> <20031024111429.841$c3@news.newsreader.com> <uefzhitzeq.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov>`

Richard Maine <nospam@see.signature> wrote

> Once "already..on their PC" changes into "buried somewhere obscure on
> the OS CD" or "downloadable from somewhere on the net", then the
> statement applies to Fortran (and other languages) as well.

Not only that, but many systems that come with Windows installed (or
specially: did come installed with '98, or 'ME) did not have the
retail install CD but had the OS on hard drive and perhaps had a
'recover CD' built specifically by the manufacturer.

These may, or may not, have had the 'OLDMSDOS' stuff.
```

## Thread: Java/Cobol interoperability on z/OS — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Java/Cobol interoperability on z/OS

- **From:** dburrow@fds.com (David Burrow)
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T07:08:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e22ff8a7.0310210608.38d2aa81@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm currently trying to determine the "tricks and traps" of using Java
on USS and z/OS -- and in particular -- the interface between Java and
OO (or "Enterprize") Cobol.  I'm hoping that there are those that have
blazed the trail ahead of me, rather than my having to re-invent the
wheel myself.

Would anyone care to comment on experiances, best practices, or
resources related to:
1) Compiling/Debugging both languages on USS and z/OS
2) Current practices interfacing Java and legacy Cobol apps
3) Performance issues related to tuning the JVM
4) Anything your experiance says is important.... :)

TIA
```

                           OMNI GROUP
                           tgugger@msn.com
                           419-380-8853

              MicroFocus Cobol  / Contract  / NC              TM478

  Please read the job description below. If interested and qualified,
Answer the questions at the bottom of the page. Email answers WITH
A resume to tgugger@msn.co. All questions MUST be answered to
Be considered.

Length: 7 months+      Area: Raleigh, NC
Due Date: 10/26/03 5:00 P.M. EDT
Start Date: November, 2003

Description:
Individual will provide programmer/analyst maintenance and support for a
client-server Human Resources application. Will handle trouble tickets,
small projects and related work. Will have regular contact with internal HR
customer and other technical support personnel.

Experience in Micro-focus cobol in a client-server environment is required.
Oracle or other SQL database experience also required. Human Resources
and/or payroll application experience is a plus.

Email answers WITH a reflective resume to tgugger@msn.com.
1. Micro Focus Cobol--------yrs + last used---------
2. Oracle exp------------------yrs
3. SQL exp (non Oracle) -----------yrs
4. HR app exp----------------------yrs
5. Payroll exp---------------------yrs
6. Final and best rate (1099)------pr hr
1099=you pay your own expenses.

<a id="t-77e340308d"></a>
## Thread: AIX ASSIGN problems — 9 msgs

### AIX ASSIGN problems

- **From:** bspiegel00 <member12146@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T14:45:12-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3507082.1066761912@dbforums.com>`

```

Hello, friends.



We're trying to compile and run our first COBOL programs on AIX (we've
done it endlessly on MVS).  As you might imagine, we're running into
some problems, probably from cluelessness -- can't fix or prevent what
you don't know enough to look for.  We're hoping that someone out
there can help.



We're using the ASSIGN clause to tie FD statements to AIX environmental
variables in the program:



FILE-CONTROL.

           SELECT O-CLM0110-XTRCT-FILE        ASSIGN TO CLM0100.

           SELECT FINC-RECON-FILE             ASSIGN TO FINRECON.

           SELECT ERROR-REPORT-FILE           ASSIGN TO ERRRPT.

           SELECT ICN-INPUT-FILE              ASSIGN TO ICNINPT.



In the execution of the load module that results from a clean bldapp,
we've got these environmental variables set up:



export CREATOR=udbtest1

export DIR=/amirs/eds/dev/test/data

export EXE=/amirs/eds/dev/test/copy

export OUT=/amirs/eds/dev/test/out

export ICNINPT="< cat ${DIR}/amirxicn.txt"

export CLM0100=$DIR/clm0110_clm0110.txt

export FINRECON=$DIR/clm0110_finrecon.txt

export ERRRPT=$DIR/clm0110_errrpt.txt



We verify the environmental variables are accurately set up by executing
env just prior to executing the load module:



env > ${OUT}/clm0110.out



We then move to the executable directory:



cd ${EXE}



And then we execute the load module:



clm0110  >> ${OUT}/clm0110.out



If you're sharper than us -- and you must be -- you know we're failing.
Here are the gory details -- indicating a file access error for our
input file, except it's already got all permissions turned on:



IWZ200S  Error detected during OPEN for file 'ICN-INPUT-FILE'. File
status is:

         91. File system indicated: Not authorized to File.

         Message routine called from offset 0x38 of routine
         iwzWriteERRmsg.

         iwzWriteERRmsg called from offset 0x490 of routine
         _iwzCallUseProc.

         _iwzCallUseProc called from offset 0x10a8 of routine
         _iwzSeqOpen.

         _iwzSeqOpen called from offset 0x4cc of routine
         _iwzGenericOpen.

         _iwzGenericOpen called from offset 0x14c4 of routine CLM0110.

IWZ901S  Program exits due to severe or critical error.







Any idea what we're doing wrong?



Thanks for the help.



Barry Spiegel

EDS

Phoenix, AZ

barry.spiegel@eds.com
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX ASSIGN problems

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T21:45:57+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f6bpv4lu19sjhohsjmcgtvooel6l00aqi@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3507082.1066761912@dbforums.com>`

```
On Tue, 21 Oct 2003 14:45:12 -0400, bspiegel00
<member12146@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>Hello, friends.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>there can help.
It would help if you told us which COBOL brand and version you are
using.
From the error messages I presume it is AIX COBOL, but I am probably
wrong.
>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>           SELECT ICN-INPUT-FILE              ASSIGN TO ICNINPT.

Some of the COBOL's I have worked with will allow this only if the
assigned variable is within quotes. e.g. ASSIGN TO "CLM0100".

>
>In the execution of the load module that results from a clean bldapp,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>export ERRRPT=$DIR/clm0110_errrpt.txt

Your users guide/language reference manual should say if this is
possible.

ACUCOBOL does and I think that MICROFOCUS also allows this, but not
sure they allow it using environment variables.
>
>We verify the environmental variables are accurately set up by executing
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any idea what we're doing wrong?
No following the manual??

>
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>barry.spiegel@eds.com



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX ASSIGN problems

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T21:36:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XFhlb.273$I04.65@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3507082.1066761912@dbforums.com>`

```
Have you checked the "permission" values that you have for the various
files?

 "Not authorized to File"

messages often mean that you don't have the correct "read/write" values set.
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX ASSIGN problems

- **From:** bspiegel00 <member12146@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T18:20:41-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3507961.1066774841@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3507082.1066761912@dbforums.com>`

```

Frederico -- thanks for your reply.  The manual -- COBOL for AIX -- has
been used a lot, but seems to lack detail on these points.  We're using
the current version of IBM COBOL for AIX.



I took your advice and tried surrounding the ASSIGN environmental
variables in quotes.  As a result, for our input file, we're using
environmental variable ICNINPT and the environmental statements that are
relevant are as follows:



export DIR=/amirs/eds/dev/test/data

export ICNINPT="< cat ${DIR}/amirxicn.txt"



The ICNINPT file has 777 privileges.



After a clean execution of bldapp, my attempt to run the executable, via
our script as described in my earlier note, resulted in an error similar
to before:



IWZ200S  Error detected during OPEN for file 'ICN-INPUT-FILE'. File
status is: 35. File

         system indicated: File Not Found.

         Message routine called from offset 0x38 of routine
         iwzWriteERRmsg.

         iwzWriteERRmsg called from offset 0x490 of routine
         _iwzCallUseProc.

         _iwzCallUseProc called from offset 0x10a8 of routine
         _iwzSeqOpen.

         _iwzSeqOpen called from offset 0x4cc of routine
         _iwzGenericOpen.

         _iwzGenericOpen called from offset 0x14c4 of routine CLM0110.

IWZ901S  Program exits due to severe or critical error.



/clm0110bs.sh[14]: 117170 IOT/Abort trap(coredump)





Any clues would be appreciated.



Thanks, Barry
```

- **References:** `<3507082.1066761912@dbforums.com> <3507961.1066774841@dbforums.com>`

On Tue, 21 Oct 2003 18:20:41 -0400, bspiegel00
<member12146@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>Frederico -- thanks for your reply.  The manual -- COBOL for AIX -- has
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>The ICNINPT file has 777 privileges.
For what I could see on the manual I'm not sure if you can use dynamic
variables like that.

But if you can I think it will be like
           SELECT ICN-INPUT-FILE              ASSIGN  USING ICNINPT.

ws
01 ICNINPT PIC X(999) value "<cat ...".

Alternatively you may with to change your "<cat aaa" to "cat aaa"
only.


Bu I really don't think it will work..

All the other assigns are ok as you had them before, and according to
the manual they should work.




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3508022.1066775101@dbforums.com>`
