```

To be explicit, the COBOL for AIX compiler is version 1.1.1.8; the other
filesets follow accordingly.



Barry
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX ASSIGN problems

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T23:40:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310212240.6e3188bb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3507082.1066761912@dbforums.com>`

```
bspiegel00 <member12146@dbforums.com> wrote

> FILE-CONTROL.
>            SELECT O-CLM0110-XTRCT-FILE        ASSIGN TO CLM0100.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> export OUT=/amirs/eds/dev/test/out

> export ICNINPT="< cat ${DIR}/amirxicn.txt"

What is this trying to achieve ?  Is the file name held in the
'amirxicn.txt' file ?

What you probaly mean is:

export ICNINPT=`cat ${DIR}/amirxicn.txt`

Note those are 'back apostrophes'  - [`] not ['].  

> export CLM0100=$DIR/clm0110_clm0110.txt
> export FINRECON=$DIR/clm0110_finrecon.txt
> export ERRRPT=$DIR/clm0110_errrpt.txt

> IWZ200S  Error detected during OPEN for file 'ICN-INPUT-FILE'. File
> status is:
> 
>          91. File system indicated: Not authorized to File.

You tried to open the file named "< cat ..." and not the file that is
named in the amirxicn.txt file.
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX ASSIGN problems

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T23:49:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310212249.233aa68d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3507082.1066761912@dbforums.com>`

```
bspiegel00 <member12146@dbforums.com> wrote
> We're using the ASSIGN clause to tie FD statements to AIX environmental
> variables in the program:
> 
> FILE-CONTROL.
>            SELECT O-CLM0110-XTRCT-FILE        ASSIGN TO CLM0100.

You probaly also need the word 'external' after 'to' to get it to use
the environment variables.  As it is it probably want to have the name
of the file in the implicitly defined working-storage variable
CLM0100.

ie it expects:

       MOVE "dirctory/nameoffile"    TO CLM0100
       OPEN OUTPUT O-CLM0110-Xtract-File

To take the name from an _external_ variable may need it to be
specified as such.
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX ASSIGN problems

- **From:** rkeane@acm.org (rkeane)
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T18:40:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e261ebcd.0310301840.4bda384e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3507082.1066761912@dbforums.com>`

```
bspiegel00 <member12146@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:<3507082.1066761912@dbforums.com>...
> Hello, friends.
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I think I see what you were trying to do with the "< cat {...etc", but
it's not needed, and as you now know it doesn't work. A simple export,
the same as you have done with the other variables is enough, eg.

export ICNINPT=$DIR/amirxicn.txt

will link the assign to name in the cobol pgm to the input file.
hope this helps.

>            SELECT O-CLM0110-XTRCT-FILE        ASSIGN TO CLM0100.
> 
…[103 more quoted lines elided]…
> barry.spiegel@eds.com
```

## Thread: Screen Section — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Screen Section

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <fjohnso5@rochester.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T20:59:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x6hlb.12195$%a2.3923@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Hello, again - though I used to post questions quite regularly, it has 
been a while since I have done so.  Having said that, a question has 
come up that I do not know the answer to.

I have a student who would like to use a different foreground color in a 
COBOL Screen depending on whether the value is positive or negative.  It 
is not clear to me (from our documentation) whether the foreground and 
background colors can be set for individual fields in the screen section 
or whether the foreground color can be set via a variable.

I do not have access to his code - but can probably get it if my 
question is not clear or needs more detail.  Thank you for any advice 
you can give.

Floyd Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Section

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T17:32:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3507871.1066771937@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<x6hlb.12195$%a2.3923@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```

you have  two  options



1 - Create  two Screens

     screen-blue

           bla bla bla bla



     screen-Red

         bla  bla bla



in procedure

     if   number < zeros

          display  Screen_red

     else

          display  Screen-Blue

      end-if





2 - get all accep  in procedure  division  instead  Screen Section.



       accept  ws-field  at  1212  with  foregroun color 14

        if  ws-field  < 0

             display  ws-filed  at 1212  with foreground color 12





Carlos Lages
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Section

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-21T22:18:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ugilb.290$I04.131@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<x6hlb.12195$%a2.3923@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Which COBOL compiler is the student using?  There are several "flavors" of
screen section, for example, AcuCOBOL provides a fully GUI-ized screen
section.  Micro Focus would probably allow you to do this via the CONTROL
phrase.  A purely "X/Open conforming" Screen Section might well NOT support
this easily.
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Section

- **From:** "Charles W. Cribbs II" <train318@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-22T10:25:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yWslb.12796$Uz6.3167@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<x6hlb.12195$%a2.3923@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Yes you can vary the forground colors.

As one responder said, the microfocus compiler allows this through the use
of control statements.  Additionally the microfocus compiler has a screen
painter that makes developing the screen section much faster and easier than
having to figure out the position of each field on the screen.


"Floyd Johnson" <fjohnso5@rochester.rr.com> wrote in message
news:x6hlb.12195$%a2.3923@twister.nyroc.rr.com...
> Hello, again - though I used to post questions quite regularly, it has
> been a while since I have done so.  Having said that, a question has
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Section

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@microfocus.uk.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-22T16:08:54+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bn66dn$kds$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<x6hlb.12195$%a2.3923@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
A couple of replies have indicated that this is possible on Micro Focus
products via the CONTROL clause. This is quite true, but you can also
specify FOREGROUND/BACKGROUND-COLOR <dataname>, which is a lot simpler than
having to specify CONTROL statements.
```

<a id="t-857f416d79"></a>
## Thread: "Bunches" of COBOL & LE SHARE requirements "Open for Discussion" — 1 msgs

### "Bunches" of COBOL & LE SHARE requirements "Open for Discussion"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-22T05:26:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1,ibm.software.cobol,ibm.software.pli
- **Message-ID:** `<Txolb.650$I04.320@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
I still have 7 or 8 more "ISO COBOL" requirements to created, but the
following requirements (COBOL and LE - so far none for PL/I) are available
in the "Open for discussion" page at:

    http://www.share.org/REQS/reqmain.nsf/Req+by+Status?OpenView&CollapseAll

I have updated a couple of the older requirements and should finish this
initial stage in the next couple of days. So far, there have been LIMITED
user discussions on what is out there. I assume (always dangerous) that this
means that what is there is clear enough - and we are (almost) ready to
vote. I hope to have ALL of the requirements available for voting before Nov
1. Then we can finish voting by the middle of December and "submit" the
priorities to IBM (hopefully for at least initial responses in Long Beach).

Requirement numbers and subjects:
    REQS_APLS_LNGC
        SSLNGC03001 Provide COBOL compiler options in CEEnDUMP
        SSLNGC03002 Allow underscore in COBOL User-Defined Words
        SSLNGC03003 Compiler option to make BLOCK CONTAINS clause SMS
compliant
        SSLNGC03004 COBOL compiler option to generate CEEUOPT run-time
options
        SSLNGC0313578 Implement the new 2002 COBOL standard in a phased
manner
        SSLNGC0313579 IBM support effort to provide Native COBOL XML support
        SSLNGC0313580 Port CEE5DLY functionality to LE for z/OS and for z/VM
        SSLNGC0313582 Assembler DSECTS to COBOL utility
        SSLNGC0313583 Increase Individual Item and Table Compiler Limits
        SSLNGC0313585 2002 ISO COBOL - EXIT and PERFORM Enhancements
        SSLNGC0313586 Enhance CEE3PRM Callable Service
        SSLNGC0313587 New LE Callable Service to get (various) Program Names
        SSLNGC0313588 2002 ISO COBOL - 1-byte and Other "True" Binary
Enhancements
        SSLNGC0313589 2002 ISO COBOL - CALL prototypes, Compile-time Arg
Checking
        SSLNGC0313590 2002 ISO COBOL - COPY & REPLACE Partial Word
Replacement
        SSLNGC0313591 2002 ISO COBOL - Compiler Directives

***
Again, if you have a SHARE userid and can't see or comment on these, please
try checking your "user profile" from:


http://www.share.org/share/website/shareweb.nsf/1d99cd65f2badf1e86256b78006ccb15/d84e1cb707541ed686256bf90066b343?OpenDocument
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-10-22T08:02:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1066809697@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: RM/Cobol85 — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### RM/Cobol85

- **From:** krisda@childrensfactory.com (Kris Dames)
- **Date:** 2003-10-22T14:10:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<499a63bb.0310221310.22f1d060@posting.google.com>`

```
Greetings.

I have been using Cipher System Ltd.'s Companion software for RM/Cobol
for some years now. I recently upgraded to RM/Cobol85 version 7.5, and
redhat linux 8.0. Now I cannot run reports on any new file views that
I install without Companion crashing. Are there any RM/Companion users
lurking about that are familiar with the file view installation
procedure? Or, does anybody know of a better report generator for
RM/Cobol85 that has a text-mode (non-gui) interface?  Any leads here
would be greatly appreciated. I can show the error I am getting if
anybody is interested; it looks as though the cmpn-obj.lib file is the
culprit.

Thanks,

Kris Dames
```

#### ↳ Re: RM/Cobol85

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-22T22:28:25+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lusdpvchqi4pak3egpck6qgendjvcc2mgj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<499a63bb.0310221310.22f1d060@posting.google.com>`

```
On 22 Oct 2003 14:10:37 -0700, krisda@childrensfactory.com (Kris
Dames) wrote:

>Greetings.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>culprit.
Thats weird.
The objects should work properly.
What version of Companion are you using?

Just out of curiosity can you do a rmmappgm on that .lib file and see
the results?
(runcobol rmmappgm a='cmpn-obj.lib')

And also try the following.
create a backup of cmpn-obj.lib (mv cmpn-obj.lib cmpn-obj.lib.bak)
runcobol rmpgmcom a='cmpn-obj.lib,cmpn-obj.lib.bak'

An try and run the programs again.


And yes I am interested on the error your're getting (213 or 214
probably)



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

errors.

Be sure to setup all the variables (path, runpath and whatever else is
done within the script that has the "runcobol xxx -l cmpn-obj.lib"

runcobol pgm -l cmpn-obj.lib
without ANY redirection ">" at the end.

Also please post the full command line used.

And PLEASE post the version of Companion you are using.
Is it the version from Liant or from Cipher?

display).

#### ↳ Re: RM/Cobol85

- **From:** t.morrison@liant.com (Tom Morrison)
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T07:32:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2d819982.0310270732.7f7b5225@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<499a63bb.0310221310.22f1d060@posting.google.com>`

```
Kris,

I have read the various posts to date after this was called to my
attention by another newsgroup reader (I have not been reading this
group during a short vacation).

1.  Did you purchase Companion from Liant sales, or directly from
Cipher?

2.  If you purchased Companion from Liant, have you made contact with
Liant support about this problem?

3.  As a technical suggestion, perhaps you can try the Z=7 compile
option when compiling the cmpn-io module which is generated when you
create a file view.  This is really only a shot in the dark, because
we would need to get your cmpn-io module to fully understand what
might be happening.

Liant will attempt to support you consistent with the support
subscription you have.  In addition, Liant is interested in this
problem in case it is exhibiting a previously undiagnosed version
compatibility issue.

Regarding your question about other product availability, to my
knowledge Companion is the only nonGUI, text-based report writer.  I
would be interested in knowing the constraints you have that create
these limits, since most of our market segment is very GUI oriented
right now.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RM/Cobol85

- **From:** krisda@childrensfactory.com (Kris Dames)
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T12:01:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<499a63bb.0310281201.9fdae0e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<499a63bb.0310221310.22f1d060@posting.google.com> <2d819982.0310270732.7f7b5225@posting.google.com>`

```
t.morrison@liant.com (Tom Morrison) wrote in message news:<2d819982.0310270732.7f7b5225@posting.google.com>...
> Kris,
> 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Liant Software Corporation



Ok.  Here are some answers.


1) The version of rm/cobol is definitely 7.50.00

2) The version of Companion is 4.1.2

3) The script that starts Companion uses this command:
     runcobol cmpn-in -k -s 00000000 -c cmpn.cfg -l cmpn-obj.lib

   I modified it to this and got the following results:
     runcobol cmpn-in -c cmpn.cfg -l cmpn-obj.lib

COBOL STOP RUN at line ? in C$RERR ().
COBOL traceback at line 000290 in CMPN-IER (/usr/dev/child/cmpn/cmpn-obj.lib)
compiled 98/12/11 08:30:21.
COBOL traceback at line 2720 in /USR/DEV/CHILD/CMPN/CMPN-IO
(/usr/dev/child/cmpn/cmpn-io.cob) compiled 2003/10/17 15:57:26.
COBOL traceback at line 033106 in CMPN-R (/usr/dev/child/cmpn/cmpn-obj.lib)
compiled 98/12/11 08:30:31.
COBOL traceback at line 004024 in CMPN-CTL (/usr/dev/child/cmpn/cmpn-obj.lib)
compiled 98/12/11 08:29:47.
COBOL traceback at line 001164 in CMPN-IN (/usr/dev/child/cmpn/cmpn-obj.lib)
compiled 98/12/11 08:30:49.


4) I need a non-GUI solution because we use dumb terminals (wyse terminals)
   that telnet in to the linux server. If there is a graphical solution
   that will work over those, I'd love to see it - but it must be as simple
   to use as Companion. 

5) I have been lead to believe that we obtained this directly from Cipher, but
   I am in the process of verifying that.


Thanks for the interest,

Kris Dames
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM/Cobol85

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T15:55:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QpRnb.473$tb5.9@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<499a63bb.0310221310.22f1d060@posting.google.com> <2d819982.0310270732.7f7b5225@posting.google.com> <499a63bb.0310281201.9fdae0e@posting.google.com>`

```
Kris,

I think there are two issues going here...

Kris Dames" <krisda@childrensfactory.com> wrote in message
news:499a63bb.0310281201.9fdae0e@posting.google.com...
> t.morrison@liant.com (Tom Morrison) wrote in message
news:<2d819982.0310270732.7f7b5225@posting.google.com>...
[snip]
> 1) The version of rm/cobol is definitely 7.50.00

How was this determined?  You have not included the copyright notice in the
output shown in 3).

What I am concerned about is that a newer version of runcobol is being used
to run the Companion editor, while an older version of runcobol is being
used to run the Companion runtime.  If this were to be the case, it is
possible that one of the files created by the Companion editor has been
created  with a file version that is not understood by the older runtime.
(RM/COBOL has long had the concept of 'file system version' that we use as
we introduce new features into the file system.  The file system version is
checked when a file is opened, and if the version is in the future, i.e.
greater than the latest file system understood by the runcobol opening the
file, the open of the file will fail with a 94,68 file error code indicating
a future file version.)

Perhaps you could do a 'find /usr -name runcobol -print' command to see if
you have multiple runcobols installed.

>
> 2) The version of Companion is 4.1.2
>
[snip 3) which is a detailed listing of program output]

This is issue 2.  There was a minor bug in version 7.50.00 that might be
related to this regarding C$RERR.  This bug was fixed in 7.50.01 but I
recommend an upgrade to 8.01.

However, this is a bug in the attempt to report the error.  What must really
be determined is what is causing the error that C$RERR is attempting to
report.

>
> 4) I need a non-GUI solution because we use dumb terminals (wyse
terminals)
>    that telnet in to the linux server. If there is a graphical solution
>    that will work over those, I'd love to see it - but it must be as
simple
>    to use as Companion.

Okay....

>
> 5) I have been lead to believe that we obtained this directly from Cipher,
but
>    I am in the process of verifying that.

I think this is most likely true, given the version of Companion you are
using.
```

<a id="t-42bf8ece71"></a>
## Thread: Bit mnipulation — 22 msgs

### Bit mnipulation

- **From:** "Steve Rainbird" <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T08:11:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```
I am migrating a Cobol source from Unisys Aseries Cobol to Microfocus.  In
Aseries Cobol there is an extension to the MOVE verb to allow bit
manipulation.

IE.

MOVE SRC TO TGT [A:B:C]

Would move C bits from bit no A from SRC to TGT starting at bit B. Bits are
number 47 to 0 from left to right.

So in my particular case

     MOVE WS01-COLUMN             TO WS01-INDEX [ 15 : 7 : 8 ].

This would move WS01-COLUMN bits 7 to 15 to WS01-INDEX bits 0 to 7 (and
leave the other bits alone).

Is there any way I can do this in Microfocus Cobol?

TIA
```

#### ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T08:55:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bn8tlq$1fr6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```

"Steve Rainbird" <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com> wrote in message
news:bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com...
> I am migrating a Cobol source from Unisys Aseries Cobol to Microfocus.  In
> Aseries Cobol there is an extension to the MOVE verb to allow bit
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Would move C bits from bit no A from SRC to TGT starting at bit B. Bits
are
> number 47 to 0 from left to right.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve

First off, we need to know more about the declarations of WS01-COLUMN and
WS01-INDEX.  I am going to presume herein that they're USAGE BINARY items
with no scale factor.  That's an A Series word-oriented integer; the topmost
bit's not used; the sign's in the next bit; the next 7 bits should be zero;
and the integer value is maintained in the remaining low-order 38 bits.

I'd expect the following to be equivalent on any sort of binary usage on
just about any system I can think of, so long as that usage covers at least
sixteen bits for both fields:
    IF WS01-INDEX IS GREATER THAN ZERO
        COMPUTE WS01-INDEX = 256 * FUNCTION INTEGER (WS01-INDEX / 256) +
                FUNCTION MOD (FUNCTION INTEGER (WS01-COLUMN / 256), 256)
     ELSE
        COMPUTE WS01-INDEX = 256 * FUNCTION INTEGER (WS01-INDEX / 256) -
                 FUNCTION MOD (FUNCTION INTEGER (WS01-COLUMN / 256), 256).

You might be able to make this a little clearer using a user field as a
temporary!

Obviously this bit transfer isn't going to be as efficient as the original
object code, which was only two instructions (ignoring the retrieval of the
words onto the stack and the storing of the destination):  ISOL (15:8) and
INSR (7:8).  That's a lot quicker than all the COMPUTE gyrations.

You'd probably be better off figuring out exactly what the code was *really*
trying to do with these word-oriented fields, and re-engineer it to use
something more generic, perhaps using *character-oriented* MOVEs.

Also, if you KNOW that WS01-INDEX is zero immediately before the MOVE, then
you don't need to worry about saving off the high-order (n - 8) bits of the
word before putting the information from WS01-COLUMN into it.

      -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

- **From:** "Steve Rainbird" <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T17:10:12+00:00

…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

topmost

zero;

>
> I'd expect the following to be equivalent on any sort of binary usage on

least

>     IF WS01-INDEX IS GREATER THAN ZERO
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

the

>

*really*

>
> Also, if you KNOW that WS01-INDEX is zero immediately before the MOVE,
then

the

>
>

Chuck,

Thanks for your help you are of course correct the data items are USAGE
BINARY.

I'll have to think about whether the rest solves this problem but I really

Once again thanks for you efforts.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T10:26:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bn92vu$1k50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <bn8tlq$1fr6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bn921k$job$1@sparta.btinternet.com>`

```
Part of the problem is what happens *on A Series* if you store something
above bit 38 in the destination word where previously there were zeroes -- 
USAGE BINARY is, in hardware, an INTEGER, not a REAL.  Bits 45:7 reflect the
EXPONENT, bit 46 the mantissa sign, and diddling with either of these won't
have the same impact on the NUMERIC value of the destination on any other
architecture.

Within limits, yes, you can come up with generic solutions -- source bit no
higher than #38, destination bit no higher than #38 -- you can probably come
up with a similar formula.

Beyond that, the code's into deliberate side-effects -- the COBOL program
could be constructing a floating-point value into a fixed-point space, with
results meaningful only on A Series, or in any other architecture or USAGE
that exactly and accurately reflects the A Series 48-bit single-precision
word format (BINARY or REAL).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

- **From:** "lkrupp@pssw.NOSPAM.com.INVALID" <"lkrupp@pssw.NOSPAM.com.INVALID">
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T10:01:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vpfuv1p09r6l42@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```
Steve Rainbird wrote:

> I am migrating a Cobol source from Unisys Aseries Cobol to Microfocus.  In
> Aseries Cobol there is an extension to the MOVE verb to allow bit
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is there any way I can do this in Microfocus Cobol?

If you can get Microfocus COBOL to do bit-wise logical operations, you
could do your own masking and shifting (you could use division by powers
of two for shifting).  You probably knew that.

If you have to, you could call C routines (if you have a C compiler) to
do the bit twiddling.  That would be awkward and possibly slow.  You
probably knew that, too.

If the line you quoted is the only place anything is moved to
WS01-INDEX, the original author may have been using a clever way of
doing something like this:

DIVIDE WS01-COLUMN BY 256 GIVING TEMP1
DIVIDE TEMP1 BY 256 GIVING TEMP2 REMAINDER WS01-INDEX

If WS01-COLUMN never has anything in bits 16 and up, you could try:

DIVIDE WS01-COLUMN BY 256 GIVING WS01-INDEX

... or is it more complicated than that?

Louis Krupp
```

#### ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T16:27:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oJWdnQoOdJtF2gWiU-KYiw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```
Steve Rainbird wrote:

This might get you started on a generic solution:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.  BITBYTE.
*=================================================================
*    NAME: BITBYTE
*
*    PURPOSE: Convert 8 bytes of flags to single byte and
*        vice-versa
*
*    INVOCATION:
*
*        CALL 'BIT-BYTE' USING SINGLE-BYTE EIGHT-BYTES
*        CALL 'BYTE-BIT' USING EIGHT-BYTES SINGLE-BYTE
*
*    DATE WRITTEN:
*
*    SIZE: CODE
*          DATA
*          TOTAL
*
*    REVISIONS:
*
*=================================================================
 COPY BIS.
 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

 01  I                           PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
 01  J                           PIC S9(2) COMP-5.
 01  TEST2                       PIC S9(4) COMP-4.
 01  FILLER REDEFINES TEST2.
     02  TEST-L                  PIC X.
     02  TEST-R                  PIC X.

 LINKAGE SECTION.

 01  SINGLE-BYTE                 PIC X.
 01  EIGHT-BYTES                 PIC X(8).

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     GO TO QUIT.
 MAIN.
     ENTRY 'BIT-BYTE' USING SINGLE-BYTE EIGHT-BYTES.

     MOVE 0 TO TEST2.
     MOVE SINGLE-BYTE TO TEST-R.
     PERFORM VARYING I FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL I > 8
         COMPUTE TEST2 = TEST2 * 2
         IF TEST-L NOT = LOW-VALUES
             MOVE '1' TO EIGHT-BYTES(I:1)
             MOVE LOW-VALUES TO TEST-L
         ELSE
             MOVE '0' TO EIGHT-BYTES(I:1)
             END-IF
         END-PERFORM.
     GO TO QUIT.

 OTHER-MAING.
     ENTRY 'BYTE-BIT' USING EIGHT-BYTES SINGLE-BYTE.
     MOVE 0 TO TEST2.
     PERFORM VARYING I FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL I > 8
         IF EIGHT-BYTES(I:1) NOT = '0'
             COMPUTE J = 8 - I
             COMPUTE TEST2 = TEST2 + 2 ** J
             END-IF
         END-PERFORM.
     MOVE TEST-R TO SINGLE-BYTE.
     GO TO QUIT.
 QUIT.
     GOBACK
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T11:03:38+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f985056_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <oJWdnQoOdJtF2gWiU-KYiw@giganews.com>`

```
Sorry to be "picky" Jerry...

This routine has no initial value on TEST-L.

When using the 'BIT-BYTE' entry it ASSUMES this field has low values in it,
and then clears it only after it has "used" it in the first iteration.

On some platforms this is an invalid assumption...

(Seeing this code brought back some memories... we wrote a very similar
routine for System 360 in the late 60s <G>)

Pete.

news:oJWdnQoOdJtF2gWiU-KYiw@giganews.com...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T19:43:45+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f9a1d6e_3@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <oJWdnQoOdJtF2gWiU-KYiw@giganews.com> <3f985056_5@news.athenanews.com> <eOSdnYdiOPzTfQSiU-KYvA@giganews.com>`

```
Ah, tricked by the old REDEFINES... <G>

Thanks for pointing that out and apologies. <Blushes with embarrassment...>

As I have been "investigating" bit manipulation in Fujitsu COBOL in
connection with other requirements, I came across the following surprising
(well to me at least) facts:

1. If you define a field as Pic 1(?) the representation of it depends on the
USAGE clause.

    So, if you say:   01 my-field pic 1(8)  DISPLAY value 11010011.

    The field is held as 8 BYTES containing 0x3131303130303131.
     (This appears to provide the same functionality as the Bit2Byte
routine, but without any computational overhead. The compiler absorbs it
all...)

    On the other hand...
     if you say:       01 my-field pic 1(8)  BIT value 11010011.

     The field is held as 8 BITS with a value of  0xD3.

      Given that you can MOVE one to the other, it appears to remove the
need for the routine you posted. (It also explains why there is no Fujitsu
equivalent routines to the MicroFocus routines that do this...)

2.  You can define bit arrays using the BIT usage.

      01   twelve-bits pic 1(12) usage BIT.
      01   bit-array REDEFINES twelve-bits  pic 1(1) BIT occurs 12

indexed by  ba-x1.

      This allows access to the twelve bits individually or as a group.

This is because Fujitsu consider pic 1 items to be BOOLEANs. I was pretty
impressed by this as I had never explored it and was used to applying
Logical operations and masks to perform bit manipulation in COBOL.  My
beloved CBL_AND, CBL_OR, CBL_IMP, CBL_EQU and CBL_XOR are now on the back
burner while I check this out, and may be rendered virtually redundant by
this functionality.

Given the above facilities, I can't see any need to do bit manipulation in
other languages. (I was going to do it in Assembler as a last resort - no
need now...)

If anyone has made use of these facilities and found them useful, I'd be
interested to hear what it was used for.

Pete.

<snipped Jerry's interesting code>
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

Pete,
  I know this will depress you <G> but what you describe below is straight
out of the ISO 2002 COBOL Standard.  If Fujitsu has implemented the "full"
Standard, you might want to also look for INTEGER-of-Boolean and
Boolean-of-Integer intrinsic functions.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T21:57:15+13:00

#### ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T16:25:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0310231525.3261f7b1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```
"Steve Rainbird" <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com> wrote in message news:<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com>...
> I am migrating a Cobol source from Unisys Aseries Cobol to Microfocus.  In
> Aseries Cobol there is an extension to the MOVE verb to allow bit
> manipulation.
> 

Steve,

if, like me, you are averse to re-inventing the wheel, I can offer you
a component that does exactly what you are looking for.

I understand that MicroFocus COBOL supports COM components so it
should be very simple to use.

I am currently working on some bit manipulation for XORed LRC check
digit computation and checking, for use in writing magnetic key cards
used by hotels, and it is very easy to extend this to do what you
want.

The component will install itself and a runtime and register itself on
your system.

You must instantiate it ONCE using the method prescribed by MF. 

(In Fujitsu it is as follows:

invoke COMClass "CREATE-OBJECT" using      ProgIDString
                                returning  BitMoveObj
end-invoke

...where "COMClass" is a name that has been equated to the COM Class
via the Repository (this will be different on MF and I have no idea or
documentation on how you achieve it in that environment. You would
need to check the MF docs on COM support...), "ProgIDString" is a
dataname with a value of "BitMove.BitMove.1" (which is the name the
component registers under on your system), and BitMoveObj is an Object
Reference of Class COMClass.)

Once installed and instantiated, it can be invoked as follows:

invoke BitMoveObj "BitMove" using SourceData
                                  TargetData
                                  SourceOffset
                                  TargetOffset
                                  NumberOfBits
end-invoke

(It is also possible to simply SET the properties then invoke the
Method with no parameters, but the above is more familiar for COBOL
programmers.)

If you want to, you can check the special register "PROGRAM-STATUS"
which will be non-zero if a problem was encountered. (I don't use
RETURNING unless I have to, and I understand it is not available on
UNIX systems anyway...)

Note that SourceData and TargetData must both be 48 bits and the
Offsets are calculated as 0 - 47 from right to left, as you described.
NumberOfBits is s9(4) comp-5.

If you are not ready for the 21st Century yet <G> I MIGHT be persuaded
to let you have the Source code so you can compile it on MF as a
CALLable subroutine instead of a component.... (it uses the CBL bit
manipulation functions which, if memory serves, are certainly
available in the MicroFocus environment.)

Mail me privately if interested.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T23:45:36+00:00

but not in the Net Express online dox.  However, what I would suggest that

http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/sx22sp1/sx22indx.htm

and look for:
  "3.5 Manipulating Bits Using Logic (Boolean) Operators"
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T17:07:07+00:00

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Interestingly enough, I can find the documentation in the Server Express -
> but not in the Net Express online dox.  However, what I would suggest that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My reference to bit manipulation was the Windows on-line help format. If he uses the
search key 'bit manipulation' then it takes him on to an old DOS program LOGPER.cbl which
does bit manipulation for Screen Section, and then you go on from there to CBL_AND, CBL_OR
etc......

Anybody new to M/F would be well advised to randomly go through the on-line help -
particularly looking at the CBL_routines which cover many facets. (Plus for printing,
PC_PRINT_routines).

As regards the M/F on-line manuals - they get better and better, incorporating much that
is in the on-line help. (The latest on Character/Screen usage includes some of the old DOS
demo programs - would be useful to Floyd's student if he *was* using an M/F compiler).).

I dislike the Windows 'help' approach intensely. It is so fragmented, and you find
yourself jumping all over the place. Nothing like a good 'read' from a book where the
topic is dealt with in logical order.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Bit mnipulation

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T17:48:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bnbolu$5f4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bn82g5$1em$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```
There are clumsy ways of doing bit manipulation using CoBOL.   If you redefine
your field as binary you can copy that binary field to a work field and do left
and right shift by multiplying and dividing by 2.  (or 4 or 8 or 16 etc).

There are some other tricks available as well like this.   Or you can call
assembler routines.
```

<a id="t-6cffec98bc"></a>
## Thread: Sequential Files in AIX — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Sequential Files in AIX

- **From:** mpineyro <member45438@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T11:45:48-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3515722.1066923948@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi,

We are migrating an application from mainframe, VSE and Cobol II, to AIX
- Visual Age Cobol. We are going to share files between both "worlds",
through FTP, and have to make a decision about what kind of the
sequential files are going to use, here is our situation:



Line Sequential

Disadvantages:

Requires modification in the SELECT of our COBOL programs.

Records are limited for X"0A" (LF) (complicates a little the bin FTP).

Only supports plain text.



Sequential RSD

Advantages

Doesn't require modification in the SELECT of our COBOL programs.

Supports any kind of data.

Disadvantages:

Records are limited for X"0A" (LF) (complicates a little the bin FTP).

Only supports fix record length.



Sequential STL

Advantages

Doesn't require modification in the SELECT of our COBOL programs.

Supports any kind of data.

Records don't need to be limited for X"0A" (LF).

Records can be variable length

Disadvantages:

The file is generated with a header of 64 bytes, and each record with a
header and a trailer of 4 bytes with the length of itself.



Sequential DDM

Advantages

Doesn't require modification in the SELECT of our COBOL programs.

Supports any kind of data.

Records don't need to be limited for X"0A" (LF).

Records can be variable length

Disadvantages:

For each file, a second and hidden file (.DDMMEA) is generated, which
seems to have information about the file.



Any help, any experience, any advice will be appreciated.

Thanks, Mariela.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sequential Files in AIX

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-23T16:06:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310231506.5800587d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3515722.1066923948@dbforums.com>`

```
mpineyro <member45438@dbforums.com> wrote

> We are migrating an application from mainframe, VSE and Cobol II, to AIX
> [...]
> Records are limited for X"0A" (LF) (complicates a little the bin FTP).

Aren't 'Mainframe, VSE' EBCDIC and AIX ASCII ?

Surely you will be doing non-binary ftp.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sequential Files in AIX

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T00:05:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j1_lb.3479$wc3.778@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3515722.1066923948@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0310231506.5800587d@posting.google.com>`

```
This doesn't have to be a problem if one uses ONLY "usage display" and "sign
is separate" for numeric fields.  In such cases, one can do an FTP with an
"automatic" conversion between ASCII and EBCDIC.  If, on the other hand,
there are any COMP-n fields, there will be SIGNIFICANT conversion problems.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sequential Files in AIX

- **From:** mpineyro <member45438@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T09:38:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3519598.1067002702@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3515722.1066923948@dbforums.com>`

```

We are using COMP fields, so we are doing the translation EBCDIC-ASCII
of the correspding fields (not de COMP ones) in the VSE and then, we
make a BIN FTP.
```

```
I am having difficulty with the field   JOB-STATUS-DATE
which is a packed decimal field. 
Using DFSORT I am trying to select records greater than 37600


using file-aid I mapped a sample record

 File-AID - Browse - TC.BGLPROD.TABLES.CL --------
 COMMAND ===> show p(icture)                              
 RECORD: 19063                      JOB-RECORD    
 ---- FIELD LEVEL/NAME ------- PICTURE- ----+----1
   10 JOB-CONT-PHONE-EXCHANGE  999      243       
   10 JOB-CONT-PHONE-NUMBER    9(4)     3044      
 5 JOB-PROGRAM-DESCRIPTION     X(40)              
 5 JOB-BILLING-CRED-DATE       9(5)     37893     
 5 JOB-STATUS-DATE             9(5)     37767     
 5 JOB-DEFAULT-INDICATOR       X    
****************************** BOTTOM OF DATA **              

 File-AID - Browse - TC.BGLPROD.TABLES.CL -------
 COMMAND ===> show f(ormat)                                     
 RECORD: 19063                      JOB-RECORD   
 ---- FIELD LEVEL/NAME ------- -FORMAT- ----+----
   10 JOB-CONT-PHONE-EXCHANGE    3/NUM  243      
   10 JOB-CONT-PHONE-NUMBER      4/NUM  3044     
 5 JOB-PROGRAM-DESCRIPTION      40/AN            
 5 JOB-BILLING-CRED-DATE         3/P    37893    
 5 JOB-STATUS-DATE               3/P    37767    
 5 JOB-DEFAULT-INDICATOR         1/AN            
 ****************************** BOTTOM OF DATA **


 File-AID - Browse - TC.BGLPROD.TABLES.CL 
 COMMAND ===>                                              
----+----7----+----8----+----9----+----0----+----1----+----
82433044                                         i   @     
FFFFFFFF44444444444444444444444444444444444444443833774   <-------
37767C  82433044000000000000000000000000000000000000000079F76C0


Selecting the records using FILE-AID works correctly using 
this criteria: 

                                             
    AND                                      
Cmd /OR Position Length RO                   
--- --- -------- ------ -- ------------------
___      1              EQ T'job'            
___ AND  512            GT X'37600C'         


Below is the DFSORT card. The last condition is where I'm having 
difficulty. I've tried many variations but had no luck. Also
IBM, Quickref, etc dont have samples for the PACKED DECIMAL format


//SYSIN    DD  *                                                  
 SORT FIELDS=COPY                                                 
 INCLUDE COND=(1,3,CH,EQ,C'JOB',AND,88,1,CH,NE,C'C',AND,          
   (11,3,CH,NE,C'ADJ',OR,11,3,CH,NE,C'OMB'),AND,85,4,PD,GT,37600)
<=========
 OUTREC FIELDS=(1,7,C'","',11,5,C'","',28,40,C'",',20X)           


I've tried these variations with no success:

85,4,PD,GT,c'37600C'
85,4,PD,GT,x'37600C' 
85,4,PD,GT,p'37600C' 
85,4,PD,GT,p'37600' 
85,4,PD,GT,37600
85,5,PD,GT,37600 


If anyone has any sample JCL, suggestions, ideas I'd
really appreciate it

Thanks in Advance

George Lewycky
NY CITY TRANSIT
Brooklyn NY  USA
gelewyc@nyct.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Returned (Finally!)

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T06:03:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20031025070728.762$uD@news.newsreader.com>`
- **References:** `<vpgou71ujlb961@corp.supernews.com> <bn9tno$4qo$1@panix1.panix.com> <vpjah1nlj66u34@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> into our configuration management system once I got back.

If you use a disk image backup program, such as Power Quest's Drive
Copy or Drive Image, or Norton Ghost, you can restore your entire
boot partition and/or any other partitions to their identical backed up
state. Even finicky 'copy protected' software can be backed up and
restored this way.

> It's a pain
> setting up all the applications again, though - it takes time to get
> them tweaked again the way I had them.

It takes me several days to set up a new PC the way I want it. Several
years ago, after a system crash had me down for nearly a week, I made
the decision to, from then on, buy my primary development PCs in
identical pairs. Everything is identical except the serial numbers. It's
more expensive, but you can buy what was state of the art technology
three or six months ago for much less. It's also handy having an extra
PC around to burn CDs and stuff while you're working on the other
machine.

I keep all files, including email, updated on both PCs and notebook at
least once a day. Using LapLink's 'clone folder' function over a LAN
takes only seconds. When I am writing code, I back up those files
several times a day, usually every hour or so, and before/after major
changes, to Zip disk or CD. My intention is to put master backups in
my safe deposit box every month. I make the master backups, but am
too lazy to take copies to the bank. :-)
```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> incrementals with WinZip.

You would be surprised (well, maybe not) how many times I have done a
lot of editing on a source file, then realized I had deleted or modified a
piece of code written only a few hours before, and needed the 'original'. In
Windows an errant click can destroy lots of stuff, and when the 'stuff' was
created earlier that day, a daily backup won't help.
```

was
> created earlier that day, a daily backup won't help.

Why don't you use source code management? Something like CVS is free.. can
run locally or remote.  That way you shouldn't ever have a problem
(especially if you combine with a quality development ide that keeps
versions as you go).

JCE

>> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

I would not be surprised by any of this at all... and my own experience 
with such things has caused me to move slowly and patiently, with 
deliberation and intention.  I also have learned that on my own PC I am no 
longer charged per CPU cycle, XIO or byte of storage... an amazing amount 
of freedom that allows me for my own personal sloppiness.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Returned (Finally!)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T22:33:28+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f9b94fc_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<vpgou71ujlb961@corp.supernews.com> <bn9tno$4qo$1@panix1.panix.com> <vpjah1nlj66u34@corp.supernews.com> <20031025070728.762$uD@news.newsreader.com> <bne0gu$fo9$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Doc,

I checked this out but it doesn't support NTFS and it is limited to the DOS
partition size of 8 Gigs.

As I am using a 40 GB NTFS partition, it isn't any use to me.

However, it DOES do an image copy which is something I would dearly like to
have. I have been toying with the idea of writing my own in Assembler but
I've been too busy lately.

I wonder if anyone has come across a similar utility that will really do the
biz for Windows systems?

Everything I have tried (including Norton Ghost...) fails dismally on locked
windows files.

All  (non-obscene) suggestions gratefully accepted <G>.

Pete.
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

In article <3f9b94fc_8@news.athenanews.com>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>Doc,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>As I am using a 40 GB NTFS partition, it isn't any use to me.

Such is Life, aye.

>
>However, it DOES do an image copy which is something I would dearly like to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>windows files.

Have you tried using Ghost after booting to DOS?  The friend I mentioned 
in another posting is using Win98 and Ghost always hangs when he tries to 
invoke it from a DOS window... but when he boots the entire system to DOS 
then Ghost runs admirably.  He's only used Ghost to write to a CD... 
hmmmmmm, perhaps I can see what Ghost does when it is directed to a Jaz 
drive...

... but enough about my problem.  Adaptec had a product called 'Take Two' 
that shipped with its Easy CD Creator software but that was discontinued 
by Roxio (who bought the software) because of problems with Win2K and 
WinXP.  What's your OS?

(I assume you are looking for what I have... the ability to, essentially, 
power down your machine, replace a fried drive, boot to DOS and restore to 
where you were previously.)

DD
```

All righty... for a start, then, take a look-see at 
http://www.disk-image.net/.  I have no experience with this software but 
it claims to have DOS-boot restore abilities for NTFS disks larger than 
8G... and there's a free, fully-functioning demo, too.  (The 'big name', 
PowerQuest's Drive Image, apparently doesn't like XP's NTFS partitions).

If that doesn't work then all I can suggest is a good Googling with "drive 
image" backup ntfs dos.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Returned (Finally!)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@XXXX.optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T18:04:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<07dnb.19323$ox.2114879@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3f9c7aa9_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<vpgou71ujlb961@corp.supernews.com> <20031025070728.762$uD@news.newsreader.com> <bne0gu$fo9$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f9b94fc_8@news.athenanews.com> <bngp5m$1so$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f9c7aa9_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> <docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bngp5m$1so$1@panix1.panix.com...
> <snip>> >
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>>WinXP.  What's your OS?

I bought the Easy CD Creator upgrade to get the Take Two option, but it 
didn't work with a W2K NTFS HDD.

> I'm using Win XP Professional, very happily.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, that is exactly the Nirvana I am seeking...<G>

I had major problems a year or 2 ago with DriveImage, meaning I simply 
couldn't get it to work with my NTFS HDD under W2K so Symantec gave me a 
refund; but have had excellent success with the current version of Ghost 
(2003.789). Good thing for me, too, because I replaced the HDD in my 
newish IBM ThinkPad with a bigger one and even though I used IBM's 
recovery CDs (which I had to request - IBM's current recovery strategy 
is to recover one's system from a hidden partition on the HDD called 
"Rapid Recovery") I ended up with an unbootable system. The recovery 
routines seemed to be working as planned and ground thru all 4 CDs, but 
PartitionMagic showed an empty, unformatted HDD when I was done. 
Fortunately, I had Ghosted the TP a week before, so I booted from the 
Ghost floppy and restored everything from the CDs I'd created. It was a 
perfect restoration - didn't even upset Norton's AntiVirus (which is 
sensitive to this sort of thing). The TP is running Win XP Pro.

Follow up on IBM's Rapid Recovery, my new HDD didn't have the hidden 
partition, of course; but I was able to create one after installing 
Rapid Recovery Ultra. The nice thing about this is that the image is of 
my current system with all my software & tweaks, so restoring wouldn't 
mean going all the way back to the factory image of the HDD.
```

```
I just saw new hard drives in the US 7200rpm 8mb cache....80GB for under
$50.

(a) Raid your PC - the initial investment is higher to buy the new system
:-)
(b) Use a boot loader and copy from one to the other.

This is cheaper and more efficient than tapes/zips/cds. If you get a nice
server case you could even just pop the suckers in and out....

JCE

Interesting stats...  I've talked with the detectives in both the city 
where it happened, and the county, which oversees the pawn shops.  The 
particular area where we were used to be really bad, but they said we 
were the first vehicle theft they had in several weeks.  But, they said, 
when they did have a really big problem, a lot of the stuff -did- show 
back up - and, since this county has lots of experience, the detective 
said they keep a pretty tight leash on them.

This is where you hope the non-law-abiding citizen sells it to a 
law-abiding pawn shop.  :)
```

clvrmnky wrote:

> Richard wrote:
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Though, I am looking at some docs on nested subprograms right now.

Keep looking.  They're great.  :)  It's like a componentized structured 
program.  And, with proper linkage sections, they can behave almost like 
a function would in some other languages.  I'm hooked - although it's 
not something I put in every program I do, I have found a couple of 
places where it greatly simplifies the code, which makes things more 
maintainable too.
```

### SHARE COBOL, PL/I, and LE Requirements

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T05:32:22+00:00

<a id="t-c98b53a314"></a>
## Thread: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii — 1 this month / 1161 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [2003-03](2003-03.md#t-c98b53a314)._

#### ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

- **From:** raul nazaret <member45525@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T01:56:51-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3518621.1066975011@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com>`

```

it is easy to say unpack the comp-3 field first but when you are
confronted with multiple number of files and you don't have anymore time
or expertise to do the unpacking the best approach i would recommend is
that you download all your mainframe files in ebcdic format and handle
the conversion of the pack field in the pc.



the hex format of any field will be preserved and just create a program
that would convert hex field to to its equivalent ascii code. of course
you need to know the location of your pack field that you can have a
special routine to convert the hex fields within the pack field.



example: when you look at a byte and it is hex C1, then you know it is
alphabet "A" and so on and forth (consult your hex table for ebcdic).
the name "ALAN" will have ebcdic hex value of C1D3C1D5".



if you dont have any time constraint, the rest of the guys are right for
you to unpack it first before you download it and many downloading tools
will automatically convert your ebcdic to ascii.
```

<a id="t-7e20878ae6"></a>
## Thread: create daily dataset — 2 msgs

### create daily dataset

- **From:** reganmian@yahoo.com (Paul)
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T05:41:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<543f201d.0310240441.1d84bc89@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi 

A novice question:

How to create/define a daily based database
is it alright for below ?
SYSUT    DD DSN=ABCD.E.F.GGGG.RPRP.KMKM.SORTED(+1),
         DISP=(NEW,CATLG),                               
         UNIT=DISK,                                     
         SPACE=(CYL,(1,1),RLSE),                      
         DCB=(TD.NULL,RECFM=FB,LRECL=100)              

and make a monthly concatenation, and initialization for the daily

many thanks for the help

Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: create daily dataset

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T22:40:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0310252140.24796928@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<543f201d.0310240441.1d84bc89@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Paul,

First, some general comments. 

Have you declared the generation group index? If not, you'll have to
do that first via IDCAMS. See the IBM Access Method Svcs Manual.

When you read the concatenation you'll be reading the most recently
created data first. If you need the data in date order, you'll have to
sort by create date. If your data doesn't have one, you've got a
problem to solve.

See your msg below for further comments specific to your JCL.

Regards, Jack.


reganmian@yahoo.com (Paul) wrote in message news:<543f201d.0310240441.1d84bc89@posting.google.com>...
> Hi 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> SYSUT    DD DSN=ABCD.E.F.GGGG.RPRP.KMKM.SORTED(+1),
===> Remember, there's a 45 pos limit on DSN including periods and
G...V..
>          DISP=(NEW,CATLG),
===> Good idea to use abend DISP, e.g. DISP=(NEW,CATLG,DELETE).
Especially
===> important with GDGs, else you'll get invalid/incomplete data
include in ===> the Group.
>          UNIT=DISK,                                     
>          SPACE=(CYL,(1,1),RLSE),                      
>          DCB=(TD.NULL,RECFM=FB,LRECL=100)
===> Don't forget to block the file. BLKSIZE=0 gives an optimal block
size for ===> the device type assigned.
> 
> and make a monthly concatenation, and initialization for the daily
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Paul
```

## Thread: ODBC — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### ODBC

- **From:** "DLI" <equisoft@starband.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T21:02:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Prgmb.1377$zd2.423938152@twister2.starband.net>`

```
Is there an ODBC driver available that would allow me to read COBOL dat
files from Crystal reports or MS Access?

TIA
```

#### ↳ Re: ODBC

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-24T16:43:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YnydnY0j04CTAASiU-KYhg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<Prgmb.1377$zd2.423938152@twister2.starband.net>`

```
DLI wrote:
> Is there an ODBC driver available that would allow me to read COBOL
> dat files from Crystal reports or MS Access?

There's no such thing as a "COBOL dat" file. A COBOL program can write files
in almost any format, but there are COBOL formats that are proprietary to
the COBOL compiler vendor.

You'll have to tell us what compiler vendor and what format. While text and
csv files are universal, relative, variable-length, and ISAM (Indexed
Sequential Access Method) files vary depending on the vendor.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC

- **From:** "DLI" <equisoft@starband.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T17:04:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y8Tmb.1646$kr1.503606333@twister2.starband.net>`
- **References:** `<Prgmb.1377$zd2.423938152@twister2.starband.net> <YnydnY0j04CTAASiU-KYhg@giganews.com>`

```
Thank you for the response.
My company is Equisoft, Inc.

Here is my situation. We provide software to the Equine industry. One of our
competitors has gone out of business. (The sole proprietor is deceased). The
competitor's name was EDMS (Equine Data Management Systems)

Their application was written in COBOL which appears to be a product vended
by
Liant Software Corporation

I have a copy of the folder that contains the COBOL source and data. The
files that contain the program information (based on their file dates and
size) have an extension of DAT

There is a reg file in the folder that contains references to
Liant Software Corporation

The potential customer would just like us to import the current list of
clients from the COBOL system into the product that we sell.

We have MS certified engineers in Access and SQL Server but none with
experience using COBOL.
I suspect that these files are ISAM but I have no way of knowing for sure.

files

> the COBOL compiler vendor.
>
> You'll have to tell us what compiler vendor and what format. While text
and
> csv files are universal, relative, variable-length, and ISAM (Indexed
> Sequential Access Method) files vary depending on the vendor.
>

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T13:12:36-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KJidnTpX6t40gQGiU-KYjQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<Prgmb.1377$zd2.423938152@twister2.starband.net> <YnydnY0j04CTAASiU-KYhg@giganews.com> <Y8Tmb.1646$kr1.503606333@twister2.starband.net>`

```
DLI wrote:
> Thank you for the response.
> My company is Equisoft, Inc.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> sure.

There are a couple of possible solutions:
1. Assuming your prospect is still running the mysterious software, he can
probably export the data to some universal (text?) format. From this
intermediate reckoning, you can probably one-off an import program.

2. If the original owner is deceased, perhaps a modest fee to his estate
will get you access to the original source code.

3. If, by inspection, you can't make heads or tails out of the data file,
the data file is probably ISAM. If the data were a relative or
variable-length file, you'd see the data with an occassional "what's this?"
intersperced. Entering the terms: "liant cobol isam" into Google resulted in
almost 100 references, including several conversion tools.

The inherent difficulty in #3 is the specificity of the data. Here's the
number "000500." Is that a price? A length? Quantity sold last December?
Number of relatives with red hair?

Our company once bought out a competitor in a situation similar to yours -
it worked out well for everyone: us, the competitor, and the customers. We
paid nothing up front, and only paid as the customers converted to our
system (at less that our normal sales commission). Our costs were a
conversion program - we gained access to the competitor's source - and a
little technical support.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T18:30:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<H8WdndaoKPmh-gGiU-KYjA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<Prgmb.1377$zd2.423938152@twister2.starband.net> <YnydnY0j04CTAASiU-KYhg@giganews.com> <Y8Tmb.1646$kr1.503606333@twister2.starband.net> <KJidnTpX6t40gQGiU-KYjQ@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> DLI wrote:
>> Thank you for the response.
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> competitor's source - and a little technical support.

Arghh! I overlooked your statement that you had the original source. That
being the case, anyone conversant with COBOL can tell the data layout. To
confirm you're dealing with an ISAM file, look at the beginning of each
source module for statements of the form:

SELECT something ASSIGN TO something
   ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
   ACCESS IS something
   RECORD KEY IS something
   FILE STATUS IS  something.

Finding the above, you've got indexed files.
```

Since there is a prospect of converting a number of users of the defunct
EDMS application, I would suggest using Relativity.

Relativity uses the COBOL source files to extract the physical schema for
the data files and allows you to create an ODBC data source which you may
use directly from Access.

So, the answer to your original question is: Yes!
http://www.liant.com/products/relativity/

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software COrporation

<a id="t-a61ad3e474"></a>
## Thread: conversion to Cobol-390 considerations — 6 msgs

### conversion to Cobol-390 considerations

- **From:** B Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T14:17:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5Cvmb.5955$v82.480684@twister.southeast.rr.com>`

```
I am considering taking a contract to upgrade IBM mainframe applications 
from Cobol-74 and Cobol II to Cobol-390. 

The company is already using LE, but since all database IO is done by 
Assembler IO modules, there is some confusion/uncertainty about link 
parameters (above/below line). The move from Cobol II seems fairly 
straightforward, recompiling & testing.  The upgrade from Cobol-74 will 
require some searching for syntax that is no longer supported (Exhibit, 
Trace), and replacing those constructs with newer variations (perhaps 
declaratives).

Can anyone think of any other considerations, or point me to 
readings/resources that might be helpful ?

Thanks -
Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: conversion to Cobol-390 considerations

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T16:10:48-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F9ACAB8.343B1047@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<5Cvmb.5955$v82.480684@twister.southeast.rr.com>`

```
B Berman wrote:
> 
> I am considering taking a contract to upgrade IBM mainframe applications
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> declaratives).

Check to see what the AMODE of the Assembler routines are.  You can use
AMBLIST or any handy utility your systems department might have.  If
they are AMODE 24, you will have to use the DATA(24) option at least
until you can get the Assembler routines upgraded to AMODE 31.  You
might see if you can get them replaced with COBOL programs.

> 
> Can anyone think of any other considerations, or point me to
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> ----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: conversion to Cobol-390 considerations

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@XXXX.optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T19:54:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7EVmb.9519$I7.2838886@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3F9ACAB8.343B1047@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<5Cvmb.5955$v82.480684@twister.southeast.rr.com> <3F9ACAB8.343B1047@istar.ca>`

```
Clark F. Morris, Jr. wrote:

> B Berman wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> might see if you can get them replaced with COBOL programs.

Or at least get the Asm routines linked as AMODE(ANY) so you can exploit 
XA storage. They really shouldn't have any problems running in 31-bit 
mode (which means nothing, of course).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: conversion to Cobol-390 considerations

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T22:04:26+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ds9opvkl85mjbgvrae7p5ro8qt6jfhuagu@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5Cvmb.5955$v82.480684@twister.southeast.rr.com> <3F9ACAB8.343B1047@istar.ca> <7EVmb.9519$I7.2838886@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
On Sun, 26 Oct 2003 19:54:43 GMT Liam Devlin <LiamD@XXXX.optonline.net> wrote:

:>Clark F. Morris, Jr. wrote:

:>> B Berman wrote:
 
:>>>I am considering taking a contract to upgrade IBM mainframe applications
:>>>from Cobol-74 and Cobol II to Cobol-390.

:>>>The company is already using LE, but since all database IO is done by
:>>>Assembler IO modules, there is some confusion/uncertainty about link
:>>>parameters (above/below line). The move from Cobol II seems fairly
:>>>straightforward, recompiling & testing.  The upgrade from Cobol-74 will
:>>>require some searching for syntax that is no longer supported (Exhibit,
:>>>Trace), and replacing those constructs with newer variations (perhaps
:>>>declaratives).
 
:>> Check to see what the AMODE of the Assembler routines are.  You can use
:>> AMBLIST or any handy utility your systems department might have.  If
:>> they are AMODE 24, you will have to use the DATA(24) option at least
:>> until you can get the Assembler routines upgraded to AMODE 31.  You
:>> might see if you can get them replaced with COBOL programs.

:>Or at least get the Asm routines linked as AMODE(ANY) so you can exploit 
:>XA storage. They really shouldn't have any problems running in 31-bit 
:>mode (which means nothing, of course).

Huh?

One does not need to linkedit a routine AMODE(ANY) to exploit XA storage. 

In fact, all linking AMODE(ANY) does is tell the contents supervisor that the
routine should be invoked in the AMODE of the caller (and does force the
loader to load the program in 24 bit storage).

Also, old assembler routines cannot be safely assumed to be able to run in 31
bit mode. It is quite possible that the top byte of registers are used for
data and not cleared.
```

#### ↳ Re: conversion to Cobol-390 considerations

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T23:26:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fEDmb.2344$Px2.947@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<5Cvmb.5955$v82.480684@twister.southeast.rr.com>`

```
1) Read (from cover to cover):
   Enterprise COBOL for z/OS and OS/390 V3R2 Migration Guide
at:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3MG10/CCONTENTS

about:
   Edge Portfolio Analyzer
at:

and
  IBM Cobol and CICS Command Level Conversion Aid for OS/390 & MVS & VM
at:

http://www-3.ibm.com/common/ssi/OIAccess.wss?DocURL=http://d03xhttpcl001g.boulder.ibm.com/common/ssi/rep_oc/e/460f2ffd5eed3e6f872567df006cd79e/index.html&InfoType=OC&InfoSubType=null&InfoDesc=Offering+Catalogue

On Sat, 25 Oct 2003 14:17:37 GMT B Berman <bberman@netbox.com> wrote:

:>I am considering taking a contract to upgrade IBM mainframe applications 
:>from Cobol-74 and Cobol II to Cobol-390. 

:>The company is already using LE, but since all database IO is done by 
:>Assembler IO modules, there is some confusion/uncertainty about link 
:>parameters (above/below line). The move from Cobol II seems fairly 
:>straightforward, recompiling & testing.  The upgrade from Cobol-74 will 
:>require some searching for syntax that is no longer supported (Exhibit, 
:>Trace), and replacing those constructs with newer variations (perhaps 
:>declaratives).

:>readings/resources that might be helpful ?

I hope your skillset includes assembler. If not, get some.

Is it possible to move the database I/O to COBOL directly?

<a id="t-d5230563ba"></a>
## Thread: Importing data into MS-SQL from AcuCobol — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Importing data into MS-SQL from AcuCobol

- **From:** "jpo" <asdf@asdf.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T03:14:40+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f9aa05e$0$23566$41ce378f@news.swiftel.com.au>`

```
Hi there people,

I've recently been dumped in at the deepend at a company which needs to
import data from a product system using AcuCobol data files. The data is
currently stored as data files and xfd files, and we are using AcuODBC as he
interface to load the data.

This is ok except that it is VERY slow - when I say slow it is currently
take a matter of hours and it SHOULD really only take about 20-30 min for
the volume of data we are using.

What is really the best way to move forward with this? I'm not getting a lot
of help from our partners here in Australia and I just feel I am spining my
wheels because I don't have the experience.

We intitally tried loading two tables (data files) at once and it
continuously crashed giving a type mismatch error - which was rubish because
it ran fine in serial. After much fiddling around I worked out you could
load two or more tables in parrallel if you created two or more odbc DSNs,
but this just meant that the loads of each table ran twice as slow, so
instead of two files taking 5 min each in serial they took 10 min in
parrallel.

The files were orriginally being accessed across a network share, so I had
the NIC interface upgraded to 1gig - this didn't change things at all, so I
arranged for the files to be delivered to the local harddrive - this changed
nothing as well.

I have been trialling AcuODBC Server but I don't think this is the tool I
want either, and besides it seems to be throwing an incorrect type
conversion error when I use the AcuODBC Server instead of a direct AcuODBC
connection. Our vendor here suggested Acu4GL, but from my reading this
appears to be a tool to read SQL RDBMS systems from a COBOL front end,
rather than load mass volumes of data from a VISION file into a SQL DWH.

Our vendor very kindly offered to build a program for each file that would
dump the data down to an ascii text file. For over 500 files I told them
"thanks - but no thanks".

I'm really out of ideas here and I'm getting kind of desperate, if anyone
has any help I would be greatfull.

My line of logic would be to write a program or use an application I can
script in Win2K to dump the contents of the data files using the xfd files
provided. I could then import this ascii file extremely quickly into SQL
server.

I've tried vutil but it doesn't seem to be the horse for the race.

Thanks very much in advance for any assistance anyone can offer.
Regards
JPO
```

>"thanks - but no thanks".
Is he charging for it? if not then let him do it..

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>server.
Writting a program to "export" each file may be the fastest way, but
that will depend on the size of the files.

If you have a ACUCOBOL Windows development you may be able to create
your own programs to read the COBOL files and upload them to SQLServer
by using direct calls to ADODB.

I don't have a Windows development so I can't try this for you, but I
am sure it will be possible.

#### ↳ Re: Importing data into MS-SQL from AcuCobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T18:48:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bzzmb.7238$8x2.3910702@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3f9aa05e$0$23566$41ce378f@news.swiftel.com.au>`

```
> I've recently been dumped in at the deepend at a company which needs to
> import data from a product system using AcuCobol data files. The data is
> currently stored as data files and xfd files, and we are using AcuODBC as
he
> interface to load the data.

Not familiar with "AcuODBC", but one of the greatest sources of slowdowns
i've seen with ODBC software in general is if the software does an
SqlExecDirect for each row instead of doing a PREPARE of the statement,
binding the parameter(s) and then just EXECUTE'ing.  I've also seen software
(e.g., Mercator mapper) which opens and closes the database connection each
time it executes.

These are both performance killers of which Quentin Tarentino would be
proud.

If "AcuODBC" is a generic tool, I'd doubt that eiher of these
situations -especailly not number two) exist, but it's probably worth asking
about.

Also, you may want to see if you can disable transaction support... since it
is a conversion, it probably is not terribly useful to carry the overhead
necessary to be able to ROLLBACK each INSERT, as you can always "start over"
with empty tables. Maybe there is some kind of option you can set for this
in AcuODBC.


MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Importing data into MS-SQL from AcuCobol

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-25T16:43:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xM6dnSkwbo-hfQeiRVn-vg@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<3f9aa05e$0$23566$41ce378f@news.swiftel.com.au>`

```
A few suggestions:

- If your database tables have any indexes, drop them before loading the
data, then recreate them after it is loaded.  This is a good idea no matter
how you are loading the data.

- Look into using DTS (Data Transformation Services) in SQL Server.  I have
not used it with AcuODBC, but it should work.  You can create a DTS package
that will read data from the Vision files (using AcuODBC) and store the data
in SQL Server.  You can script the whole thing and even schedule it to run
automatically.

- The vendor's suggestion of creating export files is not a bad one.  If you
can get the data into delimited files, ideally that mimic the table layouts
exactly, you can execute the SQL Server "bulk insert" statement or the bcp
utility, which loads data into SQL Server very quickly.  (One tip - with the
"bulk insert" statement, the filename you reference needs to exist on the
system were SQL Server is running.)

It shouldn't matter whether there are 2 or 500 files - if you have a single
program that creates all 500 files, you can set up a single SQL script with
a "bulk insert" statement (or a batch file with "bcp" commands) that will
load all 500 files.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Importing data into MS-SQL from AcuCobol

- **From:** "jpo" <asdf@asdf.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T16:25:29+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f9b59b7$0$23561$41ce378f@news.swiftel.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3f9aa05e$0$23566$41ce378f@news.swiftel.com.au> <xM6dnSkwbo-hfQeiRVn-vg@comcast.com>`

```
I think may have left out a few points.

All the stuff you can do to improve performance in SQL server has been done
(drop indexes, disable log, enable 'bulk insert/into' etc. etc.) Yes, we are
trying to use DTS. It does "work" but it is "DOG SLOW" when we import data
using the AcuODBC interface.

See the following statistics for an example of loading times for just one
simple table with 150,000 records and 120 columns

Using Bulk Insert (GUI wrapper to BCP) from ascii file    35-40 seconds
Using Oracle OLE-DB to MS-SQL OLE-DB   2 - 2.5 minutes
Using AcuODBC to MS-SQL OLE-DB    5.5 - 6 minutes

As you can see the optimal preference is an ascii flat file, or failing this
a better interface. I have looked all through the doco for the ODBC driver
and there is nothing I can see that helps with performance. I just find it
hard to believe that no one has ever experienced this problem or challenge
before. Is there anyone lurking around this newsgroup from AcuCOBOL that
could help me out with the right tool for the job? Promise the beers are on
me :-)

The vendors offer was to write one program per file - aka 500 programs for
500 files. This is a live product system and the tables are continuously
being changed as we change the product system - aka maintenance nightmare,
and yes they are charging us BIG bucks. The result, is that I'm not very
interested, more on principal, but the financial aspect is a nasty obstical,
and the maintenance issue means at least one of the programs would be
useless in a week and would have to be rewritten. So that is the end of that
solution.

My preferance is to get the data into flat ascii files and do a bulk insert
from there, but I can't work out a way to get the data into flat
files --fast and efficently-- I'm sure it can't be hard, I'm a software
engineer and I have a reasonable level of skill in developing in C and VB so
I would be happy to do it myself but I just don't know where to start, and
would rather leverage the commercial efforts if it was maintainable.

Once again if anyone can help out I would be most grateful :-)

Thanks in advance
JPO
