On Sun, 26 Oct 2003 16:25:29 +1100, "jpo" <asdf@asdf.com> wrote:

>I think may have left out a few points.
And you still haven't told us which version of the compiler you are
using, and on what OS's you have it.
>
>All the stuff you can do to improve performance in SQL server has been done
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Using Bulk Insert (GUI wrapper to BCP) from ascii file    35-40 seconds
And how long does it take to create the ascii file? 1 second? or is
that time included on these 35-40 seconds?

>Using Oracle OLE-DB to MS-SQL OLE-DB   2 - 2.5 minutes
Hum. Why oracle?
>Using AcuODBC to MS-SQL OLE-DB    5.5 - 6 minutes

Have you tried using ACUSQL? I would if you haven't done it yet.
Not sure if it will be faster in any way.
For this you would have COBOL program that reads the COBOL file and
issues SQL inserts into the SQL server.
This will also give you "maintenance nightmares" (see below for
meaning)
(and yes the use of ACUSQL is not just to read from DB's, but also to
write to them).

>
>As you can see the optimal preference is an ascii flat file, or failing this
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>me :-)
IF you can manage BCP to convert from COBOL numeric data types then
the best would be a mix of "vutil -unload " and BCP (not sure which
options of -unload would be better on this case). I could not find a
way to do it, but if your files DO NOT have COMP-xxx fields on it then
you can do it this way without any big issues.

>
>The vendors offer was to write one program per file - aka 500 programs for
>500 files. This is a live product system and the tables are continuously
>being changed as we change the product system - aka maintenance nightmare,
>and yes they are charging us BIG bucks. The result, is that I'm not very
What do you call big bucks. 1 Euro a program, 10, 20, 50, 100 or more?
Unless all your files have more than one record type (or even
sub-record type (e.g. redefines of part of the record), these are very
easy and quick programs to do. (PROBLEM A)

My company can do this, if you are interested, after consultation for
a probably very good price.

>interested, more on principal, but the financial aspect is a nasty obstical,
>and the maintenance issue means at least one of the programs would be
>useless in a week and would have to be rewritten. So that is the end of that
>solution.
Hum.
Whatever solution you go with you are going to have to maintain the
programs.
If using BCP you need to change the BCP format file.

If using a COBOL program to create a flat file you just need to change
two copybooks. One with the real file definition and the other with
the Edited file (and with the fields you need, and with the separators
you wish). Very easy to do apart from PROBLEM A mentioned before.
(and you still need to change the BCP format file)

Doing it the way you are doing it now you also need to change
something (just don't know what).

So whatever way you do it you are going to have "maintenance
nightmares".


>
>My preferance is to get the data into flat ascii files and do a bulk insert
>from there, but I can't work out a way to get the data into flat
>files --fast and efficently-- I'm sure it can't be hard, I'm a software
>engineer and I have a reasonable level of skill in developing in C and VB so
See above. Vutil is quick, but then you have the BCP problem unless
you can find a way to do it.

>I would be happy to do it myself but I just don't know where to start, and
>would rather leverage the commercial efforts if it was maintainable.
The maintenance effort on all the options will be very similar.

>
Snip all remaining
..


Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Importing data into MS-SQL from AcuCobol

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T20:44:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zqGdnTkCgs_x5QGiRVn-jg@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<3f9aa05e$0$23566$41ce378f@news.swiftel.com.au> <xM6dnSkwbo-hfQeiRVn-vg@comcast.com> <3f9b59b7$0$23561$41ce378f@news.swiftel.com.au>`

```
If the speed issue is in AcuODBC, then only Acucorp is going to be able to
answer that.  Try support@acucorp.com - they are usually pretty helpful.

My only other suggestion might be to determine where the speed issue is - is
it really with AcuODBC or is it with the combination of AcuODBC and DTS.
(Note that when you are using DTS, it is not doing a bulk insert/bcp.)

Look on www.hotfiles.com and do a search for "ODBC export".  There are
several utilities there (free and not free) that will allow you to create a
delimited file from a table through ODBC.  Download one and time how long it
takes to export data from the large Acucobol file (through AcuODBC) to a
delimited file.

If that's relatively quick, then you have the beginnings of a possible
solution.  Find an ODBC utility (or write one yourself - see below) that
lets you script your exports and use that to create the export files, then
script the imports with bcp commands or "bulk insert" commands through Query
Analyzer.

There are a variety of ways you could write the export portion yourself.  If
you are handy with C, that would be the fastest way in terms of runtime
speed.  It's not that hard if you know C and the ODBC API (I have utility
that I wrote in C that exports data from ODBC to delimited files, but it's
part of a larger system and wouldn't be of much use if I posted it - and my
employer owns the code, so I probably couldn't anyway).  But there are
several examples on msdn.microsoft.com that use the ODBC API to get data
from a "select" statement - all that is needed is to add code to open a file
and write the data with delimiters between the fields to the file.

The other ways that come to mind are to use Visual Basic or VBScript using
the Windows Scripting Host (wscript or cscript) and ADO to do it (again, you
can find examples that would provide the basis for this on
msdn.microsoft.com).  Or use perl with the DBD:ODBC module if you know perl.

But the key would be to find out if you can get data out of AcuODBC quickly
because you know you can bcp/bulk insert it into the database quickly.

them
> > > "thanks - but no thanks".
> > >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

can
> > > script in Win2K to dump the contents of the data files using the xfd
> files
> > > provided. I could then import this ascii file extremely quickly into
SQL
> > > server.
> > >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

<a id="t-ec448b5933"></a>
## Thread: [OT] DOS recognition of PCMCIA card? — 13 this month / 14 total

### [OT] DOS recognition of PCMCIA card?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T10:15:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bngof5$s94$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

Good day... I've been asked to offer a bit of assisstance and it is beyond 
my knowledge; if someone can offer a pointer or two it would be 
appreciated.

A buddy o' mine is impressed with the way I do backups (as mentioned here
earlier I use a Jaz drive and do image-copies with HD98copy) and wants to
do something similar on his laptop.  The problem is... he has an external
SCSI Jaz drive which can attach either through the parallel port or 
through a PCMCIA-to-SCSI card.  When he boots to DOS and attaches the 
drive to the parallel port (using the Jaz traveller) the system will 
recognise the drive (using the Iomega Guest utility0 but transfer rates 
make backing up 10G of disk a weary, weary time.

If he attaches the drive using the PCMCIA card the Guest utility will not 
recognise it as existing; this is, I believe, because DOS does not 
recognise PCMCIA cards unless one does... something.

Is there anyone who can point me towards what this 'something' might be?  
I have searched on 'card and socket services' but it seems that this only 
directs me towards people who want to sell me software without any 
guarantees.

Thanks much!

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] DOS recognition of PCMCIA card?

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T12:54:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-ABE728.12540526102003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bngof5$s94$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
In article <bngof5$s94$1@panix1.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> Good day... I've been asked to offer a bit of assisstance and it is beyond 
> my knowledge; if someone can offer a pointer or two it would be 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It sounds like you need an MS-DOS mode driver.

DOS has parallel port support built in, but no PCMCIA support.  The 
manufacturer of the PCMCIA-to-SCSI card might have a DOS only driver.  
That would be a sure fix.

In the event that they do not have a DOS driver for the card, can you 
run the HD98COPY from a DOS window in Windows?  In that case it would be 
the Windows driver that would handle the interface with the card.

Finally, if a DOS driver is unavailable, and HD98COPY will not run under 
Windows, you might be able to set up a lightweight Linux disk with 
support for the cards that would do the image copy using different 
tools.  This could be as simple as a floppy with a kernel supporting the 
SCSI card and the DD utility (Device to Device copy).

Best bet is a DOS driver though...
```

down, push the PCMCIA card into the slot, connect the other end to the 
drive, insert this disk into drive A:, turn it back on, follow the 
directions and replace disks when it tells you.  When it says 'all done'

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] DOS recognition of PCMCIA card?

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-26T14:38:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-B2C374.14382326102003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bngof5$s94$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-ABE728.12540526102003@corp.supernews.com> <s9udnS06_77vgAGiU-KYvA@giganews.com>`

```
In article <s9udnS06_77vgAGiU-KYvA@giganews.com>,
 "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> > In article <bngof5$s94$1@panix1.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> use tools for Windows.

It sounds like that is not an option as Windows is unable to mount its 
partitions in read only mode.


> In no account use Linux, or, if you do, don't tell anyone. Linux is a cult
> and many governments deal harshly with cults (US vs. Branch Davidians,
> France vs. Scientology, China vs. Fauln Gong, etc.).

Linux isn't a cult, it is just a very useful operationg system that 
actually works on laptops and with things like PCMCIA cards.  It is also 
very lightweight.  

You could build a kernel, or get one from a standard Linux distro "boot 
disk".  Add support for DOS eumlation and your PCMCIA card.  Then write 
a very simple shell script, to be executed on boot:

   mount dos/win partition read-only
   start emulator
   execute HD98COPY
   prompt user to eject floppy and hit the "any" key
   reboot after keypress

This is actually starting to sound pretty easy.  Do we know the make and 
model of the laptop and the SCSI card?

 
> Besides, Linux may void the warranty that comes with Windows.

Windows has a warranty?  Does it cover the users pain and suffering or  
mental anguish?
```

DD

#### ↳ Re: [OT] DOS recognition of PCMCIA card?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote 

> Is there anyone who can point me towards what this 'something' might be?  
> I have searched on 'card and socket services' but it seems that this only 
> directs me towards people who want to sell me software without any 
> guarantees.

There are many very small distributions of Linux that will boot off a
floppy disk and run from RAM.  Several will recognise PCMCIA and will
access DOS and Windows file systems.  tar cvzf will back up all files
in a filesystem with compression which caters for recovery later.

See toms.net/rb or PeeWeeLinux.org or similar.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<1ghnb.71777$ox6.1030622@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

"Anurag" <anurag01@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:608f3c71.0310270620.4aa0a14a@posting.google.com...
> I want to know the industry productivity standards for Mainframe
> development projects using CICS and Cobol. In case it is not possible
> to get down to CICS/COBOL level, the productivity figures for
> Mainframe projects would do.

If you pay by the KLOC you get longer programs.

If you pay by reuse you get more programs.

If you pay hourly it takes longer.

If you outsource it costs 1/6 the amount, takes 3 times as long and doesn't
work.

JCE

<a id="t-dc24cfaf7f"></a>
## Thread: They took away COMPAREX!!! — 3 msgs

### They took away COMPAREX!!!

- **From:** studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer)
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T07:51:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0465864.0310270751.328232c1@posting.google.com>`

```
I am feeling pretty tied up because they decided to get rid of the
COMPAREX utility and are going to force us to use SuperC and FILE-AID
to do all of our compares.

Personally I don't like this much but I get to deal with the problem.

First off, all of our code is held in librarian (ELIPS).  And SuperC
won't access librarian modules.  I really don't want to go thru the
next XX years of my life copying down librarian source code modules to
a PDS just so I can do a comparex on them.  Is there a
IEBGENER/SORT/UDACS equivalent to copy librarian members to a PDS?? 
Or a way to get SuperC to compare a librarian file???

I would perfer to run these in batch mode so any JCL would be a
godsend as well.


And I don't think I will every understand why they would take away a
utiiity that is used so much.

blah blah blah
```

#### ↳ Re: They took away COMPAREX!!!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T16:37:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lRbnb.3884$Px2.2197@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0310270751.328232c1@posting.google.com>`

```
I won't comment on why this change would be good or bad, but there are two
facilities of (current) Librarian software that MIGHT make your conversion
easier:

 Comparator (and Comparator II) which is a "built-in" comparison utility

 Lib/AM - which provides the ability for other software to read Librarian
files AS IF they were PDS (thus SuperC) should work with them.

 Check with your Librarian support person to determine if you can use either
or both of these - and if so get the correct "training" on how to use them
to do what you want.

P.S. For those reading this thread, these are all IBM mainframe products:

  Comparex - from Serena
    http://www.serena.com/product/aa_comparex.html

   File-Aid - from Compuware
     http://www.compuware.com/products/fileaid/

  Librarian - from Computer Associates
    http://www3.ca.com/Solutions/Collateral.asp?CID=33827&ID=1347

 SuperC - from IBM

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ISPZU210/APPENDIX1.1
```

<a id="t-d5f978218d"></a>
## Thread: GOBACK after EXEC CICS RETURN — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [2003-08](2003-08.md#t-d5f978218d)._

#### ↳ Re: GOBACK after EXEC CICS RETURN

- **From:** Paul Robinson <postmaster@paul.washington.dc.us>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T17:42:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F9D58DF.94378999@paul.washington.dc.us>`
- **References:** `<3173934.1059718062@dbforums.com>`

```
Ace11 wrote:

> Respected Gentlemen,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>         I wud appreciate the responses.

Some compilers squawk bloody murder if you don't have either a STOP RUN or a
GOBACK statement in a program.  Putting a STOP RUN in a CICS application
would probably get you canned because (I think) it would terminate the
entire CICS partition if for some reason it accidentally got executed by
mistake.  A GOBACK probably would just get the application tossed for
violating something or might not cause a problem.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GOBACK after EXEC CICS RETURN

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T23:28:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-C8FB34.23281827102003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3173934.1059718062@dbforums.com> <3F9D58DF.94378999@paul.washington.dc.us>`

```
In article <3F9D58DF.94378999@paul.washington.dc.us>,
 Paul Robinson <postmaster@paul.washington.dc.us> wrote:

> Ace11 wrote:
> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> violating something or might not cause a problem.

GOBACK is totally valid in CICS, it returns to the calling program 
without terminating the active frame created by the last START / LINK / 
XCTL.

I almost think that STOP RUN would have to be in the reserved word list.  
I do know it will stop an IMS control region and CICS should be no 
different -- but I don't plan on testing it ;-)
```

## Thread: Microfocus Cobol Install ? — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus Cobol Install ?

- **From:** tippcity_1999@yahoo.com (billman)
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T10:33:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4961a3da.0310271033.7b5e26e8@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

How difficult is it to install Microfocus Cobol ? 

We have about 40 batch mainframe (IBM/MVS) cobol programs used for a
data conversion process that we want to run on Windows.  Only one
programmer works on, or even runs, these programs.

Microfocus says we should buy a week of consulting services from them
to cmoe out and install the product(s).  Is it really that difficult ?
 I would have thought it would be more like Microsoft's Visual suite,
which is fairly straightforward.

Thanks for your thoughts...
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus Cobol Install ?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T21:08:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F9CE023.40C87784@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<4961a3da.0310271033.7b5e26e8@posting.google.com>`

```


billman wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What happens when he leaves, or is he planning on hanging around until he
is 84 ?

>
> Microfocus says we should buy a week of consulting services from them
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for your thoughts...

Can't blame Micro Focus for trying to make a buck <G>. But you have
probably told them a lot more about the problem than you have told us.
Presumably it is Server Express you want to install ?

The actual install - probably no big deal - BUT - (a) One week of M/F
assistance at $x as opposed to (b) Your one guy at $x per hour for 'y'
weeks - and of course this is probably just an absorbed cost not
specifically posted to a Cost Centre ?

Your man - is he a 'hot shot' COBOL programmer or has the task been
delegated to a 'junior'. No doubt on the re-compiles there will be some
syntax errors which an experienced man can fathom - a junior might
flounder around a bit but eventually get there. The 40 programs - one
conversion suite or a series of separate conversions ?  M/F might have in
mind they can be re-designed, (without affecting the business logic),  to
be more easily automated in a Windows environment.

Possible plus if M/F get involved - with them on site your guy can throw a
host of questions at them about the product - which will give him tips and
quick shortcuts to features. (As he is the only COBOL developer - make
*sure* he documents what he finds out !).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus Cobol Install ?

- **From:** Kevin Hansen <khansen@screenio.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T22:01:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9j4rpv8gbbsckgvj72omt0piequrbd2aff@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4961a3da.0310271033.7b5e26e8@posting.google.com> <3F9CE023.40C87784@shaw.ca>`

```
On Mon, 27 Oct 2003 21:08:48 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>Can't blame Micro Focus for trying to make a buck <G>. But you have
>probably told them a lot more about the problem than you have told us.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
---------
All true, and I concur that paying for a little consulting time would
be a good investment.

We've seen lots of people move from mainframes to PCs.  The COBOL
isn't usually much of an issue, but if the developer in question has
never worked in a PC-based development environment, there are a lot of
other issues to get your arms around.

How do your organize your subdirectories and files?  How about (good)
backup procedures?  Setting up a rational testing environment?
Version control?  Linking?  Object libraries?

In the mainframe environment, this is all usually done for you by the
technical services department, but on the PC, it's your problem.  This
is -good- because you can now control your environment, but -bad-
because you need to learn and take care of a lot more stuff.

Once you've had a taste of how productive you can be on a PC COBOL
platform, you'll never look back.  I still remember what a revelation
it was back in 1985 when we discovered interactive debugging on a PC
COBOL.  Fabulous!  

Kevin
Develop Windows(tm) Applications - in COBOL!
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus Cobol Install ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T22:58:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mqhnb.4550$RQ1.3942@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<4961a3da.0310271033.7b5e26e8@posting.google.com>`

```
Well, ....

Are you looking at getting Net Express or Mainframe Express?

From you description of how it will be used, I would GUESS that it would be
Mainframe Express.  If so, the answer is it is EASY to install (most of its
parts) but medium-difficult to "configure" to get working as you want.

Some of the issues:

 - Do you want to "directly" access your mainframe data files or FTP them to
the PC?
 - How do you plan on keeping your mainframe and PC source files "in sync"
 - Have you looked at EBCDIC/ ASCII issues?
 - Have you looked at "compiler compatibility" issues?
 - Do you expect ISPF or "PC" type "look and feel" on the PC?

Now, if from your description, you actually expect to PORT your applications
from the mainframe to the PC and *never* use them again on the mainframe,
then I would expect you to be getting Net Express and not Mainframe Express.
This would be easier to "configure" but harder to actually get working (as a
production environment).

Bottom-Line:
  I would ask Micro Focus (not Microfocus) to provide you with a couple of
"customer references" that you can talk to about how their install went
(either with or without the one week training).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus Cobol Install ?

- **From:** tippcity_1999@yahoo.com (billman)
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T18:37:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Greetings again !  Thank you VERY MUCH for the replies.  This passle
of programs will be used to move two client off the mainframe and then
they will never be run again.

Answering the questions in order - 

What happens when he leaves - we have other people who are familiar
with the whole process and know cobol very well.

Presumably it is Server Express you want to install - wow, don't know
for sure.  We seek to take COBOL programs as text files from the
mainframe, compiling them on Windows, and running them on Windows. 
Also take data from one UNIX system and an MVS mainframe system and
put it on Windows.  There is no GUI when the program runs, no database
access, no cross-platform access of data.  All self contained at
runtime.

cost not specifically posted to a Cost Centre ? - It is, yes.

The 40 programs - one conversion suite or a series of separate
conversions ? - All are "batch", reading one to four flat files,
output one flat file.

How do your organize your subdirectories and files?  How about (good)
backup procedures?  Setting up a rational testing environment?
Version control?  Linking?  Object libraries? - All very good points,
this is where we thought more of the time would be spent (vs. product
installation).

Do you want to "directly" access your mainframe data files or FTP them
to the PC? - "FTP"
How do you plan on keeping your mainframe and PC source files "in
sync" ? - not necessary, we can start anew as it were.
Have you looked at EBCDIC/ ASCII issues? - Just started, this is the
biggest area of peril.
Have you looked at "compiler compatibility" issues? - I assume you
mean the comp/comp-3 etc. and other different interpretations of ANSI
- just started.
Do you expect ISPF or "PC" type "look and feel" on the PC? - A nicety
but not important.  Could be command line for all we care.

Thanks again, it would seem that your thoughts collectively indicate
that a straightforward install is not that big a deal, it's the other
million things that can go wrong.

<a id="t-9a228915e1"></a>
## Thread: Restructure this! — 20 this month / 41 total

_Thread started [2003-08](2003-08.md#t-9a228915e1)._

#### ↳ Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** Paul Robinson <postmaster@paul.washington.dc.us>
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T22:07:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F9D9701.B172BFBA@paul.washington.dc.us>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
Generally, using a *downward* GOTO in order to exit a section of code is
usually not harmful.

It is only *upward* GOTOs that are dangerous.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-27T21:41:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310272141.316c9f85@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <3F9D9701.B172BFBA@paul.washington.dc.us>`

```
Paul Robinson <postmaster@paul.washington.dc.us> wrote

> Generally, using a *downward* GOTO in order to exit a section of code is
> usually not harmful.
> 
> It is only *upward* GOTOs that are dangerous.

Neither GO TO is particularly 'harmful'.  When discovered in examining
code they are always very clear as to what they will do and when -
given they have been coded correctly.

It it the required label that it harmful. Or more especially the care
that must be taken around the label because of the complexity of flow
that is introduced by the use of GO TOs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T16:28:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%Ownb.6114$RQ1.2756@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <3F9D9701.B172BFBA@paul.washington.dc.us> <217e491a.0310272141.316c9f85@posting.google.com>`

```
If by "dangerous," one is talking about error-prone in maintenance, my
PERSONAL opinion, is that neither "downward" or "upward" Go To's are
"dangerous" - but it is the MIXTURE of the two that is particularly
dangerous - as is overlapping Go To's in the same direction, e.g.

  Para1.
        Perform something
        If A
           Go To Para3
         .
   Para2.
        Perform Something
        If B
            Go To Para4
         .
   Para3.
        How do you get here?
    Para4.
         When do you get here?
   Para5.
        And what about here?


***

Clearly, this type of "maintenance nightmare" can be AVOIDED by not having
any GO To's.  It can also be avoided in code with no "Go To Label"
statements (e.g. one DOES allow EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION).  However, it can
also be avoided by ONLY allowing "GO TO" of the very next paragraph
(section) label.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T15:11:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310281511.32a0a965@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <3F9D9701.B172BFBA@paul.washington.dc.us> <217e491a.0310272141.316c9f85@posting.google.com> <%Ownb.6114$RQ1.2756@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote

> Clearly, this type of "maintenance nightmare" can be AVOIDED by not having
> any GO To's.  It can also be avoided in code with no "Go To Label"
> statements (e.g. one DOES allow EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION).  However, it can
> also be avoided by ONLY allowing "GO TO" of the very next paragraph
> (section) label.

Any restriction in the type of constructs allowed will reduce the
problems encountered during maintenance.

In your (bad) example GO TOs may be to any label.  Thus to determine
the logic flow around a specific label it is necessary to examine the
whole program for references to that label, or to prior labels that
may result in dropping through the label, or to references that may
cause the logic flow to end at that label (such as PERFORM or THRU).

Reducing the ability to GO TO to only that of the next label (and
guaranteeing this has been followed) means that only the prior
paragraph need be examined plus any PERFORM or THRU that refers to
that prior paragraph, or the one under examination.

Reducing the abilty further by disalloing _any_ GO TO or THRU reduces
the problem even further by allowing the code to be grepped to
guarantee this is in fact the case, and that there can only be one way
the flow can exist around any label.

Of course some may argue that removing all GO TOs introduces
complexities such as 'excessive nesting of IFs' and other things they
are not used.

Each chooses the balance they are happy with and can cope with.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T16:09:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bnoooj$ma6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <3F9D9701.B172BFBA@paul.washington.dc.us> <217e491a.0310272141.316c9f85@posting.google.com> <%Ownb.6114$RQ1.2756@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```

On 28-Oct-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> Clearly, this type of "maintenance nightmare" can be AVOIDED by not having
> any GO To's.  It can also be avoided in code with no "Go To Label"
> statements (e.g. one DOES allow EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION).  However, it can
> also be avoided by ONLY allowing "GO TO" of the very next paragraph
> (section) label.

One step further is to disallow drop through code.  The first paragraph ends
with a goback or stop run, all other paragraphs are stand alone.   No perform
thru nor multi-paragraph sections.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T22:13:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FA1E161.E8889D03@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <3F9D9701.B172BFBA@paul.washington.dc.us> <217e491a.0310272141.316c9f85@posting.google.com> <%Ownb.6114$RQ1.2756@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bnoooj$ma6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3FA058EB.4CC134A1@mb.sympatico.ca> <217e491a.0310301407.48b201a1@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

> Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> EVALUATE.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> eliminated (perhaps replaced by IF .. GOTO .. IF .. GOTO ...) ?

Don't put words in my mouth.  I stand by what I posted.

>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> In what way were any examples 'frightful' and who was frightened ?

see below

>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or is this just a troll ?

Nope.
Here's an example:

_____________________________________________________________
Date: Tue, 15 May 2001 01:49:10 GMT
From: "Cliff Peterson" <cliffpeterson@spamcop.net>


EVALUATE TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE
  WHEN ADD-ACTION
   ALSO W-DRM-ORIG-PRODUCT EQUAL CC-DDA
   ALSO W-DRM-DRIVER-TYPE EQUAL CC-TB
   ALSO W-DRM-REL-PRODUCT EQUAL CC-DDA
   ALSO W-DRM-INSTRUCTION-CD EQUAL CC-A
    MOVE P-REL-ACCESS-ACTY TO TCB-ACTIVITY
    PERFORM CA000-CALL-PEM
    EVALUATE TRUE
      WHEN M-RESULT-T50000D NOT EQUAL DGR-OK  OR
           M-RESULT-U50810D NOT EQUAL DGR-OK
        CONTINUE
      WHEN OTHER
        EVALUATE TRUE
          WHEN M-DRC-TB-NO-REL-ACTS EQUAL CP-999
            MOVE ZERO TO W-DRM-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
            MOVE COND-CODE-REL-DRC TO TCB-USER-CC
            PERFORM EX000-CALL-CCP
            GO TO ZZ000-END-OF-PROCESSING
          WHEN OTHER
            MOVE M-DRC-TB-NO-REL-ACTS TO M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
            PERFORM UNTIL M-RESULT-U50812D EQUAL DGR-NO-FIND
                       OR M-RESULT-U50812D EQUAL DGR-END-DATA
              ADD +1 TO M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
              MOVE DGA-READ-KEY-EQ TO M-ACTION-U50812D
              MOVE P-REL-ACCESS-ACTY TO TCB-ACTIVITY
              PERFORM CA000-CALL-PEM
            END-PERFORM
            MOVE M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO TO W-DRM-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
        END-EVALUATE
    END-EVALUATE
  WHEN OTHER
    CONTINUE
END-EVALUATE
________________________________________________________________
Another was the original code posted in this thread.


You'd have to work very hard indeed to convince me that this is as well written as
possible, or that it's easy to understand.  That's my point: horrible code can be
produced using the EVALUATE.  And since posters to this thread have opined about
"maintenance nightmares" and that therefore this or that verb should be avoided - why
don't we determine ALL the verbs that produce bad code, and eliminate them all?  We
wouldn't have much left!

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-31T07:19:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-153BCB.07191831102003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <3F9D9701.B172BFBA@paul.washington.dc.us> <217e491a.0310272141.316c9f85@posting.google.com> <%Ownb.6114$RQ1.2756@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bnoooj$ma6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3FA058EB.4CC134A1@mb.sympatico.ca> <217e491a.0310301407.48b201a1@posting.google.com> <3FA1E161.E8889D03@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
In article <3FA1E161.E8889D03@mb.sympatico.ca>,
 Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> Here's an example:
> 
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
> wouldn't have much left!

While I agree that this is rather tough to read, I think that is more 
due to the authors lack of whitespace lines and lower case characters.  

I also think it looks like completely contrived code -- I can't imagine 
the most twisted of c-l-c-ers writting this unless they were indulging 
in a priviate obfusicated code contest.

That said, do you imagine the cooresponding IF would be simpler?  Lets 
look at it:

  IF ADD-ACTION
     AND W-DRM-ORIG-PRODUCT EQUAL CC-DDA
     AND W-DRM-DRIVER-TYPE EQUAL CC-TB
     AND W-DRM-REL-PRODUCT EQUAL CC-DDA
     AND W-DRM-INSTRUCTION-CD EQUAL CC-A
      MOVE P-REL-ACCESS-ACTY TO TCB-ACTIVITY
      PERFORM CA000-CALL-PEM
      IF M-RESULT-T50000D NOT EQUAL DGR-OK  OR
         M-RESULT-U50810D NOT EQUAL DGR-OK
          CONTINUE
      ELSE
         IF M-DRC-TB-NO-REL-ACTS EQUAL CP-999
            MOVE ZERO TO W-DRM-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
            MOVE COND-CODE-REL-DRC TO TCB-USER-CC
            PERFORM EX000-CALL-CCP
            GO TO ZZ000-END-OF-PROCESSING
         ELSE
            MOVE M-DRC-TB-NO-REL-ACTS TO M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
            PERFORM UNTIL M-RESULT-U50812D EQUAL DGR-NO-FIND
                       OR M-RESULT-U50812D EQUAL DGR-END-DATA
              ADD +1 TO M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
              MOVE DGA-READ-KEY-EQ TO M-ACTION-U50812D
              MOVE P-REL-ACCESS-ACTY TO TCB-ACTIVITY
              PERFORM CA000-CALL-PEM
            END-PERFORM
            MOVE M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO TO W-DRM-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
         END-IF
     END-IF
     ELSE
       CONTINUE
  END-IF

It still looks quite nasty.  The problem with this usage, and its 
structure, is that it truly is a series of nested IF's, at each node 
there is only a single condition to be tested.  Written with an eye for 
the human maintenance programmer it might look like this:

If ADD-ACTION
   and ( W-DRM-ORIG-PRODUCT = CC-DDA )
   and ( W-DRM-DRIVER-TYPE  = CC-TB  )
   and ( W-DRM-REL-PRODUCT  = CC-DDA )
   and ( W-DRM-INSTRUCTION-CD = CC-A )

      Move P-REL-ACCESS-ACTY to TCB-ACTIVITY
      Perform CA000-CALL-PEM

      Evaluate true

        when ( M-RESULT-T50000D not = DGR-OK )
             or ( M-RESULT-U50810D not = DGR-OK )

           continue

        when ( M-DRC-TB-NO-REL-ACTS = CP-999 )

           Move 0 to W-DRM-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
           Move COND-CODE-REL-DRC to TCB-USER-CC
           Perform EX000-CALL-CCP
           GO TO ZZ000-END-OF-PROCESSING

        when other

           Move M-DRC-TB-NO-REL-ACTS to M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
           Perform 
              until ( M-RESULT-U50812D = DGR-NO-FIND )
                 or ( M-RESULT-U50812D = DGR-END-DATA )

               Add +1 to M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
               Move DGA-READ-KEY-EQ to M-ACTION-U50812D
               Move P-REL-ACCESS-ACTY to TCB-ACTIVITY
               Perform CA000-CALL-PEM

            End-Perform
            Move M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO to W-DRM-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO

      End-Evaluate

End-If

It doesn't look nearly so frightening now, does it?
```

code

opined

> It doesn't look nearly so frightening now, does it?

Given that you just rewrote it in less that a day (I didn't check for
accuracy) it cannot be thought of nightmarish to begin with :-)
Of course I do wonder where that GO TO ZZ000-END-OF-PROCESSING goes...and I
sure hope that it does really "END PROCESSING" :-)

The conversion of the EQUALS to =  *REALLY* helps the most.  Seeing it here
makes it obvious that your brain is trained to look for operator symbols
versus text.

IF A-EQUAL EQUAL 'EQUAL'  looks weird but IF A-EQUAL = 'EQUAL'  almost looks
sensible.

JCE

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-10-31T19:08:05-06:00

```
Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote 

> > It is just another way of doing a case statement instead of using a
> > series of IF .. ELSE IF ... Are you saying that this structure should
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Don't put words in my mouth.  I stand by what I posted.

I wasn't, I was asking a question about you would propose to do
multiple alternate selection.  So how would you ?
 
> EVALUATE TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE
>   WHEN ADD-ACTION
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> Another was the original code posted in this thread.

While I may tend to just use IF for that particular case, it is just
another way of doing IF ( several conditions ) ELSE .. and no more
'frightening' than equivalent IFs. (though the GO TO in the middle is
a bit of a worry).

   IF ADD-ACTION
    AND W-DRM-ORIG-PRODUCT EQUAL CC-DDA
    AND W-DRM-DRIVER-TYPE EQUAL CC-TB
    AND W-DRM-REL-PRODUCT EQUAL CC-DDA
    AND W-DRM-INSTRUCTION-CD EQUAL CC-A
     MOVE P-REL-ACCESS-ACTY TO TCB-ACTIVITY
     PERFORM CA000-CALL-PEM
     IF M-RESULT-T50000D NOT EQUAL DGR-OK  OR
        M-RESULT-U50810D NOT EQUAL DGR-OK
         CONTINUE
     ELSE
         IF M-DRC-TB-NO-REL-ACTS EQUAL CP-999
             MOVE ZERO TO W-DRM-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
             MOVE COND-CODE-REL-DRC TO TCB-USER-CC
             PERFORM EX000-CALL-CCP
             GO TO ZZ000-END-OF-PROCESSING
         ELSE
             MOVE M-DRC-TB-NO-REL-ACTS TO M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
             PERFORM UNTIL M-RESULT-U50812D EQUAL DGR-NO-FIND
                        OR M-RESULT-U50812D EQUAL DGR-END-DATA
               ADD +1 TO M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
               MOVE DGA-READ-KEY-EQ TO M-ACTION-U50812D
               MOVE P-REL-ACCESS-ACTY TO TCB-ACTIVITY
               PERFORM CA000-CALL-PEM
             END-PERFORM
             MOVE M-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO TO W-DRM-REL-EXEC-SEQ-NO
         END-IF
     END-IF
 ELSE
     CONTINUE
 END-IF

Is this less frightening to you ?

Actually I do dislike the EVALUATE TRUE style

The 'ALSO' clauses allow the EVALUATE to be used directly as a
decision table, though it isn't used here as one.  This makes it a far
more useful contruct than a simple case statement.

> You'd have to work very hard indeed to convince me that this is as well written as
> possible, or that it's easy to understand.  That's my point: horrible code can be
> produced using the EVALUATE.

Horrible code can be produced using any statement or any language.
Removing the EVALUATE and forcing use of IF in the above would be a
direct replacement and would not make it more or less horrible.

You seem to want to blame the tool.  

>  And since posters to this thread have opined about
> "maintenance nightmares" and that therefore this or that verb should be avoided - 

Actually, you may find that I have been pointing out that it isn't the
statements or clauses (eg GO TO, NEXT SENTENCE) that should be avoided
but the poor structuring of code that should be avoided. 
Specifically, it is the use of labels (or anonymous labels) in
multiple ways that cause 'nightmares' (in my experience). As it
happens, when code is developed in a way that avoids the multiple uses
of labels, there is no place for GO TO or NEXT SENTENCE.

> why
> don't we determine ALL the verbs that produce bad code, and eliminate them 
> all?  We wouldn't have much left!

It isn't verbs that produce bad code, it is programmers.
```

Peter Lacey wrote:
>>>and judging by the frightful examples of its use posted
>>>in this NG it can EASILY cause "maintenance nightmares".
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> EVALUATE TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE

.shudder.

>   WHEN ADD-ACTION
>    ALSO W-DRM-ORIG-PRODUCT EQUAL CC-DDA
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>         EVALUATE TRUE

I don't remember the original context, but what this looks to me like is 
someone who vowed never to use an If statement.  :)  This is a great 
illustration of using the right tool for the job.  An Evaluate with only 
one valued When condition and a When Other is the exact same thing as an 
If statement (logically, at least - maybe not in object code).

> You'd have to work very hard indeed to convince me that this is as well written as
> possible, or that it's easy to understand.  That's my point: horrible code can be
> produced using the EVALUATE. 

And I would not let something like the above pass without a comment 
about maintainability.  I've used a grand total of 1 "Also" clause on an 
Evaluate statement, and that was when I was doing a matrix of values, 
the combination of which each having its own code.
```

<a id="t-ab1a99d595"></a>
## Thread: using call versus copy / include — 16 this month / 17 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### using call versus copy / include

- **From:** henry.weesie@nebus.nl (henry weesie)
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T01:25:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com>`

```
We are using CobolII in a idms mainframe.
I'm told that using copy-members should generate less cpu than using
subprograms and therefore the use of copy-members should be preferred
above the use of subprograms.

So far my employer uses copy statements to copy source into each
program. However they are open for other structures like using
call-statements.

I expect more advantages of using calls instead of copy's in respect
of maintenance. No compile of all programs using copy when source in
the object is changed, no separate declarations en subroutines when
using calls.
But much more expensive CPU?

I'd like to have a strong case in trying to set up some form of object
oriented programming in Cobol II.

What are your experiences in the use of calls versus copy's?
```

#### ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T12:56:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XHtnb.6176$P%1.4703295@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com>`

```
"henry weesie" <henry.weesie@nebus.nl> wrote in message
news:3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com...
> We are using CobolII in a idms mainframe.
> I'm told that using copy-members should generate less cpu than using
> subprograms and therefore the use of copy-members should be preferred
> above the use of subprograms.

Yes, Copy + perform  is more CPU than CALL <subprogram>, that's true..It's
also true that 72,128,875 is greater than 72,128,871.  That is, the
difference is barely measurable, assuming you are not doing CALL+CANCEL,
CALL+CANCEL., CALL+CANCEL many many times in a single program run.

> I expect more advantages of using calls instead of copy's in respect
> of maintenance. No compile of all programs using copy when source in
> the object is changed, no separate declarations en subroutines when
> using calls.

For these maintenance benefits alone when you have a common routine subject
to change, a dynamic CALL was, is and will continue to be my 'first choice.'
It's often not just recompiling, it's finding all the programs you must
recompile.

One other benefit.. if you do have a large procedure which is only called
once or twice per program, CALL+CANCEL frees the system resources used by
that called module when that module is no longer needed.

Bottom line, there is no one 'right' way to handle common code, it is
extremely application and environment specific; and since these methods are
NOT mutually exclusive, no reason you can't have one routine COPY'd (e.g.,
standard date arithmetic routines, which hardly ever change) and other
routines which do change periodically  (e.g., pricing or commission
calculation routines) residing in a callable module.
```

#### ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T06:58:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BzSdnQRpp9qO9QOiU-KYiA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com>`

```
henry weesie wrote:
> We are using CobolII in a idms mainframe.
> I'm told that using copy-members should generate less cpu than using
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> What are your experiences in the use of calls versus copy's?

The main program has to branch to either the copied code or a subprogram, so
I can't see it makes much difference. The increase in Linker time (for a
subprogram) is offset by the increase in compile time (for a copy member).
The difference in execution time has got to be almost unmeasurably small.
Further, if you change something in a subordinate module, you have to
recompile all the target programs if using a copy vs re-link all the target
programs when using a subprogram.

As with virtually all attempts at micro-efficiency, you've already probably
wasted more time on the issue than you'll probably ever retrieve. But, what
the heck, how hard could it be to benchmark the sucker? Kludge up a
prototype test, loop it 1000 (or 1 million) times and clock it.

I, personally, prefer subprograms. It 'black-boxes' the code, gets the code
out of the way and allows me to concentrate on the main driving program,
prevents fiddling with the fenced-off code, eases maintainability, and
decreases bugs.
```

#### ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T16:41:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l%wnb.6134$RQ1.1903@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com>`

```
Of course, as we all know <G>, IBM has dropped support for the VS COBOL II
compiler YEARS ago.  Therefore, let me provide some information from the
CURRENT "IBM Enterprise COBOL" performance tuning paper:

"Performance considerations using DYNAM with CALL literal (measuring CALL
overhead only):

On the average, for a CALL intensive application, the overhead associated
with the CALL using

     DYNAM ranged from 16% slower to 100% slower than NODYNAM.

Note: This test measured only the overhead of the CALL (i.e., the subprogram
did only a GOBACK);
thus, a full application that does more work in the subprograms is not
degraded as much."

but also

"Using CALLs

When using CALLs, be sure to consider using nested programs when possible.
The performance of a CALL to a nested program is faster than an external
static CALL; external dynamic calls are the slowest. CALL identifier is
slower than dynamic CALL literal. Additionally, you should consider space
management tuning (mentioned earlier in this paper) for all CALL intensive
applications.

With static CALLs, all programs are link-edited together, and hence, are
always in storage, even if you do not call them. However, there is only one
copy of the bootstrapping library routines link-edited with the application.

With dynamic CALLs, each subprogram is link-edited separately from the
others. They are brought into storage only if they are needed. However, each
subprogram has its own copy of the bootstrapping library routines
link-edited with it, bringing multiple copies of these routines in storage
as the application is executing.

Performance considerations for using CALLs (measuring CALL overhead only):

    CALL to nested programs was 50% to 60% faster than static CALL.
    Static CALL literal was 45% to 55% faster than dynamic CALL literal.
    Static CALL literal was 60% to 65% faster than dynamic CALL identifier.
    Dynamic CALL literal was 15% to 25% faster than dynamic CALL identifier.

Note: These tests measured only the overhead of the CALL (i.e., the
subprogram did only a GOBACK); thus, a full application that does more work
in the subprograms may have different results."

***

Note: NONE of this deals with the "ease" or "difficulty" in maintenance (and
re-testing).  It should also be noted that STATIC calls require link-editing
in object code.  Therefore, there is LIMITED advantages of static calls
(rather than dynamic calls) over NESTED programs and/or COPY procedures -
when it comes to maintenance issues.

 ***

For the full Performance Paper, see:

 http://www.ibm.com/support/docview.wss?rs=203&q=7001475&uid=swg27001475
```

<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

:>Performance considerations for using CALLs (measuring CALL overhead only):

:>    CALL to nested programs was 50% to 60% faster than static CALL.
:>    Static CALL literal was 45% to 55% faster than dynamic CALL literal.
:>    Static CALL literal was 60% to 65% faster than dynamic CALL identifier.
:>    Dynamic CALL literal was 15% to 25% faster than dynamic CALL identifier.

I find it interesting that dynamic call literal is faster than dynamic call
identifier, especially on the initial call.

If the address is cached and reused (for subsequent calls) it should be about
as fast as static call literal.

Is there an explanation?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T17:14:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Stxnb.5406$Px2.4996@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com> <l%wnb.6134$RQ1.1903@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <di8tpvs73gi3unagd7qj16iqv2iv72mj45@4ax.com>`

```
I don't know (and I don't think it is "documented") HOWEVER, it *might* be
that when you use the DYNAM compiler option, the run-time doesn't have to
"think about" which type of CALL each call is - while when you use NODYNAM
with CALL identifier, the run-time needs to "look at each" CALL to determine
whether it is dynamic or static.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T07:45:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-FA756E.07453229102003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com> <l%wnb.6134$RQ1.1903@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net> <di8tpvs73gi3unagd7qj16iqv2iv72mj45@4ax.com>`

```
In article <di8tpvs73gi3unagd7qj16iqv2iv72mj45@4ax.com>,
 Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

> On Tue, 28 Oct 2003 16:41:53 GMT "William M. Klein"
> <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is there an explanation?

Dynamic call identifier has the additional overhead of checking to see 
if the identifier value has changed and looking for a cached address of 
an already loaded module to reuse.

That is such a small operation compared to the dynamic call literal, 
which does not check the literal for change, but still has to resolve 
the address of the module if previously used.

The fact that such a tiny operation is 15% to 25% of the entire call 
ought to let us know that the concern about the time spent on any of it: 
(4 * tiny) to (6 * tiny) is really not worth the concern.
```

#### ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T09:13:26+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f9ecdff_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com>`

```
Henry,

the posts here are offering good advice and you can see the consensus is in
favour of CALL.

If you intend to go to OO COBOL you will obtain all the advantages of  a
dynamic calling environment without some of the disadvantages (see post from
Bill Klein).

Personally, if I couldn't use OO, I would (and have...) use a dynamic
calling environment. This provides the most flexibility and ease of
maintenance and (for me at least) the overheads have never been a problem.

(There is no need to even re-link applications when a module changes.
Instead you can spend the time on drawing up your regression testing
plan...<G>)

My own opinion on COPY (when used in the procedure division) is that it is
an abomination.

To me this is exactly the same as NOT using PERFORM for code that is used in
several places in a program.

There was a time (long, long, ago...) when it was necessary to do this
because there were few other options and a COPY ensured that common code
remained consistent across the programs that used it. In this day and age
that is just nonsense.

It also encourages the use of REPLACING to make it look like it was tailored
for a specific circumstance and is not REALLY just duplicate code....

A better solution is to write one set of generalised code and dynamically
call it.

Nested programs are a good solution if you are really worried about dynamic
CALL overheads.

(But you shouldn't be. Despite Bill's alarming 16 - 100% on CALL overhead,
you will never notice this. If something takes 10 milliseconds or 20, a
human certainly won't tell the difference. The only time when this MIGHT be
a consideration would be when processing in a batch environment, where you
are incurring the extra 10 milliseconds possibly millions of times. Usually
Batch jobs are running unattended and are expected to take a long time
anyway...)

Go with CALL. There is really NO argument to use Procedure includes/COPYs
any more.

The best solution of all is to use OO COBOL. You get the lot and you can
wrap your Classes into COM components and re-use them anywhere.

Pete.

"henry weesie" <henry.weesie@nebus.nl> wrote in message
news:3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com...
> We are using CobolII in a idms mainframe.
> I'm told that using copy-members should generate less cpu than using
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> What are your experiences in the use of calls versus copy's?
```

Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
> Henry,
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

No, true abominations are copies in the procedure division using 
REPLACING.  I am working with code that uses the same code for all 
files, and another 30 page chunk for each screen.  It is copied 
multitudinous times in each program, merrily changing all the paragraph 
names, half the code, and most of the data names each time it is copied. 
In many cases, a single word is replaced by an entire paragraph. The 
copy statements are half a screen long each, and the resultant code is 
enough to make you run screaming and crying back to unit record 
equipment.  May the programmer that wrote it rot in hell.

Donald
PS.  To make it worse, they decided to be fiendishly clever, and each 
field on each screen calls the entire screen routine over again 
re-cursively, until the exit button is hit.  Then it screams backwards 
out of the recursion, executing about 4 million lines of code for the 
single keystroke.  I think it was probably written by a computer science 
teacher.  I have better than two million lines of source, of which I'll 
bet 250,000 lines or so actually have anything at all to do with the 
actual system logic.
Donald
```

On 28-Oct-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> My own opinion on COPY (when used in the procedure division) is that it is
> an abomination.

I believe there's a place for this.   All of my IDMS programs have a standard
IDMS copy that does error checking after IDMS commands.   They get called a LOT,
and are always the same.

I have seen copied abort paragraphs, and even copied declaratives.  (plus a lot
of copied code that I agree is unwarranted or worse).

Not as useful - we used Y2K date conversion copy members.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-31T07:59:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hDoob.115005$ox6.1584909@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com> <3f9ecdff_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```
I use them because I have a nested module where there is a fixed
multidimensional array.

The problem is that the fixed sizes are different in each instance so I have
a copy replace SIZE-1 BY xx and SIZE-2 BY yy and SIZE-3 BY zz....  If I
chose standard linkage I would have to have occurs depending on with occurs
depending on with occurs depending on.

It might be an abomination but it was very efficient and people can now do
my very important function that is required always by just COPY replace.....

I'd be interested to know how else you could have done this in COBOL in a
manner that was as quick for people to pick up.  What's nice is that people
now can use this and get output that they are expecting and understand but
have no idea how it is working (in both the called module and the data they
are populating.  Just by following a setup/use/call which is almost all cut
and paste that is essentially creating a number of pointers that they later
use).

I must admit that I have also misused this capability - just not realizing
it at the time...now it's a pain to clean up.


JCE



"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f9ecdff_7@news.athenanews.com...

>
> the posts here are offering good advice and you can see the consensus is
in
> favour of CALL.
>
> If you intend to go to OO COBOL you will obtain all the advantages of  a
> dynamic calling environment without some of the disadvantages (see post
from
> Bill Klein).
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> To me this is exactly the same as NOT using PERFORM for code that is used
in
> several places in a program.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> It also encourages the use of REPLACING to make it look like it was
tailored
> for a specific circumstance and is not REALLY just duplicate code....
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nested programs are a good solution if you are really worried about
dynamic
> CALL overheads.
>
> (But you shouldn't be. Despite Bill's alarming 16 - 100% on CALL overhead,
> you will never notice this. If something takes 10 milliseconds or 20, a
> human certainly won't tell the difference. The only time when this MIGHT
be
> a consideration would be when processing in a batch environment, where you
> are incurring the extra 10 milliseconds possibly millions of times.
Usually
> Batch jobs are running unattended and are expected to take a long time
> anyway...)
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T18:59:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vpu47hajp3m74e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com>`

```
henry weesie wrote:

> We are using CobolII in a idms mainframe.
> I'm told that using copy-members should generate less cpu than using
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> What are your experiences in the use of calls versus copy's?

I can't speak to the overhead on an IBM mainframe, but in a statically 
linked executable, I would expect that a call might be a little more 
intensive than a perform, since the former is resolved during linking 
(when the object code is being tied together), while the latter is 
resolved at compile time (when the object code is actually being 
generated).  You'll also have a bit of overhead for each chunk of memory 
you're passing between the two.  The downside to this is that you still 
must re-link each executable when one of the subroutines changes.

That being said, I'm a big fan of calls instead of copybooks.  They 
better represent a componentized structure (or they can, if done right) 
by breaking the system into smaller, less-complex pieces.

If there is a way you can utilize dynamic linking, this has the 
advantage of not having to relink the executables; however, you then 
incur the CPU overhead to resolve the call each time it's called during 
each run.  Unisys mainframes have a means where you can statically link 
a vector to an installed "subsystem", which is loaded into memory the 
first time it's called, and merely executed after that.  This gives you 
the best of both worlds - the speed of a static link, with the ability 
to swap out modules out without having to re-link (provided the size and 
number of the parameters do not change).  I'm actually in the process of 
trying to convert some of our most commonly-executed programs into a 
scenario like this.
```

#### ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T16:13:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bnop05$nhj$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com>`

```
Any difference in efficiency can be important if the code is performed a million
times.   If it is used one time, then who cares about the overhead involved in
the call - other considerations are more important.

It is in between where judgment calls need to be made.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T08:30:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bnopuj$12bv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com> <bnop05$nhj$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
... And what is efficient in one implementation may entail disastrously high
overhead in another.  Know what's efficient and what's not on your
particular platform and your particular implementation of COBOL.

    -Chuck Stevens


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bnop05$nhj$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> Any difference in efficiency can be important if the code is performed a
million
> times.   If it is used one time, then who cares about the overhead
involved in
> the call - other considerations are more important.
>
> It is in between where judgment calls need to be made.
```

#### ↳ Re: using call versus copy / include

- **From:** jeff.lanam-nospam-@compaq-dot-com.not.net (Jeff Lanam)
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T21:18:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3fa17e7f.705004582@news.cpqcorp.net>`
- **References:** `<3501a0b5.0310280125.270858af@posting.google.com>`

```
On 28 Oct 2003 01:25:21 -0800, the evil clone of henry.weesie@nebus.nl
(henry weesie) emitted:

>We are using CobolII in a idms mainframe.
>I'm told that using copy-members should generate less cpu than using
>subprograms and therefore the use of copy-members should be preferred
>above the use of subprograms.


From the standpoint of a compiler writer, large and complex programs
are harder to generate optimal code for than small, straightforward
programs.  Whatever you gain in eliminating CALL instructions, you 
may lose because the compiler can't optimize the generated code.
Things like register spilling are more likely, while some
optimizations may be impossible.

Large programs are also more likely to expose compiler bugs.  You
are more likely to run into architectural limits such as the
maximum number of nested IF statements or PERFORM operations.
These of course vary from compiler to compiler.

Structure your code into managable, logical chunks using subprograms,
not copybooks.


Jeff Lanam  jeff.lanam at hp.com
COBOL for HP NonStop Systems
Hewlett-Packard
INCITS/J4 COBOL Committee member
```

## Thread: Character Based UI Design Suggestions — 12 msgs

### Character Based UI Design Suggestions

- **From:** ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T11:16:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0310281116.548b4053@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm looking for opinions regarding the best method of designing and
implementing a character based user interface. Here is some background
info:

OS       - HP UX 11i
COBOL    - MF Object Cobol 4.2
DATABASE - Oracle 9i


I am working with an extremely large existing application (which I
inherited responsibility for) an I am unhappy with how this
application handles the user interfaces, so I'd like to solicit
opinions on possible better approaches.

Here is a snip of psuedo-code for one of our very basic
add/change/delete application on the existing system:

LOOP

  GATHER DATA FOR DISPLAY

  DISPLAY SCREEN-1

  DISPLAY SCREEN-1B

  ACCEPT SCREEN-1C

  EVALUATE USER KEY PRESS

  ACT APPROPRIATELY

END-LOOP

SCREEN-1 is a 1920 byte 01 level data item in the working storage
section, and SCREEN-1B and SCREEN-1C simply redefine SCREEN-1. The
logic behind this is so that different elements of the screen can be
display in varying colors.

This is very cumbersome, as EVERY program that requires user
interaction now needs to carry the display logic, as well as the user
keypress evaluations, etc.

I'd like to separate things into a more "tiered" approach, but this
being my first real chance to venture into this area, I'm not sure the
best way to start out.

Any suggestions/recommendations/pointers would be greatly appreciated.

I've looked into MF's Dialog and Screens utilities, but they are
extremely cumbersome to deal with - I'd like to avoid them if
possible.

Thanks in advance!

Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: Character Based UI Design Suggestions

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T20:43:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F9E2BA7.DF8F9AD@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0310281116.548b4053@posting.google.com>`

```


Chris wrote:

> I'm looking for opinions regarding the best method of designing and
> implementing a character based user interface. Here is some background
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> Chris

1. Not sure on this - but Bob Wolfe at Flexus will have to confirm - I
*think* his SP2 software allows you to go character based. The Screen biz
is "tiered" - you call SP2 from your COBOL programs. (???)

2. Judson McClendon is a Screen Section guru using M/F - suggestions
Judson ?

3. From the way you've described it, looks like you've got your Screen
Section in the 'Business Logic' program. Hive off Screen Section as
separate programs that you can call with entry points - to get different
layouts for the same screen. In a sort of hierachial way, have a
Screen1.cbl which could in turn call ScreenAttributes.cbl to set say
colours. To get full advantage of the Mouse in character based - you would
need to take a look at Admouse.cbl (If you haven't got it, the text for
that is in Programming -> Character Based Interface for N/E V 4.0 - see
http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/nx40indx.htm).

Using Dialog System in GUI mode of course means a learning curve, and you
have got to get into Window event handling. But I note the above
referenced books refer to Character Based use of Dialog system - do you
have that in your M/F O V 4.2 ?

One thought - did the original designers go 'colour mad' ? Might simplify
things if you reduce colour, using it very selectively to highlight -
against that of course the users might moan, "Where are all our pretty
colours ?"

I don't think Screen Section is quite as cumbersome as you suggest - years
ago I had menu bars and drop-down menus designed in Screen Section - using
as the basis the M/F program Logper.cbl. (The text for the menus was held
in an ISAM file). Going back even further - to Radio Shack Model II days -
where you only had white on black - I was using box characters with
RM/COBOL to pretty-up a screen so that the display looked like a form.

Regardless of tools - the real success will come from a three-tiered
system approach - Business Logic, UI(User Interface), DBI(Database
Interface  - COBOL files or DBMS).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Character Based UI Design Suggestions

- **From:** ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2003-10-28T19:23:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0310281923.77ce9434@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0310281116.548b4053@posting.google.com> <3F9E2BA7.DF8F9AD@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3F9E2BA7.DF8F9AD@shaw.ca>...
> Chris wrote:
> 
…[93 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB


Jimmy,

Thanks for the suggestions. I agree that the three tier approach is
best, but never having actually implemented it, I'm curious as to
design pointers based on people's experiences with it.

Sorry if I wasn't clear, but there actually is NO Screen Section in
any of these programs. SCREEN-1 is an 01 level item defined in the
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION of the code. That's why its so cumbersome.
Every single screen is displayed bvy executing the following sequence:

DISPLAY SCREEN-1
  FOREGROUND-COLOR 7
  BACKGROUND-COLOR 1
  HIGHLIGHT.

DISPLAY SCREEN-1B
  FOREGROUND-COLOR 4
  BACKGROUND-COLOR 1
  HIGHLIGHT.

ACCEPT SCREEN-1C
  FOREGROUND-COLOR 3
  BACKGROUND-COLOR 1
  PROMPT "_"
  HIGHLIGHT.

This is done so that literal (fixed) data can be displayed white on
blue, non-editable data values are yellow on blue, and editable data
values are cyan on blue.

It gives a very nice appearance, but for every screen, you've got to
have a 1920 byte 01 level with two 1920 byte "redefinitions" to get
things to display properly. This get especially cumbersome when you
have multiple screens per program. It gets even more tedious when you
want to vary which fields are editable based on a user's access level
(or something equivalent).

Yes - your assumption is correct - all of the application logic is
contained within each "module" or sub-program: they are all
responsible for their own business logic, user i/o and data access.

I'd like to avoid 3rd party products where possible, as life is
difficult enough simply dealing with MF and their tech support
department (especially having to go through HP first).

I don't have a problem using the Screen Section - and I'm actually
very familiar with it from a previous job. My question is more toward
the application architecture itself.

For example: which part of the application is in the "driver's seat"?
I'm assuming it's the user interface, and any business logic and/or
data access should be driven by actions the user is taking.

Thanks again for the direction on this!

Chris
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Character Based UI Design Suggestions

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T19:23:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F9F6A94.B0EE7C37@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0310281116.548b4053@posting.google.com> <3F9E2BA7.DF8F9AD@shaw.ca> <8cc07162.0310281923.77ce9434@posting.google.com>`

```


Chris wrote:

> Jimmy,
>
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> Chris

The only way to illustrate pointers on three-tier is a simple example. Now you've got me
going back to the drawing board to get a handle on SCREEN SECTION again.. Give me a bit of
time and I'll code a simple example, (including SQL), plus I have never used entry points,
so I'll have to figure those out too <G>

One immediate comment on your 01 Level Working Storage 'screen entries'. *IF* currently a
particular screen is being used in several programs - then make it a copy file. Now as to
the colouring - check the Screen syntax - your colour can be a  :-

Background Color - General Format

 BACKGROUND-COLOUR  IS   integer-1
 BACKGROUND-COLOUR  IS   identifier-1 .

So you can specify it as you have - or use an indentifier to move. I haven't tried it yet,
but something like :-

--------------
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

copy "mycolors.cpy".
*>------------- mycolors.cpy----------------
01 MyBackground            pic 9(02).
01 MyForeground            pic 9(02).

01 MyBlack                     value 6. (I *think*)
01 MyRed                        value ?
01 MyWhite                     value ?
*>---------------------------------------------------
Following syntax is not correct, but you'll get the idea :-

SCREEN SECTION.
copy "ascreen.cpy".
*>------------------ ascreen.cpy-------------------
01 AScreen foreground-color MyForeground
                   background-color MyBackground.
     05 etc........
*>--------------------------------------------------

Procedure Division.

move MyRed to MyForeground
move MyWhite to MyBackground
display Ascreen

*> with a three-tier approach this becomes something like -
*> assuming you already have the Screen program loaded :-

CALL 'DisplayIt'  using MyForeground, MyBackground
--------------------------------------------------

As you can see from his 'unashamed plug' <G> - Bob's SP2 is very flexible .I should also
have mentioned Kevin Hansen's Norcomm Screen-IO - but I don't know whether he does
Character Screens - Kevin confirm please ?.

I take your point about not using third party - the screens are already functioning and you
would have to sit down and re-design and then go through testing.

Now there is one other possibility - I don't know your product but it is OO. Check out the
OO examples for Net Express at :-

http://www.syntax.gr/departments/CobolSolutions/files/downloads/demos.htm

Hopefully Net Expres, using the same syntax you have,  is an M/F enhancement of what is
available in your OO COBOL. Rather than using entry points to CALL you could invoke classes
for Screen Programs and SQL Programs - nothing fancy,  just use handles (object references)
to get at methods in the classes (you invoke a method as opposed to CALLing using an entry
point). Let me know the score on that one - and I could illustrate.

Incidentally Donald Tees up here in Toronto uses Flexus SP2 (Character mode) with Fujitsu
OO and has he is currently dabbling with N/E V 3.0 - he is probably doing the same with M/F
OO.

One thing to be very careful on - you are concentrating on flexibility for Screen handling,
(at the moment), and the possibility of getting into a three-tier - before you know where
you are, you finish up with a whole daffy of changes to your existing application - that in
turn may take your thought processes down the route to changing logic - Watch  it ! You
finish up with a complete revamp of the application !

So for starters, evaluate how far you want to go - then make a judgement call on your
approach.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
