###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Character Based UI Design Suggestions

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T15:18:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310291518.f4b6f2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0310281116.548b4053@posting.google.com> <3F9E2BA7.DF8F9AD@shaw.ca> <8cc07162.0310281923.77ce9434@posting.google.com>`

```
ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris) wrote

> Sorry if I wasn't clear, but there actually is NO Screen Section in
> any of these programs. SCREEN-1 is an 01 level item defined in the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> ACCEPT SCREEN-1C ...
 
> This is done so that literal (fixed) data can be displayed white on
> blue, non-editable data values are yellow on blue, and editable data
> values are cyan on blue.

This works because the elementary _named_ items only are displayed and
not the FILLERs.  Presumably Screen-1 is PIC X(1920) and the others
are redefinitions with named fields where appropriate.

I have used this mechanism since the late 70s with CIS Cobol, though I
had to devise my own colouration mechanism.  Over the years I
developed code generators to create the screens and to generate blocks
of code to do the display and accepts and data moves.  The input is a
text file that has the screen layout with variable fields tagged with
'%' characters.  A list of field descriptors in the file is matched
sequentially with the tags and each field is thus given a name, its
origin, a type, and a prompt (displayed in line 25) plus some other
stuff.

In the example the 'order' is the relative order that the fields will
be accepted as I accept each field separately. 0 is display only. 
'Paragraph' is the optional paragraph name to be performed to validate
the field.

*PREFIX
PM-
*FIELDS
- screenfield,order,movefield,type,paragraph,text
IKEY,0,PKEY
IGROUP,1,PGP,,PROC-GROUP,Product Group code - Scan for help. F6 to
delete product
DGROUP,0
IDIM,29,PDIM,,,Non-Dimishing - Use 'Y' for non stock items
TAG,29,PTag,0,,Tag is 01 to 12
IEQUIV,2,PEQV,,,Substitute can only be entered if Group is $$
IUNIT,2,PUNI,,,Unit
ICOMP,30,PCOMP,,,Component parts
IPCP,30,PPCP,,,Proprietry parts Y/N
IBARCODE,2,PBARCODE,,PROC-BarCode,Barcode of product
ILABELS,2,PLABELS,,,Labels may be produced for products when received
ISUPPRESS,31,PSUPPRESS,,,Suppress product on disk catalogue Y/N
ISERIAL,31,PSerialNo,,,Requires Serial Numbers Y/N
IDESC,2,PDESC,,,Description for Invoices
IINDENT,32,PIndent,,,Product is only available on indent
INLA,32,PNLA,,PROC-NLA,No Longer Available. Disallows back orders
ID2USE,2,PD2USE,,,Use supplentary description in HTML. Y/N
IDESC2,2,PDESC2,,,Supplentary description
ICATSORT,34,PCAT,,,Catalouge sort key
IFACTOR,6,PFACTOR,3,,Freight factor for calculating costs
ICAT2,34,PCAT2,,,Catalouge sort key 2
ICAT3,34,PCAT3,,,Catalouge sort key 3
ICAT4,34,PCAT4,,,Catalouge sort key 4
ISUPP,6,PSUPP,,PROC-SUPP,Supplier code - Scan for help
DSUPP,0
ISECTION,35,PCAT-SECTION,,,Catalogue section
IPAGE,36,PCAT-PAGE,,,Catalogue page
IVPART,6,PVENDOR-NO,,,Part number used by supplier
IGRAPHIC,36,PGRAPHIC,,,Graphic GIF file name for output to HTML
IVDESC,6,PVENDOR-DESC,,,Description used by supplier
IAREA,37,PSTK
IVPRICE,6,PSUPRICE,2,,Suppliers price in foreign currency
IVCURR,10,PCURRENCY,,PROC-CURR,Currency code - scan for help
DCURR,0
IMIN,38,PMIN,0,,Minimum Total Stock
IPPER,10,PPPER,0,,Number in Suppliers price (eg per 100)
IORIGIN,10,POrigin,,PROC-ORIGIN,Country of Origin of Goods
DORIGIN,0
IMAX,38,PMAX,0,,Maximum Total Stock
ICOST,10,PCOST,2,,Last landed cost price $NZ
IAVECOST,10,PAVECOST,2,,Average Valuation cost $NZ
IBIN1,38,PBIN(1),,,Main branch bin location
ITAX,10,PTAX,,PROC-TAX,GST Tax code - normally 1
DTAXRATE,0
IDUTY,11,PDUTY-RATE,1,,NZ Duty Rate %
IBIN2,39,PBIN(2),,,Branch 2 bin location
ITRADE,12,PTRADE,2,Proc-Trade,Trade price
IRETAIL,13,PRETAIL,2,,Retail Price
IBIN3,40,PBIN(3),,,Branch 3 bin location
ISPECIAL,14,PSPECL,2,,Special Price
ICOSTPLUS,14,PCOSTPLUS,1,,Cost Plus override
IBIN4,41,PBIN(4),,,Branch 4 bin location
IAPPLY,15,PRINGYN,,PROC-APPLY
IBIN5,42,PBIN(5),,,Branch 5 bin location
IQTY3,16,PRQTY3,0
IQTY2,17,PRQTY2,0
IQTY1,18,PRQTY1,0
IBIN6,43,PBIN(6),,,Branch 6 bin location
IRING3,19,PRING3,2
IRING2,20,PRING2,2
IRING1,21,PRING1,2
IBIN7,44,PBIN(7),,,Branch 7 bin location
IDISC,22,PDISC,
IBIN8,45,PBIN(8),,,Branch 8 bin location
IDISC0,23,PDISC0,0
IDISC1,24,PDISC1,0
IDISC2,25,PDISC2,0
IDISC3,26,PDISC3,0
IDISC4,27,PDISC4,0
IDISCP,28,PDISCP,0
IBIN9,46,PBIN(9),,,Branch 9 bin location
*SCREEN
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Character Based UI Design Suggestions

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T13:48:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f8gvpv86hr6jh9kj8pl7u9edep6ag27kns@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0310281116.548b4053@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0310281515.6c9ef022@posting.google.com> <8cc07162.0310281925.24cca849@posting.google.com>`

```
ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris) wrote:

>riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0310281515.6c9ef022@posting.google.com>...
>> ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris) wrote
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>Chris

Chris:

I've been following this thread with interest.

[warning, shameless commercial plug to follow]

COBOL sp2 does indeed support character mode screens in HP-UX/Micro
Focus COBOL environment.......but it will also give you many more
options for future enhancements.

If you use COBOL sp2 for your character mode screens, your program can
also run in a 2-tier or 3-tier client server architecture with no
change to the screen handling source code or the screen definitions.

We offer a Thin Client product which allows you to run your COBOL
program on the HP-UX machine and display the sp2 screens in 100%
Microsoft Windows GUI across a TCP/IP network.  This allows you to
easily migrate your application to an Internet/intranet GUI
client/server architecture with little or no changes.

If you prefer, we also offer a Web Client product which allows you to
continue to run your COBOL program on the HP-UX machine and display
the sp2 screens in a web browser on remote workstations across the
TCP/IP network.  The Web Client product now supports the ability to
display the user interface screens using Macromedia Flash forms.
Flash is highly efficient for web browser-based data entry/inquiry
forms.  In addition, Flash data entry/display forms have a very modern
GUI appearance.    This allows you to easily migrate your application
to an Internet/intranet GUI web browser display architecture with
little or no changes.

Another advantage is the fact that your COBOL application becomes far
more COBOL compiler independent.  The COBOL sp2 screen handling source
is transparent across all major COBOL compilers.  You avoid getting
your self locked in to a screen handling solution which is proprietary
to a specific COBOL compiler.

We also offer lots more solutions for Windows printing, e-mail support
from COBOL, access to many Windows API functions from COBOL,
management of multiple windows and many other modern capabilities.

...so the bottom line is that sp2 can not only serve your character
mode screen requirements today, it can support possible future plans
for multi-tier architecture, GUI screen handling, Internet/intranet
support, e-mail support, Windows printing and lots of other features
which you may wish to add later.  You don't have to make any
significant code changes to your sp2 screen handling source or screen
definitions to enhance your application for your future plans.

Contact me by personal e-mail if you have additional questions or
would like to evaluate.  PLEASE note that you will need to modify my
e-mail reply address when you reply.

Thanks.

[end of shameless commercial plug]



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-0f1000b6f5"></a>
## Thread: Getting SQL Row to Slow (Power x SQL) — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Getting SQL Row to Slow (Power x SQL)

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T19:42:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3538911.1067474565@dbforums.com>`

```

HI , i have routine where the use  type an specific date

and i  get  all  rows   one  by one and  shows all

field  in a forms.

Using other  fields  works fine  , but using  Date field

defined in SQL  as Smalldatetime  is too Slow and  sometimes i get
Status  S1T00 (Timeout Expired)



does  any body  knows  why ?



tks

Carlos Lages



by the way, this routine was made to get one by one

always getting the Next  record



           EXEC  SQL

            SELECT top 1

             CAST(REPLACE(YEAR(tit_dtemissao), ' ', 0) +

                  REPLACE(STR(MONTH(tit_dtemissao), 2), ' ', 0) +

                  REPLACE(STR(DAY(tit_dtemissao), 2), ' ', 0)   +

                  REPLACE(STR(tit_numero, 6), ' ', 0) +

                  REPLACE(STR(tit_desd, 2), ' ', 0)  AS
                  NUMERIC(16,0) ) , *

             INTO

                   :sql-chavedata , :regtit

             FROM   Teste.dbo.titulo WITH (NOLOCK)

             WHERE

             CAST(REPLACE(YEAR(tit_dtemissao), ' ', 0) +

                  REPLACE(STR(MONTH(tit_dtemissao), 2), ' ', 0) +

                  REPLACE(STR(DAY(tit_dtemissao), 2), ' ', 0)   +

                  REPLACE(STR(tit_numero, 6), ' ', 0) +

                  REPLACE(STR(tit_desd, 2), ' ', 0)  AS NUMERIC(16,0) )

                       > :sql-chavedata  and

                          tit_empresa  = :sql-empresa

            ORDER  BY  tit_dtemissao, tit_numero, tit_desd

           END-EXEC
```

#### ↳ Re: Getting SQL Row to Slow (Power x SQL)

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-29T21:25:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vq115l9f1qt1ea@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3538911.1067474565@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3538911.1067474565@dbforums.com>`

```
Carlos lages wrote:
> HI , i have routine where the use  type an specific date
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Status  S1T00 (Timeout Expired)

Just guessing - look at the indexes on the table.  Is there one on that 
table?  Try the query in a different tool and see if it still takes a 
long time.  If SQL Enterprise Manager (or Access, or whatever you're 
using) does it quickly but the COBOL takes a long time, maybe one of the 
more experienced PowerCOBOL folks can help.  But, if it's still slow, it 
may be an index problem.  Are there a lot of rows in this table?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Getting SQL Row to Slow (Power x SQL)

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T05:25:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3540145.1067509514@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3538911.1067474565@dbforums.com> <vq115l9f1qt1ea@corp.supernews.com>`

```

THIS  table  has  already an index  and has  about  45.000

records.



tks



i am working on it
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Getting SQL Row to Slow (Power x SQL)

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T15:44:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7laob.81372$Go5.1379188@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3538911.1067474565@dbforums.com> <vq115l9f1qt1ea@corp.supernews.com> <3540145.1067509514@dbforums.com>`

```
45,000 rows is not many and shouldn't take *too* long.
A more *specific* question...is that COLUMN indexed?

JCE

We are converting from Compaq Cobol to MicroFocus Cobol and are having
problems compiling.

All of our source starts in  CC 1, for some reason the MicroFocus compiler
doesn't like continuations to start in CC 1. (This is valid source inserted
by the precompiler) The code compiles clean under the old version but the
new one justs gives us grief. I am assuming that I am missing a directive
that allows the source to begin in CC 1.

Can any one help? Below is sample output from the compile.

Thanks,
Dan

> Here are messages that displayed when I compiled the test program.
>
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>

#### ↳ Re: Compiler Directives?

- **From:** "Steve Rainbird" <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T09:18:50+00:00

#### ↳ Re: Compiler Directives?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T10:06:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0310301006.571d8c81@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vq1gsbe1kmk1da@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Dan" <deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com> wrote 

> All of our source starts in  CC 1, for some reason the MicroFocus compiler
> doesn't like continuations to start in CC 1.

As I understand it, if you have freeform source then you don't need,
and can't have, continuations.  As freeform is an extension in '85
Cobol then any implementation can do this differently.

> (This is valid source inserted by the precompiler) 

You may need to have directives in the source to turn freeform off and
on.

> > * Accepted - sourceformat'free'

You seem to have it set for freeform.

> > -    "D  ,C.FIRST_NAME  ,C.MIDDLE_INITIAL  ,C.LAST_NAME  ,C.STREET
> > **    First character of a continued literal not a quote. Quote
> > assumed.

But continuations do not use '-' when using freeform (AFAIK).
```

recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it?

Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more

<a id="t-395a4eaf9f"></a>
## Thread: Compiler OPtions and CBL Statement — 2 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Compiler OPtions and CBL Statement

- **From:** akent29@comcast.net (Arthur M. Kent)
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T15:09:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akent29-3010030711570001@12.242.180.227>`

```
I need to compile a VS-COBOL program which contains some calls to
Assembler modules.  The organization I work for has setup COBOL "Processor
Groups", which are a short hand way of stating Compiler Options. 
Unfortuantely, the processor group which had been used before for this
program now "invokes" the compiler options RMODE=31 and AMODE=ANY.  What I
need are the options RMODE=ANY and AMODE=24.

I understand there is a "CBL (Process)" statement which can be inserted
before the IDENTIFICATION DIVISION sttaement which can accomplis this, but
I am unsure of the exact syntax.  Would it be:

   CBL (AMODE=24, RMODE=ANY)

Any advice/assitance etc. would be appreciared.
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiler OPtions and CBL Statement

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attgloabl.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T10:37:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3FA15A59.AB3CD553@attgloabl.net>`
- **References:** `<akent29-3010030711570001@12.242.180.227>`

```
I think you are getting Compiler options and Program Binder (Linkage Editor)
options mixed up.

The relevant COBOL compiler options are DATA(24) or DATA(31), RENT or NORENT,
and RMODE(AUTO) or RMODE(24) or RMODE(ANY).  These determine what AMODE and
RMODE are sent to the Program Binder.

If your "processor group" truly specifies AMODE(ANY) and RMODE(31) to the
Program Binder, you may have a hard time changing it, and you didn't supply
enough information for us to help you.  If these settings are a result of
COBOL compiler options, you can use the CBL statement to override the settings
(unless your shop has designated the options as non-overrideable).

I prefer to write CBL (which can also be specified as PROCESS, and is shown
under that heading in the Programming Guide) starting in the normal COBOL
columns - say, column 8.  You would code:

CBL DATA(24), RMODE(24)

That is all it takes....

"Arthur M. Kent" wrote:

> I need to compile a VS-COBOL program which contains some calls to
> Assembler modules.  The organization I work for has setup COBOL "Processor
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any advice/assitance etc. would be appreciared.
```

## Thread: Cobol85 Problem — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Cobol85 Problem

- **From:** Greg Good <member46468@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T20:28:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3543767.1067563715@dbforums.com>`

```

I've just downloaded Cobol85.  I am able to compile link and execute.  I
wrote a program that reads a file with records 1200 bytes long.  I can
read the records and write them to an output file ok.  When I try to
break the input record down in working storage I am getting bad results.
The data in working storage is loosing the data on the right end of the
field at the rate of 1 character per record.  Anybody have any ideas?



The Program:

000010 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

000020  PROGRAM-ID. Chk810tst.

000030*

000040 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

000050 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

000060 FILE-CONTROL.

000070     SELECT 810-FILE

000080     ASSIGN       TO  infile

000090     ORGANIZATION IS  SEQUENTIAL.

000100

000110     SELECT 810fix-FILE

000120     ASSIGN       TO  OUTFILE

000130     ORGANIZATION IS  SEQUENTIAL.

000140*

000150 DATA DIVISION.

000160 FILE SECTION.

000170 FD  810-FILE.

000180 01  810-RECORD                pic x(1201).

000190

000200

000210 FD  810fix-FILE.

000220 01  810fix-RECORD             pic x(50).

000230

000240 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

000250 01  recid-hold                pic x(5).

000260 01  rec-count                 pic 9(5).

000270 01  HdrRec-Info               pic x(50).

000280

000290 01  wk-RECORD.

000300     02  wk-HdrRec-info.

000310       03  wk-recid            pic x(5).

000320       03  filler              pic x(45).

000330     02  filler                pic x(1151).

000340

000350 PROCEDURE DIVISION.

000360      OPEN INPUT  810-fILE.

000370      OPEN OUTPUT 810fix-FILE.

000380      move 0 to rec-count.

000390*

000400 Read-input.

000410      READ 810-FILE AT END GO TO End-Prog.

000420      move 810-record to wk-record.

000430      move wk-HdrRec-info to 810fix-record.

000440

000450      WRITE 810fix-record.

000460      add 1 to rec-count.

000470      if rec-count > 10 then

000480        go to end-prog

000490      end-if.

000500

000510      GO TO Read-input.

000520*

000530 End-Prog.

000540      CLOSE  810-FILE 810fix-FILE.

000550 END PROGRAM Chk810tst.

000560



Help Please,

Greg Good
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol85 Problem

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-30T21:39:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vq3mc1pifodm06@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3543767.1067563715@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3543767.1067563715@dbforums.com>`

```
Greg Good wrote:
> I've just downloaded Cobol85.  I am able to compile link and execute.  I
> wrote a program that reads a file with records 1200 bytes long.  I can
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> field at the rate of 1 character per record.  Anybody have any ideas?
...
> 000170 FD  810-FILE.
> 
> 000180 01  810-RECORD                pic x(1201).

If this is actually reading the file byte by byte, and the records are 
1200, the FD is your problem.  The first read would get the 1200 bytes 
from record 1, and the first byte from record 2.  Then, the 2nd read 
would get bytes 2-1200 of record 2, and 2 bytes of record 3; the 3rd 
read gets 3-1200 of record 3 and 1-3 of record 4; you get the idea. 
Since you're only writing the first 50 bytes of the record, it's 
gradually shifting over.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol85 Problem

- **From:** "Dan" <deacondan25@N.O.S.P.A.M.hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-31T02:01:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vq45ln2hd3re14@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3543767.1067563715@dbforums.com>`

```

"Greg Good" <member46468@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3543767.1067563715@dbforums.com...
>
> I've just downloaded Cobol85.  I am able to compile link and execute.  I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> field at the rate of 1 character per record.  Anybody have any ideas?

I seem to remember having this problem when I converted to Unix. You seem to
be trying to account for the LF character at the end of the record.
Change
01  810-RECORD                pic x(1201).
back to
01  810-RECORD                pic x(1200).

and use
    SELECT FILE-NAME    ASSIGN TO 'filename'
       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

Instead of Organization is Sequential.

If I remember correctly, that's all we had to do.

--Dan
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol85 Problem

- **From:** Greg Good <member46468@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-31T11:52:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3546003.1067619134@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3543767.1067563715@dbforums.com>`

```

Dan,

Thank you for your suggestion about adding LINE to Sequence.  That did
the trick.

Greg Good
```

<a id="t-09cc786e9f"></a>
## Thread: Form feed within string sent from Mainframe to PC — 3 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Form feed within string sent from Mainframe to PC

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-10-31T17:05:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bnu4p2$r12$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
We are experimenting in writing XML using IBM Mainframe CoBOL.   We are writing
the code in a huge buffer that gets passed to other programs which will result
in it being used in an ASCII environment.

This huge XML line isn't easy to read, so we would like to add line feeds.  
What should we consider while doing this?
```

#### ↳ Re: Form feed within string sent from Mainframe to PC

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-31T17:17:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3Pwob.84604$Go5.1539870@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<bnu4p2$r12$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Why do you need to add line feeds?

If you use an XML viewer to view the data (Internet Explorer/XMLSpy) it
should format the document for you.  There are a number of free tools for
this.

Or write a very simple program that uses an XML parser that will display the
data exactly how you want it.  I assume that you are writing this in
UTF/Unicode.

If you aren't "viewing" the data it should matter even less and you wouldn't
ask this question.

If you hell bent, make sure you understand that the line feed is not the
same across all ascii platforms and is notorious for causing problems.

JCE

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bnu4p2$r12$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> We are experimenting in writing XML using IBM Mainframe CoBOL.   We are
writing
> the code in a huge buffer that gets passed to other programs which will
result
> in it being used in an ASCII environment.
>
> This huge XML line isn't easy to read, so we would like to add line feeds.
> What should we consider while doing this?
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Form feed within string sent from Mainframe to PC

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-10-31T20:17:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-B75C02.20172231102003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bnu4p2$r12$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <bnu4p2$r12$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
 "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> We are experimenting in writing XML using IBM Mainframe CoBOL.   We are 
> writing
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> What should we consider while doing this?

You could always add EBCDIC CR/LF chars.  The various translation 
packages I've dealt with seem to convert them nicely.

As these are always considered xml "whitespace" you can place them 
anywhere a space would be appropriate.

Depending on your preserve whitespace options in your schema, you could 
add them at the start of any tag under the ignore-leading-whitespace 
rule.

Or you can always add them between a consecutive ">" and "<" sequence.
```
